It is a **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** and **WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group)** standard that introduces semantic markup, native multimedia support, form enhancements, and powerful client-side APIs. HTML5 is not a single technology but a **collection of features, technologies, and APIs** that empower modern web applications, including Canvas, Video, Geolocation, Web Storage, and Web Workers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** HTML5 is a **markup language**, not a programming language. topics.title: Styling Web Pages Using CSS - Introduction, Inline Styles, Embedded Style Sheets, Linking External Style Sheets, Positioning Elements: Absolute Positioning, Z-index, Positioning Elements: Relative Positioning, Span, Backgrounds, Element Dimensions, Box Model and Text Flow, Media Types and Media Queries, Drop-Down Menus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Styling Web Pages Using CSS - Introduction, Inline Styles, Embedded Style Sheets, Linking External Style Sheets, Positioning Elements: Absolute Positioning, Z-index, Positioning Elements: Relative Positioning, Span, Backgrounds, Element Dimensions, Box Model and Text Flow, Media Types and Media Queries, Drop-Down Menus # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to CSS **Cascading Style Sheets (CSS)** is a declarative style sheet language used to describe the presentation of documents written in markup languages such as **HTML** and **XML**. As per the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** standard, CSS separates **content (HTML)** from **presentation (CSS)**, enabling developers to control layout, colors, fonts, spacing, and responsive behavior across multiple web pages from a single source. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term *cascading* in CSS refers to the priority scheme (specificity, importance, source order) used to determine which style rule applies when multiple rules target the same element. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an **HTML page as the skeletal structure (bones and muscles) of a human body**, and **CSS as the skin, clothing, and makeup**. A skeleton alone looks bare; adding CSS is like dressing it in a shirt (color), trousers (layout), and a hat (borders). Just as one dress code (style sheet) can be applied to many people (web pages), a single CSS file can style thousands of HTML pages consistently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Definition Style):** CSS is a rule-based language in which each rule consists of a *selector* and a *declaration block*. The selector identifies the HTML element(s), and the declaration block contains one or more *property:value* pairs separated by semicolons. --- ## 1.2 The Three Methods of Applying CSS CSS can be inserted into an HTML document in **three standard ways** as per the W3C specification: ### 1.2.1 Inline Styles An **inline style** is applied directly to a single HTML element using the `style` attribute. It has the **highest specificity** (except for `!important` rules) and overrides embedded and external styles. ```html

This is an inline styled paragraph.

``` **Real-world analogy:** Inline styles are like writing a personal note on a single document with a marker — quick but inefficient for many pages. ### 1.2.2 Embedded (Internal) Style Sheets An **embedded style sheet** is defined inside the ` ``` **Real-world analogy:** Embedded styles are like a company-wide dress code memo on a single office noticeboard — it applies to everyone in that one building. ### 1.2.3 External Style Sheets An **external style sheet** is a separate `.css` file linked to one or more HTML documents using the `` element. This is the **most scalable and KTU-recommended** method for production websites. ```html ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always use the `rel="stylesheet"` and `type="text/css"` attributes when linking external CSS. The `href` attribute specifies the path to the CSS file. **Real-world analogy:** External CSS is a centralized style manual shared across all branches of a multinational company — change the manual once, and every branch updates automatically. --- ## 1.3 Positioning Elements CSS provides the **position** property to control how elements are placed in the document layout. The five key values are: | Position Value | Behavior | |----------------|----------| | `static` | Default; follows normal document flow | | `relative` | Positioned relative to its normal position | | `absolute` | Positioned relative to nearest positioned ancestor | | `fixed` | Positioned relative to the viewport | | `sticky` | Toggles between relative and fixed based on scroll | ### 1.3.1 Absolute Positioning An **absolutely positioned** element is removed from the normal document flow and positioned relative to its **nearest positioned ancestor** (an ancestor with `position` other than `static`). If no such ancestor exists, it is positioned relative to the **initial containing block** (the viewport). ### 1.3.2 Relative Positioning A **relatively positioned** element remains in the normal document flow but can be offset using `top`, `right`, `bottom`, and `left` properties. The space it originally occupied is **preserved**. ### 1.3.3 Z-Index The **z-index** property controls the **stacking order** of positioned elements along the virtual z-axis (depth). Higher z-index values appear in front of lower values. It only works on elements with `position` set to `relative`, `absolute`, `fixed`, or `sticky`. > [!NOTE] > **Default z-index:** `auto` — the element follows the document order. **Negative values** are allowed and place the element behind its parent's background. --- ## 1.4 Span, Backgrounds, and Element Dimensions - **``**: An inline, non-semantic container used to apply styles to a portion of text without breaking the line. - **Backgrounds**: Set using `background-color`, `background-image`, `background-repeat`, `background-position`, and the shorthand `background` property. - **Element Dimensions**: Controlled via `width`, `height`, `min-width`, `max-width`, `min-height`, and `max-height`. --- ## 1.5 Box Model and Text Flow The **CSS Box Model** describes how every HTML element is rendered as a rectangular box consisting of four areas (from inside out): **Content → Padding → Border → Margin** - **Content**: The actual text, image, or other media. - **Padding**: Space between content and border (transparent, part of element's clickable area). - **Border**: The line surrounding the padding. - **Margin**: Space outside the border (transparent, separates elements). --- ## 1.6 Media Types and Media Queries **Media types** define the type of device the CSS is intended for (`all`, `screen`, `print`, `speech`). **Media queries** extend this concept by allowing conditional CSS based on device characteristics such as viewport width, height, orientation, and resolution. They are the foundation of **responsive web design**. ```css @media screen and (max-width: 768px) { body { font-size: 14px; } } ``` --- ## 1.7 Drop-Down Menus A **drop-down menu** is a graphical control element that reveals a list of options when the user hovers over or clicks a parent item. In CSS, drop-down menus are commonly built using nested unordered lists (`
    ` and `
  • `) with `:hover` pseudo-classes and `display: none` / `display: block` toggling. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 CSS Syntax Breakdown A CSS rule has the following structure: ```css selector { property: value; property: value; } ``` For example: ```css h1 { color: red; font-size: 24px; } ``` ### 2.1.1 Components Explained 1. **Selector**: Pattern that matches HTML elements (`h1`, `.class`, `#id`, `*`, `element element`). 2. **Declaration Block**: Enclosed in curly braces `{}`, contains one or more declarations. 3. **Property**: A stylistic aspect to change (`color`, `margin`, `font-family`). 4. **Value**: The setting for the property (`red`, `10px`, `Arial`). 5. **Declaration**: A `property:value` pair ending with a semicolon. --- ## 2.2 Selector Specificity Hierarchy When multiple CSS rules target the same element, specificity determines which rule wins. Specificity is calculated as a four-part value: **(inline, IDs, classes/attributes/pseudo-classes, elements/pseudo-elements)**. ### KTU Specificity Cheat Sheet | Selector Type | Example | Specificity Score (a,b,c,d) | |---------------|---------|------------------------------| | Inline style | `style="..."` | (1,0,0,0) | | ID selector | `#header` | (0,1,0,0) | | Class / attribute / pseudo-class | `.btn`, `[type]`, `:hover` | (0,0,1,0) | | Element / pseudo-element | `p`, `::before` | (0,0,0,1) | | Universal | `*` | (0,0,0,0) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **!important** rule overrides all specificity calculations. Use it sparingly as it breaks the natural cascade and is considered bad practice in production code. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.3.1 Box Model Total Width Formula $$\text{Total Width} = \text{Width} + 2 \cdot \text{Padding} + 2 \cdot \text{Border} + 2 \cdot \text{Margin}$$ ### 2.3.2 Box Model Total Height Formula $$\text{Total Height} = \text{Height} + 2 \cdot \text{Padding}_{\text{vertical}} + 2 \cdot \text{Border}_{\text{vertical}} + 2 \cdot \text{Margin}_{\text{vertical}}$$ ### 2.3.3 Box Sizing Adjustment With `box-sizing: border-box`, the formula becomes: $$\text{Total Width} = \text{Specified Width} + 2 \cdot \text{Margin}$$ (Content + Padding + Border together equal the specified width.) ### 2.3.4 Z-Index Stacking Rule $$\text{Visible Order} = \max(z\text{-index}) \text{ among overlapping positioned elements}$$ > [!NOTE] > Z-index only applies to elements with `position` set to a value other than `static`. ### 2.3.5 Media Query Logical Structure $$\text{Conditional CSS} = \text{Media Type} \;\wedge\; \text{(Media Feature: Value)}$$ Common media features: `max-width`, `min-width`, `orientation`, `resolution`, `aspect-ratio`. ### 2.3.6 Position Offset Properties For `relative`, `absolute`, `fixed`, `sticky` positioning: | Property | Effect | |----------|--------| | `top` | Distance from top edge of containing block | | `right` | Distance from right edge of containing block | | `bottom` | Distance from bottom edge of containing block | | `left` | Distance from left edge of containing block | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility CSS is the backbone of every modern web application. Its practical applications include: 1. **Responsive Web Design (RWD)**: Using media queries to adapt layouts for mobile, tablet, and desktop. 2. **Print Stylesheets**: Separate `print` media rules for printer-friendly pages (e.g., removing navigation, using black-and-white colors). 3. **Accessibility**: Ensuring sufficient color contrast and scalable fonts. 4. **Theming**: White-labeling websites by swapping a single CSS file. 5. **Animations and Transitions**: Modern CSS3 supports `transition`, `animation`, `transform` for interactive UI. 6. **Cross-Browser Consistency**: Vendor prefixes (`-webkit-`, `-moz-`, `-ms-`) ensure uniform rendering. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** The cascading order precedence (lowest to highest) is: User Agent styles < User styles < Author styles < Author `!important` < User `!important`. --- ## 2.5 The Three CSS Application Methods — Comparison | Feature | Inline | Embedded | External | |---------|--------|----------|----------| | Specificity | Highest | Medium | Lowest (but cached) | | Reusability | None | Single page | Multiple pages | | Maintainability | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | | Performance | No extra HTTP request | No extra HTTP request | One extra HTTP request (cached) | | Use Case | Quick overrides | Single-page demos | Production websites | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 CSS Rule Parsing — Step-by-Step **Given the following CSS rule:** ```css nav ul li a { color: white; text-decoration: none; padding: 10px 15px; } ``` ### Step 1: Identify the Selector The selector is `nav ul li a` — a **descendant combinator** chain. This means: "Apply the rule to any `` element that is a descendant of an `
  • `, which is a descendant of a `
      `, which is a descendant of a `
      `." ### Step 2: Compute Specificity Each part contributes to the specificity score: - `nav` → (0,0,0,1) - `ul` → (0,0,0,1) - `li` → (0,0,0,1) - `a` → (0,0,0,1) Total specificity: **(0, 0, 0, 4)** ### Step 3: Apply Declaration Block Each declaration is parsed left-to-right: 1. `color: white` → text color is set to `#FFFFFF`. 2. `text-decoration: none` → removes the default underline from anchors. 3. `padding: 10px 15px` → sets top/bottom padding to `10px` and left/right padding to `15px`. ### Step 4: Cascade Resolution If another rule targets the same element with equal or higher specificity, the **last-declared** rule wins (source order). With higher specificity, the more specific rule wins regardless of source order. --- ## 3.2 Box Model Width Calculation — Exhaustive Derivation **Problem:** Compute the total horizontal space occupied by a `
      ` element with the following CSS: ```css div.box { width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 5px solid black; margin: 10px; } ``` ### Step 1: Identify the Components - **Content width**: $w_c = 200$ px - **Padding (left + right)**: $p_{lr} = 20 + 20 = 40$ px - **Border (left + right)**: $b_{lr} = 5 + 5 = 10$ px - **Margin (left + right)**: $m_{lr} = 10 + 10 = 20$ px ### Step 2: Apply the Total Width Formula (default `box-sizing: content-box`) $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{total}} &= w_c + 2 \cdot p + 2 \cdot b + 2 \cdot m \\ &= 200 + (2 \times 20) + (2 \times 5) + (2 \times 10) \\ &= 200 + 40 + 10 + 20 \\ &= 270 \text{ px} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Verify with `box-sizing: border-box` $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{total-border-box}} &= w_c + 2 \cdot m \\ &= 200 + 20 \\ &= 220 \text{ px} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > With `box-sizing: border-box`, the `200px` width includes content + padding + border. Only the margin is added externally. --- ## 3.3 Z-Index Stacking Derivation **Problem:** Three divs overlap. Determine the visual stacking order. ```css #a { position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; z-index: 1; } #b { position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; z-index: 5; } #c { position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; z-index: 3; } ``` ### Step 1: List All Z-Index Values - `#a`: $z_a = 1$ - `#b`: $z_b = 5$ - `#c`: $z_c = 3$ ### Step 2: Apply the Visible Order Rule $$ \text{Visible Order (back to front)} = \arg\text{sort}_{\text{ascending}}(z) $$ Sorting in ascending order: $$ 1 < 3 < 5 \implies z_a < z_c < z_b $$ ### Step 3: Final Stacking (back → front) $$ \text{Back-to-front order} = \#a \rightarrow \#c \rightarrow \#b $$ Therefore, **`#b` is the topmost visible element**. --- ## 3.4 Media Query Resolution — Step-by-Step **Given CSS:** ```css body { font-size: 16px; } @media screen and (max-width: 600px) { body { font-size: 12px; } } ``` ### Step 1: Default Rule Without any media query match, `body { font-size: 16px; }` applies. ### Step 2: Media Query Evaluation The browser checks: - Is the media type `screen`? → Yes (assumed for desktop/mobile browsers). - Is `viewport-width ≤ 600px`? → **True** on mobile, **False** on desktop. ### Step 3: Conditional Application $$ \text{Font Size} = \begin{cases} 12 \text{ px}, & \text{if } w_{\text{viewport}} \leq 600 \text{ px} \\ 16 \text{ px}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ --- ## 3.5 Drop-Down Menu Structural Analysis A typical CSS drop-down uses nested lists. The hidden submenu is displayed on hover: ```css ul { list-style: none; } ul li { position: relative; } ul li ul { display: none; position: absolute; top: 100%; left: 0; } ul li:hover ul { display: block; } ``` ### Analytical Logic | Step | Trigger | Effect | |------|---------|--------| | 1 | Page load | `ul li ul` has `display: none` → hidden | | 2 | User hovers over parent `
    • ` | `:hover` pseudo-class activates | | 3 | `ul li:hover ul` rule applies | Submenu becomes visible | | 4 | `position: absolute; top: 100%` | Submenu appears directly below parent | | 5 | User moves cursor away | `:hover` deactivates → submenu hides | --- ## 3.6 Specificity Numerical Comparison **Problem:** Which rule wins between two competing selectors? ```css #nav .item a { color: red; } /* Rule A */ ul#nav li.item a { color: blue; } /* Rule B */ ``` ### Step 1: Compute Specificity for Rule A - `#nav` → (0,1,0,0) - `.item` → (0,0,1,0) - `a` → (0,0,0,1) - **Total A**: (0, 1, 1, 1) ### Step 2: Compute Specificity for Rule B - `ul` → (0,0,0,1) - `#nav` → (0,1,0,0) - `li` → (0,0,0,1) - `.item` → (0,0,1,0) - `a` → (0,0,0,1) - **Total B**: (0, 1, 1, 3) ### Step 3: Compare Left to Right $$ \begin{aligned} a_1 &= 0 = b_1 \\ a_2 &= 1 = b_2 \\ a_3 &= 1 = b_3 \\ a_4 &= 1 < b_4 = 3 \end{aligned} $$ Since Rule B has a higher element count, **Rule B wins** (final color is `blue`). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CSS Application Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HTML Document] --> B{Apply CSS How?} B -->|Inline| C[style attribute on element] B -->|Embedded| D[style tag in head section] B -->|External| E[link tag to external .css file] C --> F[Browser CSS Engine] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Parse Selectors and Properties] G --> H[Compute Specificity] H --> I[Resolve Cascade] I --> J[Render Styled Web Page] H --> K[Apply Box Model] K --> J F --> L[Evaluate Media Queries] L --> J ``` --- ## 4.2 Box Model Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Element Box] --> B[Margin: Outer Space] A --> C[Border: Surrounding Line] C --> D[Padding: Inner Space] D --> E[Content: Text and Media] B --> B1[Transparent] C --> C1[Color, Width, Style] D --> D1[Part of element click area] E --> E1[Width and Height apply here] ``` --- ## 4.3 Positioning Type Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CSS Position Property] --> B{Which Value?} B -->|static| C[Default Normal Flow] B -->|relative| D[Offset from Normal Position] B -->|absolute| E[Relative to Nearest Positioned Ancestor] B -->|fixed| F[Relative to Viewport] B -->|sticky| G[Relative Until Scroll Threshold] D --> D1[Space Preserved] E --> E1[Removed from Flow] F --> F1[Stays on Screen During Scroll] G --> G1[Toggles Between Relative and Fixed] ``` --- ## 4.4 Z-Index Stacking Context Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Browser Viewport] --> B[Stacking Context Root] B --> C[Element Z1] B --> D[Element Z3] B --> E[Element Z5] C -->|Behind| D D -->|Behind| E E -->|Topmost Visible| F[User Sees This] ``` --- ## 4.5 Drop-Down Menu DOM Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ul.main-menu] --> B[li: Home] A --> C[li: Products] A --> D[li: Contact] C --> E[ul.sub-menu] E --> F[li: Electronics] E --> G[li: Clothing] E --> H[li: Books] F --> I[ul.sub-sub-menu] I --> J[li: Phones] I --> K[li: Laptops] ``` > [!NOTE] > In this hierarchical structure, hovering over **Products** reveals the sub-menu containing **Electronics**, **Clothing**, and **Books**. Hovering over **Electronics** further reveals **Phones** and **Laptops** — a multi-level drop-down. --- ## 4.6 Media Query Evaluation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Load HTML and CSS] --> B[Browser Detects Device] B --> C{Match Media Query} C -->|True| D[Apply Conditional CSS Block] C -->|False| E[Skip Block Use Default] D --> F[Combine with Base Styles] E --> F F --> G[Render Final Page] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the three methods of applying CSS to an HTML document with suitable examples.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks) The three methods of applying CSS are: 1. **Inline Styles**: Applied using the `style` attribute directly on an HTML element. Example: `

      Text

      `. (1 Mark) 2. **Embedded (Internal) Style Sheets**: Defined within a ` ``` (1 Mark) 3. **External Style Sheets**: Stored in a separate `.css` file and linked via the `` element. Example: ```html ``` (1 Mark) --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between relative and absolute positioning in CSS.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks) | Aspect | Relative Positioning | Absolute Positioning | |--------|---------------------|---------------------| | Reference Point | Its own normal position | Nearest positioned ancestor | | Document Flow | Preserves original space | Removed from flow | | Use Case | Minor offsets | Precise layout placement | | Example Offset | `top: 10px` moves element 10px down from its original spot | `top: 10px` places element 10px from the ancestor's top edge | (3 Marks for complete differentiation with example) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the CSS Box Model in detail. Describe the roles of `content`, `padding`, `border`, and `margin`. Illustrate with a diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write an HTML page demonstrating an external style sheet that uses the box model properties to create a card layout with rounded corners, a shadow effect, and 20px internal padding. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — CSS Box Model Explanation (7 Marks):** The CSS Box Model is a fundamental concept that treats every HTML element as a rectangular box composed of four areas (from inside out): 1. **Content Area** (1 Mark): Contains the actual content — text, images, or other media. Its dimensions are controlled by `width` and `height`. 2. **Padding Area** (1 Mark): The transparent space between the content and the border. It expands the clickable area of the element and inherits the element's background color. 3. **Border Area** (2 Marks): A line that surrounds the padding and content. Its style, width, and color are controlled by `border-style`, `border-width`, and `border-color`. 4. **Margin Area** (2 Marks): The outermost transparent space that separates the element from neighboring elements. Unlike padding, margin is not part of the element's clickable area. **Total Width Formula** (1 Mark): $$W_{\text{total}} = W + 2P + 2B + 2M$$ ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Margin: Outer Space] --> B[Border: Surrounding Line] B --> C[Padding: Inner Space] C --> D[Content: Text and Media] ``` **Part (b) — Card Layout HTML + CSS (7 Marks):** `card.html`: ```html

      KTU Web Programming

      This is a styled card component.

      ``` (2 Marks) `card.css`: ```css .card { width: 300px; padding: 20px; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 10px; box-shadow: 2px 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.2); background-color: #f9f9f9; margin: 20px; } ``` (3 Marks) **Explanation of properties** (2 Marks): - `padding: 20px` — internal spacing of 20px on all sides. - `border: 1px solid #ccc` — a 1-pixel gray border. - `border-radius: 10px` — rounded corners. - `box-shadow` — drop shadow effect. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the concept of media queries in CSS. Discuss the role of media types and media features with examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design a responsive navigation menu using CSS that collapses into a vertical layout on screens narrower than 768px. Use external CSS and drop-down submenus. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Media Queries Explanation (7 Marks):** **Definition** (1 Mark): A media query is a CSS technique introduced in CSS3 that allows the application of styles conditionally based on the characteristics of the device or viewport. **Syntax** (1 Mark): ```css @media media-type and (media-feature: value) { /* CSS rules */ } ``` **Media Types** (2 Marks): - `all` — suitable for all devices. - `screen` — computer screens, tablets, smartphones. - `print` — printers and print preview. - `speech` — screen readers. **Media Features** (2 Marks): - `max-width`, `min-width` — viewport width thresholds. - `orientation` — `portrait` or `landscape`. - `resolution` — device pixel density. **Example** (1 Mark): ```css @media screen and (max-width: 768px) { body { font-size: 14px; } } ``` **Part (b) — Responsive Navigation with Drop-Down (7 Marks):** `menu.html` (3 Marks): ```html
      • Home
      • Services
        • Web Design
        • SEO
      • Contact
      ``` `menu.css` (4 Marks): ```css .nav { list-style: none; display: flex; background: #333; } .nav li { position: relative; } .nav a { color: white; text-decoration: none; padding: 15px 20px; display: block; } .nav li:hover { background: #555; } .dropdown { display: none; position: absolute; top: 100%; left: 0; list-style: none; background: #444; min-width: 150px; } .nav li:hover .dropdown { display: block; } @media screen and (max-width: 768px) { .nav { flex-direction: column; } .dropdown { position: static; } } ``` --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Web Programming Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting `type="text/css"`** in the `` tag — While HTML5 makes it optional, KTU board answers **always** include it for full marks. Lose 0.5 mark if omitted. > > 2. **Confusing `position: relative` with `position: absolute`** — Students often write that absolute positioning is relative to the page. **Correction:** It is relative to the **nearest positioned ancestor**, not the page or viewport. > > 3. **Box Model Calculation Errors** — Forgetting to multiply padding/border/margin by 2 (both left and right sides). Use the formula method shown in Section 3.2. > > 4. **Z-Index with `position: static`** — Writing `z-index` on a statically positioned element has no effect. Always pair z-index with `relative`, `absolute`, `fixed`, or `sticky`. > > 5. **Media Query Syntax** — Missing the `and` keyword: writing `@media (max-width: 600px)` instead of `@media screen and (max-width: 600px)`. This may still work but is technically incorrect for KTU board standards. > > 6. **Drop-Down without `position: relative` on parent** — The submenu will not align correctly if the parent `
    • ` lacks `position: relative`. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist** - **CSS** stands for **Cascading Style Sheets** and is used to style HTML documents. - The three CSS application methods are: **Inline**, **Embedded**, and **External**. External is best practice. - The **link tag** for external CSS: ``. - **CSS Rule Syntax**: `selector { property: value; }`. - **Specificity** order: Inline > ID > Class > Element. Calculated as `(a,b,c,d)`. - **Position values**: `static` (default), `relative`, `absolute`, `fixed`, `sticky`. - **Absolute positioning** is relative to the **nearest positioned ancestor**. - **Relative positioning** preserves the element's space in document flow. - **Z-index** controls stacking order — higher values appear in front. Requires `position` to be set. - **Span** is an inline container for styling text portions. - **Backgrounds** use `background-color`, `background-image`, `background-repeat`, `background-position`, or the shorthand `background`. - **Element dimensions** are controlled via `width`, `height`, `min-width`, `max-width`, `min-height`, `max-height`. - **Box Model** order: Content → Padding → Border → Margin. - **Total width formula**: $W = w + 2p + 2b + 2m$. - **`box-sizing: border-box`** makes width include padding and border. - **Media types**: `all`, `screen`, `print`, `speech`. - **Media query syntax**: `@media screen and (max-width: 600px) { ... }`. - **Drop-down menus** use nested `
        ` lists with `:hover` and `display: none` / `block`. - **Cascade precedence** (low to high): User Agent < User < Author < Author `!important` < User `!important`.

> - **Topic:** Extensible Markup Language - Introduction, XML Basics, Structuring Data, XML Namespaces, Document Type Definitions (DTDs), XML Vocabularies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is XML? — The Formal Academic Definition

**Extensible Markup Language (XML)** is a **W3C-recommended**, text-based, platform-neutral markup language designed to **store, transport, and structure data** in a format that is both **human-readable** and **machine-parsable**. Unlike HTML, which has a *fixed* set of tags defined for display, XML allows the designer to **define custom tags** (hence *extensible*) that describe the *meaning* (semantics) of the data rather than its appearance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim-grade):**
> XML is a meta-language used to define other markup languages. It is a subset of SGML (Standard Generalized Markup Language) standardized by the **W3C in February 1998 (XML 1.0)** and refined in subsequent editions. It separates **content**, **structure**, and **presentation** — a foundational principle of modern web data interchange.

### The Three Pillars of XML (KTU Board Favourite)

| Pillar | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Extensible** | Designer creates custom tags | `<book>`, `<invoice>` |
| **Markup** | Uses tags to annotate data | `<price currency="INR">499</price>` |
| **Language** | Strict, rule-governed syntax | Must have one root, case-sensitive |

---

## 1.2 The Intuition — An Everyday Analogy

Think of XML as a **Universal Postal Form** used across an international shipping company.

Imagine a courier company that delivers packages between India, Japan, and Brazil. Every country has its own native form (some write dates as `DD/MM/YYYY`, some as `MM-DD-YY`; some use metric, some imperial). The chaos of interpretation breaks the supply chain.

The company designs a **Universal Form (XML)** that has labelled fields: `<from>`, `<to>`, `<weight unit="kg">`, `<declared_value currency="INR">`. **The fields (tags) can be invented by the company itself** (extensible), but once defined, **every form must follow the same template** strictly. Any country receiving the form knows exactly where to look.

In the same way:
- **HTML = a wall painted for display** (look matters).
- **XML = the labelled crate inside the wall** (what's inside matters, and labels are *self-defined*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU cares:** XML is the *backbone* of web services (SOAP/WSDL), RSS feeds, SVG, configuration files (Tomcat, Maven, Android), and document formats (OOXML, ODF). It directly answers the question, *"How does the web move structured data between systems that don't share a database?"*

---

## 1.3 XML vs HTML — The Conceptual Distinction

| Feature | HTML | XML |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Display data | Carry data |
| **Tag Set** | Fixed (predefined) | User-defined (extensible) |
| **Case Sensitivity** | Insensitive | **Sensitive** |
| **Closing Tags** | Optional for some | **Mandatory** |
| **Errors** | Browser forgives minor ones | **Parser must reject malformed docs** |
| **Output** | Visual page | Structured data feed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU One-Liner to Memorise:**
> *"HTML describes **presentation**; XML describes **structure and meaning**."*

---

## 1.4 The XML Information Set — Vocabulary Primer

Before diving deeper, lock in the **11 official XML information items** as per the W3C Infoset specification:

1. **Document** — the entire XML instance.
2. **Element** — a tagged structural unit.
3. **Attribute** — a name–value pair inside a start tag.
4. **Processing Instruction (PI)** — directives to the application (e.g., `<?xml-stylesheet ?>`).
5. **Comment** — `<!-- ... -->` (for humans, ignored by parsers).
6. **Text** — character data between tags.
7. **CDATA Section** — `<![CDATA[ ... ]]>` — raw text, no parsing.
8. **Entity Reference** — `&`, `<` etc.
9. **Character Reference** — `A` or `A` (numeric).
10. **Document Type Declaration** — links to a DTD.
11. **Namespace** — disambiguates tag names.

> [!NOTE]
> The first **three** (Document, Element, Attribute) are tested most frequently in KTU Part A questions. The full list of 11 is expected knowledge for Part B.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Well-Formed XML Document

A *well-formed* document is one that obeys every syntactic rule of the W3C XML 1.0 specification. Validity (against a DTD/Schema) is a stricter subset.

### The Mandatory Skeleton

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="yes"?>
<!--  ^^^^^^^^ XML Declaration (optional but recommended) -->

<library xmlns:bk="http://example.com/books">
    <bk:book id="101" lang="en">
        <bk:title>Web Programming</bk:title>
        <bk:author>Rajiv</bk:author>
        <bk:price currency="INR">499.00</bk:price>
    </bk:book>
</library>
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Mandatory Well-Formedness Rules (Board Question Every Year):**
> 1. Exactly **one** root element (the *document element*).
> 2. Every start tag has a matching end tag.
> 3. Tags must be **properly nested** (no overlap).
> 4. Attribute values must be **quoted** (`"..."` or `'...'`).
> 5. There is exactly **one** XML declaration (and if present, it must be the very first thing — no whitespace before it).

---

## 2.2 XML Components — Deep Dive

### (a) The XML Declaration

$$\text{<?xml } \text{version} = \text{"1.0"} \quad \text{encoding} = \text{"UTF-8"} \quad \text{standalone} = \text{"yes"} \text{?>}$$

| Attribute | Allowed Values | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `version` | `1.0` (most common) / `1.1` | Required | XML spec version |
| `encoding` | `UTF-8`, `UTF-16`, `ISO-8859-1` | `UTF-8` | Character encoding |
| `standalone` | `yes` / `no` | `no` | Does document depend on external markup declarations? |

### (b) Elements — The Heart of XML

An **element** is a logical chunk of data bounded by a start tag and an end tag. Elements may contain: text, other elements, or both (mixed content).

$$\text{Element} = \underbrace{\langle \text{tagname} \rangle}_{\text{start}} \; + \; \text{Content} \; + \; \underbrace{\langle / \text{tagname} \rangle}_{\text{end}}$$

**Empty element shorthand** is allowed:
$$\text{<image src="x.png" />} \quad \equiv \quad \text{<image src="x.png"></image>}$$

### (c) Attributes — Metadata About an Element

$$\text{Attribute} = \text{Name} \; + \; \text{Quoted String Value}$$

**Rules:**
- Unique within an element (no duplicate attribute names).
- Values **must** be quoted.
- Attributes are for **metadata**, not bulk data (a W3C design guideline — *avoid data-hiding in attributes*).

### (d) Character Data and CDATA Sections

$$\text{PCDATA} = \text{Parsed Character Data (default)}$$

To embed **markup-resembling text** (e.g., HTML code in a tutorial), use a **CDATA section**:

```xml
<explanation><![CDATA[
   The tag <student> must be closed. Use </student>.
]]></explanation>
```

### (e) Comments and Processing Instructions

```xml
<!-- This is a comment, ignored by parsers -->
<?xml-stylesheet type="text/xsl" href="style.xsl"?>
```

The **PI** is the bridge between XML data and the application (commonly used to attach XSLT stylesheets).

### (f) Entity References

| Built-in Entity | Symbol | Numeric Ref |
|---|---|---|
| Less than | `<` | `<` |
| Greater than | `>` | `>` |
| Ampersand | `&` | `&` |
| Apostrophe | `'` | `'` |
| Quotation | `"` | `"` |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five Forbidden Character Substitutions:** If you literally need `<`, `>`, `&`, `'`, `"` inside text, **never** write them raw — always use the entity. KTU frequently asks for the full list.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — XML Syntax Rules

| # | Rule | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | One root element | Defines the document's outermost scope |
| 2 | Case-sensitive tags (`<Book>` ≠ `<book>`) | Avoids ambiguity in international markup |
| 3 | Closing tag mandatory | Forces well-formedness |
| 4 | Proper nesting (LIFO) | Mirrors tree structure |
| 5 | Attribute values always quoted | Lexer simplicity |
| 6 | Whitespace preserved inside text | Default behaviour (unlike HTML) |
| 7 | Comments cannot contain `--` | Resolves SGML ambiguity |
| 8 | CDATA cannot contain `]]>` | Self-termination of section |
| 9 | Element/attribute names: letter or `_` start, no spaces | XML Name production rule |
| 10 | Reserved prefix `xml` (case-insensitive) is reserved | Reserved for XML spec |

### XML Name Production (Formal Rule)

A legal XML name follows the production rule:

$$\text{Name} \; ::= \; \text{Letter} \; \mid \; \text{`_`} \; \mid \; \text{`:`} \; \rightarrow \; \text{(Letter \mid `\_` \mid `:` \mid Digit \mid `.` \mid `-`)}^*$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Tag names cannot start with a digit, cannot contain spaces, and the colon `:` is reserved for namespace separation (more on this in §3).

---

## 2.4 Why XML Matters in Real Engineering Systems

| Domain | XML Use-Case |
|---|---|
| **Web Services (SOAP)** | Message envelope, WSDL contract |
| **Configuration** | `web.xml`, `pom.xml`, `server.xml` |
| **Document formats** | `.docx`, `.xlsx`, `.odt` |
| **Finance** | FIXML, FpML trading messages |
| **Publishing** | DocBook, TEI, JATS |
| **Mobile & Android** | Layout XML, Manifest XML |
| **Data interchange** | RSS, Atom, KML (Google Earth) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern REST/JSON dominates *lightweight* APIs, but XML remains entrenched in **enterprise integration**, **regulatory submission** (pharma, banking), and **long-term archival** because schema validation and namespace-aware processing are far more mature than JSON's ecosystem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Structures & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Building a Well-Formed XML Document — Step-by-Step Construction

We will construct a valid XML document representing a **library catalogue** step by step, justifying every design decision.

### Step 1 — The XML Declaration (Foundational Header)

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?>
```

**Logic:**
- `version="1.0"` — Universally supported; `1.1` adds minor Unicode line-separator handling.
- `encoding="UTF-8"` — Default; supports every human language.
- `standalone="no"` — Permits an external DTD (will be added in §3.4).

> **Transition Reason:** Without the declaration, parsers must *guess* — modern strict parsers will refuse to process the file.

### Step 2 — Define the Root Element

The **single root element** is the entire document's container. We choose `<catalogue>` because it is the most generic, semantic, and extensible name.

```xml
<catalogue>
</catalogue>
```

> **Why not `<library>`?** Because XML is *self-describing* — the tag *is* the data. `catalogue` matches our business context more precisely.

### Step 3 — Add Child Elements with Hierarchical Logic

A *book* is not a flat string; it has structure: title, author(s), price, ISBN, year.

```xml
<catalogue>
    <book>
        <title>Web Programming</title>
        <author>Rajiv</author>
        <isbn>978-81-203-1234-5</isbn>
        <price currency="INR">499.00</price>
        <year>2024</year>
    </book>
</catalogue>
```

**Logic of Step 3:**
- One book = one `<book>` element (object–element mapping).
- Multiplicity handled by repeating `<book>` (no arrays in XML — *repetition* is the way).

### Step 4 — Add the Second Book with Attributes for Metadata

```xml
<book isbn="978-81-203-9999-9" lang="en" edition="2">
    <title>Database Systems</title>
    <author>Navathe</author>
    <price currency="USD">79.99</price>
    <year>2023</year>
</book>
```

**Logic of Step 4:**
- `isbn` is moved to an attribute because it acts as a **unique identifier** (metadata, not displayable text).
- `currency` is an attribute on `<price>` because it is *metadata* about the value, not separate data.
- `edition="2"` — a small flag; perfect attribute use.

### Step 5 — Add a CDATA Section for Special Content

```xml
<book>
    <title>XML & Web Services</title>
    <summary><![CDATA[
        This book covers <parser> internals and DTD syntax.
        Use & and < carefully.
    ]]></summary>
</book>
```

**Logic of Step 5:**
- The `&` in the title is escaped to `&` because it lies in PCDATA.
- The `<parser>` text is placed inside CDATA because it is *literal* text, not markup.

### Final Document — Complete Form

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?>
<catalogue>
    <book isbn="978-81-203-1234-5" lang="en">
        <title>Web Programming</title>
        <author>Rajiv</author>
        <price currency="INR">499.00</price>
        <year>2024</year>
        <summary><![CDATA[Covers HTML5, CSS3, JS, and XML.]]></summary>
    </book>
    <book isbn="978-81-203-9999-9" lang="en" edition="2">
        <title>Database Systems</title>
        <author>Navathe</author>
        <price currency="USD">79.99</price>
        <year>2023</year>
    </book>
</catalogue>
```

---

## 3.2 Deriving the Tree Model — XML as a DOM Tree

Every well-formed XML document has **exactly one** equivalent tree model called the **Document Object Model (DOM)**. Let us derive it.

### Mathematical Tree Definition

An XML document $D$ is a tuple:

$$
D = (V, E, r, \text{label})
$$

Where:
- $V$ = set of *nodes* (elements, text, attributes, comments)
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ = set of *parent–child* edges
- $r \in V$ = the **root** element (unique)
- $\text{label}: V \rightarrow \text{String}$ = node naming function

### Mapping the Catalogue Document to a Tree

For the document above, the derived tree is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{root} &= \langle \text{catalogue} \rangle \\
\text{children}(r) &= \{ \langle \text{book}_1 \rangle, \langle \text{book}_2 \rangle \} \\
\text{children}(\langle \text{book}_1 \rangle) &= \{ \langle \text{title} \rangle, \langle \text{author} \rangle, \langle \text{price} \rangle, \langle \text{year} \rangle, \langle \text{summary} \rangle \}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering utility:** The DOM tree is what **XPath, XSLT, and JavaScript (DOMParser)** traverse. Every "XML query" is a tree-traversal problem.

---

## 3.3 XML Namespaces — Step-by-Step Derivation of the Problem and the Solution

### Step 1 — Identify the Conflict

Suppose two organisations, **LibraryOrg** and **EduOrg**, both define a tag called `<title>`:

$$\text{LibraryOrg} \rightarrow \langle \text{title} \rangle_{\text{book}} = \text{"Web Programming"}$$
$$\text{EduOrg} \rightarrow \langle \text{title} \rangle_{\text{course}} = \text{"B.Tech"} \quad \text{(academic degree)}$$

If merged into a single document, a parser **cannot** distinguish them. This is the **namespace collision problem**.

### Step 2 — Formal Definition of an XML Namespace

A **namespace** is a uniquely identified collection of names, declared via a URI Reference (Uniform Resource Identifier):

$$
\text{NS} = (\text{Prefix}, \text{URI}), \quad \text{where Prefix} \in \text{String}, \; \text{URI} \in \text{URI-REF}
$$

### Step 3 — Declare and Use the Namespaces

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<catalogue
   xmlns:lib="http://library.org/ns"
   xmlns:edu="http://edu.org/ns">

   <lib:book lib:isbn="978-81-203-1234-5">
       <lib:title>Web Programming</lib:title>
       <lib:author>Rajiv</lib:author>
       <edu:title>B.Tech CSE</edu:title>
   </lib:book>
</catalogue>
```

### Step 4 — Resolve the Expanded Names

The parser expands each prefixed name into a *QName* (qualified name) tuple:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle \text{lib:title} \rangle &\;\longrightarrow\; (\text{"http://library.org/ns"}, \text{"title"}) \\
\langle \text{edu:title} \rangle &\;\longrightarrow\; (\text{"http://edu.org/ns"}, \text{"title"})
\end{aligned}
$$

These are now **distinct**, even though the local name `title` is identical.

### Step 5 — The Default Namespace Shortcut

If an entire subtree uses one namespace, declare it as the *default* (no prefix):

```xml
<catalogue xmlns="http://library.org/ns">
    <book isbn="978-81-203-1234-5">
        <title>Web Programming</title>
    </book>
</catalogue>
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **W3C Convention:** A namespace URI is **not** required to be dereferenceable. It is an *identifier*, not a *location*. Many novices (and unfortunately some textbooks) confuse this.

### KTU High-Yield Namespace Rules

| # | Rule |
|---|---|
| 1 | URI is an **identifier**, not a network address |
| 2 | Prefix `xml` is **reserved** for XML spec use |
| 3 | Prefix `xmlns` is **reserved** for namespace declarations |
| 4 | A prefix must be **declared** before use |
| 5 | Scope of a prefix = the element where it is declared and its descendants |
| 6 | `xmlns` (no prefix) declares the **default** namespace |

---

## 3.4 Document Type Definitions (DTDs) — Full Derivation

### Step 1 — Why DTDs?

A *well-formed* XML document guarantees syntax, but **not meaning**. For example, the parser accepts `<price>abc</price>` even if business rules require a number. DTDs (and later XML Schemas) impose **semantic constraints**.

### Step 2 — Internal DTD Declaration

The DTD is enclosed in a `<!DOCTYPE>` declaration immediately after the XML declaration:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE catalogue [
    <!ELEMENT catalogue (book+)>
    <!ELEMENT book (title, author, isbn, price, year)>
    <!ELEMENT title (#PCDATA)>
    <!ELEMENT author (#PCDATA)>
    <!ELEMENT isbn (#PCDATA)>
    <!ELEMENT price (#PCDATA)>
    <!ELEMENT year (#PCDATA)>
    <!ATTLIST book
        isbn CDATA #REQUIRED
        lang CDATA #IMPLIED
        edition CDATA #IMPLIED>
]>
<catalogue>
    <book isbn="978-81-203-1234-5" lang="en">
        <title>Web Programming</title>
        <author>Rajiv</author>
        <isbn>978-81-203-1234-5</isbn>
        <price currency="INR">499.00</price>
        <year>2024</year>
    </book>
</catalogue>
```

### Step 3 — Decoding the DTD Grammar

| DTD Construct | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `<!ELEMENT name (content)>` | Declares element `name` and its content model |
| `(#PCDATA)` | Parsed Character Data only — no children |
| `(EMPTY)` | No content at all |
| `(ANY)` | Any content (discouraged) |
| `(a, b, c)` | Sequence: `a` then `b` then `c` |
| `(a \vert b)` | Choice: `a` or `b` |
| `(a)+` | One or more occurrences |
| `(a)*` | Zero or more |
| `(a)?` | Zero or one |
| `<!ATTLIST el name type default>` | Declares attributes of `el` |
| `#REQUIRED` | Attribute must be present |
| `#IMPLIED` | Attribute optional |
| `#FIXED "val"` | Attribute must equal `"val"` if present |

### Step 4 — External DTD Subset (Modular Validation)

For **reuse** across many documents, place the DTD in a separate file `catalogue.dtd`:

```xml
<!-- catalogue.dtd -->
<!ELEMENT catalogue (book+)>
<!ELEMENT book (title, author, isbn, price, year)>
<!ELEMENT title (#PCDATA)>
<!ELEMENT author (#PCDATA)>
<!ELEMENT isbn (#PCDATA)>
<!ELEMENT price (#PCDATA)>
<!ELEMENT year (#PCDATA)>
<!ATTLIST book isbn CDATA #REQUIRED>
```

Reference it from the XML document:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?>
<!DOCTYPE catalogue SYSTEM "catalogue.dtd">
<catalogue> ... </catalogue>
```

### Step 5 — DTD Limitations (Why XML Schema Exists)

| Limitation | Consequence |
|---|---|
| No data types (everything is text/CDATA) | Cannot enforce `price > 0` |
| Limited cardinality | Cannot easily express "1 to 5" |
| No namespace awareness | Difficult for modern XML |
| Non-XML syntax | Cannot be parsed by generic XML tools |

> [!NOTE]
> These limitations are exactly why W3C designed **XSD (XML Schema Definition)**. KTU expects students to know DTDs first, then XSD as a successor.

---

## 3.5 XML Vocabularies — Real-World Standards

An **XML vocabulary** is a *set of element and attribute names* defined for a specific domain by a standards body. Below is the KTU-essential list:

| Vocabulary | Domain | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **XHTML** | Web pages | HTML reformulated as well-formed XML |
| **SVG** | Graphics | Vector 2D images |
| **MathML** | Mathematics | Mathematical notation |
| **XSL / XSLT** | Transformation | Convert XML to HTML/PDF/other XML |
| **XPath** | Querying | Address parts of an XML document |
| **XLink / XPointer** | Hyperlinking | Multi-directional links in XML |
| **XSD** | Schema | Successor to DTD for type-rich validation |
| **SOAP** | Web services | XML-based RPC protocol |
| **WSDL** | Web services | Service description language |
| **RDF / OWL** | Semantic web | Knowledge representation |
| **RSS / Atom** | Syndication | News/blog feeds |
| **UBL** | Business | Universal Business Language (invoicing) |
| **OOXML / ODF** | Documents | `.docx` and `.odt` file formats |
| **DocBook** | Publishing | Books, articles, technical docs |
| **SMIL** | Multimedia | Synchronised multimedia integration |
| **VoiceXML** | Voice | Voice-response applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "X Marks the Vocab":** XHTML, XSLT, XPath, XLink, XSD, XPointer, XML, XQuery, XForms. Most XML vocabularies begin with **X** — but not all (SVG, RSS, SOAP, RDF, MathML don't).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — XML Document Lifecycle

| Phase | Action | Tool/Standard |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Authoring** | Create XML file | Text editor, IDE, XML Spy |
| 2. **Validation** | Check against DTD/XSD | `xmllint`, validators |
| 3. **Parsing** | Build DOM/SAX tree | `xml.dom.minidom`, SAX parsers |
| 4. **Transformation** | Convert to HTML/PDF | XSLT processor |
| 5. **Querying** | Extract data | XPath, XQuery |
| 6. **Exchange** | Transport | HTTP + SOAP, REST/XML |
| 7. **Storage** | Persist | XML databases (eXist, BaseX), or relational |
| 8. **Display** | Render in browser | XSLT + CSS, or XHTML |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
