# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The XML Document Tree (DOM Model)

This diagram represents the *catalogue* example from §3.1 as a hierarchical tree — the internal model a parser builds in memory.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[catalogue ROOT] --> B[book id 101]
    A --> C[book id 102]
    B --> D[title Web Programming]
    B --> E[author Rajiv]
    B --> F[isbn 978...]
    B --> G[price INR 499]
    B --> H[year 2024]
    B --> I[summary CDATA]
    C --> J[title Database Systems]
    C --> K[author Navathe]
    C --> L[price USD 79.99]
    C --> M[year 2023]
```

> **How to read:** Every box is an *element node*; lines are *parent–child* edges. The parser constructs this tree in memory before any XSLT/XPath query can be issued.

---

## 4.2 XML Document Processing Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

This schematic traces what happens from the moment a developer writes `data.xml` until a browser displays the result.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Author Writes XML File] --> B[XML Declaration Check]
    B --> C{Well Formed?}
    C -- No --> Z1[Parser Error Fatal]
    C -- Yes --> D[Build DOM Tree in Memory]
    D --> E{DTD or XSD Present?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Validate Against Schema]
    E -- No --> G[Skip Validation]
    F --> H{Valid?}
    G --> H
    H -- No --> Z2[Validation Error]
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply XSLT Transformation]
    I --> J[Generate Output XHTML or PDF]
    J --> K[Render in Browser or Application]
```

> **Engineering Insight:** Validation is **optional** in real-world XML processing. Browsers can render well-formed but schema-invalid XML. This is why most modern stacks use *progressive enhancement* — strict validation only at trusted boundaries (e.g., B2B gateways).

---

## 4.3 Namespace Resolution Flow

How a parser disambiguates a prefixed tag like `<lib:title>`.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Doc as XML Document
    participant Parser as XML Parser
    participant NSTable as Namespace Scope Table
    participant App as Application Code

    Parser->>Doc: Read start tag
    Doc-->>Parser: lib:title
    Parser->>NSTable: Look up prefix 'lib'
    NSTable-->>Parser: URI = http://library.org/ns
    Parser->>Parser: Expand QName to (URI, local)
    Parser-->>App: Deliver expanded name and content
```

> **Key Concept:** The parser converts a *prefixed* name into a *two-part* identifier `(URI, local-name)`. The prefix is *discarded* after resolution — the application sees only the URI, ensuring the same vocabulary is recognisable regardless of which prefix an author chose.

---

## 4.4 DTD Validation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[XML Instance Document] --> B[Parser Reads Doctype]
    B --> C{Internal or External DTD}
    C -- Internal --> D[Read Inline Subset]
    C -- External --> E[Fetch SYSTEM or PUBLIC DTD File]
    D --> F[Build Content Model Automaton]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Walk DOM Tree Element by Element]
    G --> H{Element Matches ELEMENT Rule}
    H -- Yes --> I{Attribute Matches ATTLIST Rule}
    H -- No --> X1[Report Element Content Mismatch]
    I -- Yes --> J[Document is Valid]
    I -- No --> X2[Report Attribute Violation]
```

> **Note:** DTD validators do **not** understand XML Namespaces in their original form. This is one of the historical reasons XSD replaced DTDs for new designs — but DTDs remain in KTU syllabus for foundational understanding.

---

## 4.5 XML vs JSON Decision Tree (Conceptual Schematic)

A visual guide to *when* a developer should reach for XML in 2024+.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Need to Move Data Between Systems] --> B{Need Strict Schema Validation}
    A --> C{Need Mixed Content and Markup}
    A --> D{Mobile Lightweight App}

    B -- Yes --> E[Use XML with XSD]
    B -- No --> F{Consumer is JavaScript Heavy}
    C -- Yes --> E
    C -- No --> F
    F -- Yes --> G[Use JSON]
    F -- No --> H{Enterprise Legacy Integration}
    D -- Yes --> G
    D -- No --> H
    H -- Yes --> E
    H -- No --> G
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Define XML. List any FOUR features of XML that distinguish it from HTML.**

**Model Answer:**

**XML (Extensible Markup Language)** is a W3C-standardised, text-based markup language derived from SGML, used to **store, transport, and exchange structured data** across heterogeneous systems.

| # | Distinguishing Feature | HTML | XML |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tag Set | Fixed, predefined | **User-defined, extensible** |
| 2 | Purpose | Display/presentation | **Data description/transport** |
| 3 | Case Sensitivity | Insensitive | **Sensitive** |
| 4 | Closing Tags | Optional for some | **Mandatory** |
| 5 | Error Handling | Browser-tolerant | **Strict — well-formedness required** |
| 6 | Namespace Support | None | **Built-in** |

*(Any four well-explained features earn full 3 marks. Each feature with contrast = 0.75 mark.)*

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a CDATA section in XML? When is it used? Give a small example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **CDATA (Character Data) section** is a marked block of an XML document in which the parser **does not attempt to interpret markup**. It begins with `<![CDATA[` and ends with `]]>`.

**Purpose:** It is used when text content contains characters that would otherwise be parsed as markup — for example, sample HTML code, mathematical expressions with `<` or `>`, or pre-formatted technical text.

**Example:**

```xml
<note>
    <example><![CDATA[
        <student>John</student> is a typical record.
        Use the & symbol carefully.
    ]]></example>
</note>
```

**Key Rule:** A CDATA section **cannot contain** the literal sequence `]]>` because that would terminate it prematurely. *(3 marks: definition = 1, purpose = 1, example with rule = 1.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two 7-mark sub-parts. *Students answer ONE of the two alternatives.*

---

### **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **well-formedness rules of XML** with suitable examples. Why are these rules important? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **structure of an XML document** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **PCDATA** and **CDATA**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — Well-Formedness Rules (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** A document is *well-formed* if it satisfies every syntactic requirement of the W3C XML 1.0 specification.

**The Seven Key Rules:**

1. **Single Root Element** — The document must have exactly one outermost element that contains all others. Multiple roots produce an *XML fragment*, not a *document*.
   ```xml
   <root>
       <a/><b/>
   </root>   <!-- valid -->
   ```

2. **Closing Tags Required** — Every start tag must have an end tag. Empty elements may use shorthand `<x/>`.
   ```xml
   <p>Hello</p>          <!-- correct -->
   <p>Hello              <!-- WRONG -->
   ```

3. **Case Sensitivity** — Tags `<Book>` and `<book>` are *different*.
   ```xml
   <Title>Web</Title>     <!-- distinct from <title> -->
   ```

4. **Proper Nesting** — Elements must close in **LIFO order** (last-in-first-out). Overlapping is forbidden.
   ```xml
   <!-- WRONG: <b><i>text</b></i> -->
   <b><i>text</i></b>     <!-- correct -->
   ```

5. **Quoted Attribute Values** — All attribute values must be enclosed in `"` or `'`.
   ```xml
   <book id="101"/>       <!-- correct -->
   <book id=101/>         <!-- WRONG -->
   ```

6. **Single XML Declaration Position** — If present, the XML declaration must be the first line, with no preceding whitespace.

7. **Reserved and Illegal Characters** — `<`, `>`, `&` cannot appear raw in content; they must be escaped using entities (`<`, `>`, `&`).

**Why Important:** Well-formedness enables **interoperability** — any conforming parser (browser, Java, Python) can read the document without ambiguity. It is the *baseline contract* of XML.

---

#### **Solution (b) — XML Document Structure & PCDATA vs CDATA (7 Marks)**

**Structure of an XML Document (Layered View):**

| Layer | Component | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prolog** (optional) | `<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>` |
| 2 | **Document Type Declaration** (optional) | `<!DOCTYPE root [...]>` |
| 3 | **Comments** | `<!-- ... -->` |
| 4 | **Root Element** (mandatory) | `<catalogue>...</catalogue>` |
| 5 | **Child Elements & Content** | nested tags, text |
| 6 | **Processing Instructions** | `<?xml-stylesheet ...?>` |
| 7 | **Epilogue** (optional) | whitespace and comments after root |

**Tree Diagram Representation:**

```
            [Root: catalogue]
              /          \
        [book]            [book]
       /  |  \            /  |  \
   [title][author][price] [title][author][price]
```

**PCDATA vs CDATA — Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | PCDATA (Parsed Character Data) | CDATA (Character Data) |
|---|---|---|
| **Parser Behaviour** | Parsed — entities and tags recognised | **Not** parsed — treated as raw text |
| **Syntax** | Default text content | `<![CDATA[ ... ]]>` block |
| **Entities** | `&`, `<` etc. must be used | **No** entity processing |
| **Markup Inside** | Not allowed (must be escaped) | Allowed verbatim |
| **Use Case** | Normal textual content | Code snippets, HTML samples, special symbols |

**Conclusion:** A well-formed XML document is *layered* — prolog, optional DTD, root, and content — and the choice between PCDATA and CDATA depends on whether the text contains parse-sensitive characters.

---

### **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model]`

**(a)** What are **XML Namespaces**? Explain why they are needed and how they are declared. Provide a complete example showing the resolution of a **namespace conflict** for a `<title>` element used by two different vocabularies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Document Type Definition (DTD)** with an example. Discuss **internal vs external DTDs** and list the **limitations of DTDs** that motivated XML Schema. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — XML Namespaces (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** An **XML Namespace** is a W3C-recommended mechanism (Recommendation, January 1999) to **disambiguate element and attribute names** in XML documents that combine multiple vocabularies. A namespace is identified by a **URI Reference** and bound to a **prefix** (or used as the default).

**Why Needed:**
- The same tag name (e.g., `<title>`) can mean different things in different domains.
- A single document may aggregate data from multiple XML vocabularies.
- Avoids **element name collision** and supports **modular composition**.

**Declaration Syntax:**

```xml
xmlns:prefix="URI-Reference"
```

**Complete Example Demonstrating Conflict Resolution:**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<aggregator
    xmlns:lib="http://library.org/ns"
    xmlns:edu="http://edu.org/ns"
    xmlns="http://default.org/ns">

    <lib:book lib:isbn="978-81-203-1234-5">
        <lib:title>Web Programming</lib:title>      <!-- book title -->
        <lib:author>Rajiv</lib:author>
    </lib:book>

    <edu:student id="S001">
        <title>B.Tech CSE</title>                   <!-- academic degree -->
        <year>2024</year>
    </edu:student>
</aggregator>
```

**Resolution Mechanism:**

| Prefix | URI | Local Name | Expanded QName |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lib` | `http://library.org/ns` | `title` | `{http://library.org/ns}title` |
| *(default)* | `http://edu.org/ns` | `title` | `{http://edu.org/ns}title` |

The parser expands every prefixed name into the **Clark notation** `{URI}local`. Even though both are named `title`, the URIs make them distinct.

**Important Notes:**
- The URI is an **identifier** — it need not be dereferenceable.
- The prefix `xml` and `xmlns` are reserved.
- A **default namespace** is declared by `xmlns="URI"` (no prefix), applying to all unqualified elements in scope.

---

#### **Solution (b) — DTD Explanation (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** A **Document Type Definition (DTD)** is a set of declarative rules that defines the **legal building blocks** (elements, attributes, entities, notations) of an XML document, thereby constraining its structure. A document conforming to a DTD is called *valid* (a stricter property than *well-formed*).

**Example DTD (Internal Subset):**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE library [
    <!ELEMENT library (book+)>
    <!ELEMENT book (title, author, isbn, price)>
    <!ELEMENT title (#PCDATA)>
    <!ELEMENT author (#PCDATA)>
    <!ELEMENT isbn (#PCDATA)>
    <!ELEMENT price (#PCDATA)>
    <!ATTLIST book
        isbn CDATA #REQUIRED
        lang CDATA "en">
]>
<library>
    <book isbn="101" lang="en">
        <title>XML Guide</title>
        <author>Rajiv</author>
        <isbn>101</isbn>
        <price>499</price>
    </book>
</library>
```

**Internal vs External DTDs:**

| Aspect | Internal DTD | External DTD |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Inside `[...]` of `<!DOCTYPE>` | Separate `.dtd` file |
| **Reusability** | One document only | **Shared across many documents** |
| **Syntax to Link** | — | `<!DOCTYPE root SYSTEM "file.dtd">` |
| **PUBLIC Identifier** | Not applicable | Optional for standardised vocabularies |
| **Maintenance** | Harder (changes need editing of every doc) | **Easier (single source of truth)** |

**Limitations of DTDs — Why XSD Was Needed:**

1. **No Data Types** — Everything is text (`#PCDATA` or `CDATA`); cannot enforce numeric, date, or boolean constraints.
2. **Limited Cardinality** — Only `?`, `*`, `+` quantifiers; cannot express "between 2 and 5".
3. **No Namespace Support** — Original DTDs predate namespaces.
4. **Non-XML Syntax** — DTDs use a different syntax, so generic XML tools cannot process them.
5. **Limited Extensibility** — Cannot derive new types by inheritance (no `extension` or `restriction`).
6. **No Default Values for Elements** — Only attributes can have defaults.

**Conclusion:** DTDs remain historically important and are sufficient for simple document structures, but XSD (XML Schema Definition) — which is itself an XML document with full namespace support and rich type systems — has become the modern standard for XML validation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Before Writing):**
> 1. **Mixing up DTD syntax with XSD** — DTD uses `(a, b, c)` and `(a|b)`; XSD uses `<sequence>` and `<choice>`. Writing the wrong one in the wrong question costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that XML is case-sensitive** — `<Book>` and `<book>` are *different*. Examiners look for this in design questions.
> 3. **Treating namespace URI as a URL** — The W3C explicitly states the URI need *not* be dereferenceable. Writing "the URL points to the schema" loses a mark.
> 4. **Missing the `xmlns` declaration for prefixes** — Using `bk:title` without first declaring `xmlns:bk` is a fatal error in code, and in theory questions.
> 5. **Confusing well-formedness with validity** — Well-formed = syntactically correct. Valid = conforms to a DTD/Schema. Both terms are *expected* in definitions.
> 6. **Not quoting attribute values** — `<book id=101>` is *wrong*; attribute values must be quoted.
> 7. **Placing XML declaration on line 2** — It must be the very first line. Even a single space of whitespace before it is technically an error.
> 8. **Confusing `&` vs `&`** — Writing `&` raw in content is the most common well-formedness violation.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **XML is meta-language** — it lets you invent your own tags.
- **XML separates data from display** — the opposite of HTML's mission.
- **A well-formed document has one root, balanced tags, quoted attributes, and proper nesting.**
- **A valid document is well-formed AND conforms to a DTD or XSD.**
- **Five mandatory entity references**: `<` `>` `&` `'` `"`.
- **CDATA sections** allow raw markup-resembling text without escaping.
- **XML Namespaces** solve element name collisions across vocabularies using URI identifiers.
- **Namespace prefixes are declared with `xmlns:prefix="URI"`**; the URI is an identifier, not a URL.
- **DTDs use `<!ELEMENT>` and `<!ATTLIST>`** with content models `(a, b)`, `(a|b)`, quantifiers `? * +`.
- **Internal DTDs** are inline; **External DTDs** are referenced via `SYSTEM` or `PUBLIC`.
- **DTD limitations** (no data types, no namespaces) led to **XSD** in modern systems.
- **15+ XML vocabularies** exist; recall the major ones: XHTML, SVG, MathML, XSLT, XPath, XSD, SOAP, WSDL, RSS, RDF, ODF, OOXML, DocBook.
- **The 5 forbidden character substitutions** and the **11 XML information items** are favourite viva questions.
- **Real-world XML use-cases**: web services, configuration, document formats, finance, mobile manifests.
- **Always remember**: an XML parser builds a **DOM tree** — every XML operation (XPath, XSLT, XQuery) is a tree traversal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Making Asynchronous Requests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Making Asynchronous Requests # 📘 MODULE 2 — MAKING ASYNCHRONOUS REQUESTS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Asynchronous Request (Async Request):** A network communication paradigm in web applications where the client (browser) sends an HTTP request to a server **without blocking the execution thread or reloading the current web page**. The response is handled via a callback, Promise, or event-driven mechanism once the server completes processing. The primary JavaScript mechanisms used to make asynchronous requests are: | # | Mechanism | Generation | Native Support | |---|-----------|------------|----------------| | 1 | `XMLHttpRequest` (XHR) | Legacy (1999–) | All Browsers | | 2 | `Fetch API` | Modern (ES6 / 2015–) | Modern Browsers | | 3 | `async` / `await` + Promises | ES2017+ | Modern Browsers | | 4 | Third-party libs (Axios, jQuery `.ajax()`) | Wrapper | Bundled | > [!NOTE] > **AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** is the **umbrella technique**, NOT a technology. It refers to the *pattern* of sending asynchronous HTTP requests from the browser using JavaScript and exchanging data (typically JSON today, historically XML). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter > [!NOTE] > 🍽️ **Think of a restaurant as your web page and the kitchen as the server.** - **Synchronous (Bad UX):** You walk to the kitchen, stand at the counter waiting for food, and ignore every other customer at your table. Nobody eats until the chef finishes. *(This is the OLD `form.submit()` + page reload model.)* - **Asynchronous (Good UX):** The **waiter** (the `XMLHttpRequest` / `Fetch` object) takes your order to the kitchen. You return to your table and continue talking. When the food is ready, the waiter delivers it. *(This is the AJAX model — the page never reloads, and JavaScript handles the response when it arrives.)* ### 1.3 Why Asynchronous? — The Blocking Problem ```javascript // SYNCHRONOUS (Blocking) — Page freezes! let data = fetchSync('/api/users'); // ❌ Browser hangs here console.log(data); // Never runs during the wait ``` ```javascript // ASYNCHRONOUS (Non-Blocking) — Page stays interactive fetch('/api/users') .then(res => res.json()) // Runs LATER .then(data => console.log(data)); // Page keeps working meanwhile ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** In a single-threaded JavaScript runtime, asynchronous requests are handled by the **Event Loop** and the **Web APIs** of the browser. The request is sent via a background thread; the main thread continues executing and processes the response only when it returns (the *callback* fires). --- ## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **XHR** | `XMLHttpRequest` object — the original AJAX workhorse | | **Fetch** | Modern Promise-based replacement for XHR | | **Promise** | A proxy for a value not yet known (ES6 feature) | | **CORS** | Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — server-side permission | | **Same-Origin Policy** | Browser security model restricting cross-domain calls | | **REST** | Architectural style: GET / POST / PUT / DELETE on resources | | **JSON** | JavaScript Object Notation — today's de-facto data format | | **readyState** | Integer 0–4 tracking XHR lifecycle | | **HTTP Status Code** | 3-digit server response code (200, 404, 500, …) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Asynchronous Request Lifecycle The request undergoes a **well-defined 5-stage lifecycle**. Mastering these stages is essential for KTU exam answers. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Stage 1 — Object Creation** A request object is instantiated. Either: - `const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();` (legacy), OR - `fetch(url, options)` is invoked (modern, returns a Promise). 2. **Stage 2 — Initialization** The HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.), target URL, and async flag are set. - XHR: `xhr.open("GET", "/api/data", true);` - The 3rd argument `true` ⇒ **asynchronous** (default in Fetch). 3. **Stage 3 — Header Configuration** Metadata about the request is configured (e.g., `Content-Type`, `Authorization`). - XHR: `xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/json");` - Fetch: pass `headers` inside the options object. 4. **Stage 4 — Sending** The request is dispatched to the server. The JS thread is *not* blocked. - XHR: `xhr.send(payload);` - Fetch: the Promise is dispatched automatically; `.then()` attaches callbacks. 5. **Stage 5 — Response Handling** The browser fires events / resolves the Promise when the server replies. - XHR: `xhr.onreadystatechange = function() { … }` - Fetch: `.then(response => response.json())` --- ## 2.2 The XMLHttpRequest `readyState` Lifecycle This is a **favourite KTU question**. Memorize the table: | readyState | Name | Description | |:----------:|------|-------------| | **0** | `UNSENT` | `open()` not yet called | | **1** | `OPENED` | `open()` invoked | | **2** | `HEADERS_RECEIVED` | `send()` called, headers received | | **3** | `LOADING` | Downloading; `responseText` partially available | | **4** | `DONE` | Operation complete — all data received | > [!IMPORTANT] > **In KTU answers, ALWAYS check `xhr.readyState === 4` AND `xhr.status === 200`** before reading the response. This is the standard valuation pattern. --- ## 2.3 HTTP Methods Used in Async Requests | Method | Idempotent | Body? | Typical Use | |--------|:----------:|:-----:|-------------| | **GET** | ✅ | ❌ | Retrieve data (search, fetch list) | | **POST** | ❌ | ✅ | Create a new resource (sign up, submit form) | | **PUT** | ✅ | ✅ | Replace an existing resource | | **PATCH** | ❌ | ✅ | Partial update of a resource | | **DELETE** | ✅ | ❌ | Remove a resource | --- ## 2.4 HTTP Status Codes (Must-Know for KTU) | Code Range | Category | Common Examples | |:----------:|----------|-----------------| | **1xx** | Informational | `100 Continue` | | **2xx** | **Success** | `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content` | | **3xx** | Redirection | `301 Moved Permanently`, `304 Not Modified` | | **4xx** | **Client Error** | `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `404 Not Found` | | **5xx** | **Server Error** | `500 Internal Server Error`, `503 Service Unavailable` | > [!WARNING] > **Examiners LOVE asking:** "What is the difference between 401 and 403?" > - `401 Unauthorized` → **Authentication** missing or invalid (you haven't logged in). > - `403 Forbidden` → **Authorization** failed (you ARE logged in, but lack permission). --- ## 2.5 The XHR vs Fetch API Comparison (High-Yield) | Feature | `XMLHttpRequest` | `Fetch API` | |---------|:----------------:|:-----------:| | Return type | None (uses events) | **Promise** | | Syntax | Verbose, event-based | Clean, chainable | | Streaming | ❌ | ✅ | | Abort support | `xhr.abort()` | `AbortController` | | Cookies auto-sent | ✅ (default) | ❌ (need `credentials: 'include'`) | | Error on HTTP 404 | ❌ (only on network fail) | ✅ (Promise resolves, but `ok: false`) | | Browser Support | All (legacy included) | Modern only | | Event handlers | `onload`, `onerror`, `onprogress` | `.then`, `.catch`, `AbortController` | --- ## 2.6 Promises — The Theoretical Foundation > [!NOTE] > A **Promise** is an object representing the *eventual completion (or failure)* of an asynchronous operation. It exists in one of three states: | State | Meaning | |-------|---------| | `pending` | Initial state — neither fulfilled nor rejected | | `fulfilled` | Operation completed successfully → `.then()` runs | | `rejected` | Operation failed → `.catch()` runs | ``` pending ──success──▶ fulfilled ──▶ .then(onFulfilled) │ └────failure────▶ rejected ──▶ .catch(onRejected) ``` --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Asynchronous requests power nearly every modern web feature: - **Live search** (Google Suggest, Amazon autocomplete) - **Social feeds** (infinite scroll on Twitter, Instagram) - **Form validation** (email availability check during sign-up) - **Single Page Applications (SPA)** (Gmail, Trello, Figma) - **Real-time dashboards** (stock tickers, IoT monitoring) - **Chat applications** (WhatsApp Web, Slack) # 3. Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > Below are **fully expanded, line-by-line code derivations** for both XHR and Fetch. Each line is annotated to match KTU's step-marker evaluation style. --- ## 3.1 WORKED EXAMPLE A — `XMLHttpRequest` (GET Request) ```javascript // ============================================= // EXHAUSTIVE WALKTHROUGH — XHR GET REQUEST // ============================================= // STEP 1: Instantiate the XHR object // WHY: This creates the request "vehicle" backed by browser C++ networking code. const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest(); // STEP 2: Register an event handler for state changes // WHY: The browser fires this on EVERY readyState transition (0→1→2→3→4). xhr.onreadystatechange = function () { // STEP 2a: Check if the request has FINISHED // WHY: readyState 4 = DONE. Earlier states have incomplete data. if (xhr.readyState === 4) { // STEP 2b: Check HTTP success // WHY: status 200 = OK. Anything else means an error to handle. if (xhr.status === 200) { // STEP 2c: Parse the JSON string into a JS object // WHY: The server returns TEXT, not a JS object. const data = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText); // STEP 2d: Use the data — e.g., inject into the DOM document.getElementById("output").innerHTML = "Hello, " + data.name + "!"; } else { // STEP 2e: Handle HTTP error console.error("Server error:", xhr.status); } } }; // STEP 3: Initialize the request // WHY: "GET" = method, "api/user.json" = endpoint, true = async. xhr.open("GET", "api/user.json", true); // STEP 4: Set optional request headers (commented for GET) // xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/json"); // STEP 5: Send the request — payload is null for GET xhr.send(null); ``` ### Evaluation Trace | Line | Action | Browser Internals | |------|--------|-------------------| | `new XMLHttpRequest()` | Create XHR | Allocates C++ network object | | `xhr.open("GET", …)` | Configure | Validates URL, queues state change to 1 | | `xhr.send(null)` | Dispatch | Sends to background thread; main JS continues | | (Server processes) | Network IO | Main thread is **free** | | State changes 2→3 | `onreadystatechange` fires | `responseText` updates as data arrives | | State 4 arrives | Final fire | `responseText` is complete | --- ## 3.2 WORKED EXAMPLE B — `XMLHttpRequest` (POST Request with JSON) ```javascript // ============================================= // EXHAUSTIVE WALKTHROUGH — XHR POST REQUEST // ============================================= const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest(); const url = "https://api.example.com/users"; // Event handler — fires 4 times during the request lifecycle xhr.onreadystatechange = function () { if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 201) { // 201 Created — typical for successful POST const newUser = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText); console.log("Created user with ID:", newUser.id); } else if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status >= 400) { console.error("Failed:", xhr.status, xhr.responseText); } }; // Configure as POST xhr.open("POST", url, true); // CRITICAL: tell the server we're sending JSON xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/json"); // Build the payload as a JSON string const payload = JSON.stringify({ name: "Anu Krishnan", email: "anu@ktu.ac.in", role: "student" }); // Send the request WITH the body xhr.send(payload); ``` --- ## 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE C — `Fetch API` (Modern Approach) ```javascript // ============================================= // EXHAUSTIVE WALKTHROUGH — FETCH GET REQUEST // ============================================= // STEP 1: Call fetch() — it returns a Promise immediately // WHY: Promise is "pending" until the network I/O completes. fetch("https://api.example.com/users/42") // STEP 2: First .then() — convert the raw Response to JSON // WHY: response.json() itself returns a Promise. .then(function (response) { // Check the 'ok' flag (true for 200-299 status codes) if (!response.ok) { throw new Error("HTTP " + response.status); } return response.json(); // Returns a Promise → chain continues }) // STEP 3: Second .then() — now we have the parsed JS object .then(function (user) { console.log("Fetched user:", user.name); }) // STEP 4: Catch any error from anywhere in the chain .catch(function (err) { console.error("Network or parse failure:", err.message); }); ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** `fetch()` does **NOT** reject on HTTP errors (e.g., 404 or 500). The Promise *resolves* with a `Response` object whose `ok` property is `false`. You must check `response.ok` manually — this is the most common valuation deduction point. --- ## 3.4 WORKED EXAMPLE D — `async` / `await` (Syntactic Sugar over Promises) ```javascript // ============================================= // EXHAUSTIVE WALKTHROUGH — ASYNC/AWAIT POST // ============================================= async function createUser(userData) { // The 'await' keyword pauses THIS function (not the whole page!) // WHY: Awaits the Promise to settle; the event loop continues serving the UI. try { const response = await fetch("https://api.example.com/users", { method: "POST", headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" }, body: JSON.stringify(userData) // JS object → JSON string }); if (!response.ok) { throw new Error("HTTP " + response.status); } // Await again to deserialize the response const created = await response.json(); console.log("User created with ID:", created.id); return created; } catch (error) { console.error("Failed to create user:", error.message); // Re-throw if the caller needs to know throw error; } } // Invoking the async function createUser({ name: "Rahul", email: "rahul@ktu.in" }); ``` ### Analytical Comparison Table | Aspect | Callbacks | Promises | async/await | |--------|:---------:|:--------:|:-----------:| | Readability | ❌ (Pyramid of Doom) | ✅ | ✅✅ (Best) | | Error Handling | Manual | `.catch()` | `try/catch` | | Parallelism | Manual | `Promise.all()` | `Promise.all()` | | Modern Standard | Legacy | ES6+ | ES2017+ | --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Method Should I Use?" ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ QUESTION: When do I use XHR vs Fetch in KTU 2024 answers? │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ Need to support IE / very old browsers? ──YES──▶ XMLHttpRequest │ NO │ ┌─────────────────────────┴────────────────────┐ │ │ Reading server-sent data Using Promises (file uploads, progress bar) throughout codebase │ │ ▼ ▼ XMLHttpRequest Fetch API (xhr.upload.onprogress) (cleaner code) ``` # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Asynchronous Request–Response Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[USER ACTION in Browser
e.g. button click] --> B[JS Event Handler fires] B --> C[Create XHR or call fetch] C --> D[open method sets URL and HTTP verb] D --> E[setRequestHeader if needed] E --> F[send payload] F --> G[Browser dispatches request
via background Web API thread] G --> H[Main JS thread is FREE
UI stays responsive] G --> I[Server processes request] I --> J[Server sends HTTP Response] J --> K[Browser receives response] K --> L{Is the data ready?} L -- readyState 4 --> M[Trigger callback or resolve Promise] L -- earlier states --> N[Wait for next event tick] M --> O[Parse JSON or XML] O --> P[Update DOM with new data] P --> Q[User sees updated content
NO PAGE RELOAD] ``` ## 4.2 XHR readyState State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> UNSENT UNSENT --> OPENED : open called OPENED --> HEADERS_RECEIVED : send called
response headers arrive HEADERS_RECEIVED --> LOADING : response body downloading LOADING --> DONE : transfer complete DONE --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Promise State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> pending pending --> fulfilled : resolve value pending --> rejected : reject error fulfilled --> [*] rejected --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Synchronous vs Asynchronous ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SYNC[OLD SYNCHRONOUS MODEL] A1[Browser submits form] --> A2[Full page unloads] A2 --> A3[Server processes] A3 --> A4[Server returns full HTML] A4 --> A5[Browser re-renders whole page] end subgraph ASYNC[MODERN ASYNC MODEL] B1[JS fires fetch or XHR] --> B2[Background network thread] B2 --> B3[Server processes small payload] B3 --> B4[Server returns JSON or XML] B4 --> B5[JS callback or .then runs] B5 --> B6[DOM is updated in place] end ``` ## 4.5 Request Anatomy — What Goes On the Wire ```mermaid flowchart TB REQ[HTTP REQUEST STRUCTURE] --> REQ1[Request Line
GET or POST plus URL plus HTTP version] REQ --> REQ2[Headers
Host, User Agent, Content Type, Authorization] REQ --> REQ3[Empty Line] REQ --> REQ4[Body
JSON or form data or empty for GET] RES[HTTP RESPONSE STRUCTURE] --> RES1[Status Line
HTTP version plus code plus reason] RES --> RES2[Headers
Content Type, Set Cookie, Cache Control] RES --> RES3[Empty Line] RES --> RES4[Body
JSON or XML or HTML] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a request-response diagram, label the **status line**, **headers**, and **body** distinctly. Examiners award partial marks for the *anatomy*, not just the flow. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > - ❌ Forgetting to check `xhr.status === 200` before reading data → **−2 marks** > - ❌ Confusing `401 Unauthorized` with `403 Forbidden` → **−1 mark** > - ❌ Saying `fetch()` throws on 404 — **wrong**, it resolves; you must check `response.ok` → **−1 mark** > - ❌ Writing `fetch()` without using `.then()` / `await` → **−2 marks** (incomplete async pattern) > - ❌ Mixing up `readyState` values 2 and 3 → **−1 mark** --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Define AJAX. List any four advantages of making asynchronous requests in a web application.** **Model Answer:** > **AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** is a web development technique that uses a combination of: > - `XMLHttpRequest` object (or modern `Fetch API`), > - JavaScript and DOM, and > - A data interchange format such as JSON or XML, > > to send and receive data from a server **asynchronously** — without requiring a full page reload. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Improved User Experience (UX):** Pages do not reload, so the application feels faster and more responsive. 2. **Reduced Bandwidth:** Only the necessary data (e.g., JSON) is exchanged, not entire HTML pages. 3. **Increased Interactivity:** Enables real-time features like live search, autocomplete, and chat. 4. **Decoupled Front-End and Back-End:** The server can expose a clean REST API consumed by any client. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Explain the five `readyState` values of the `XMLHttpRequest` object.** **Model Answer:** | Value | Name | Description | |:-----:|------|-------------| | `0` | `UNSENT` | The `open()` method has not been called yet. | | `1` | `OPENED` | The `open()` method has been invoked; request headers can be set. | | `2` | `HEADERS_RECEIVED` | The `send()` method has been called; response headers are available. | | `3` | `LOADING` | The response body is being downloaded; `responseText` holds partial data. | | `4` | `DONE` | The entire operation is complete; data is ready in `responseText`. | > **Examination Cue:** Examiners specifically look for the **numeric-to-name mapping** and the order — memorize all 5. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the lifecycle of an asynchronous HTTP request made using the `XMLHttpRequest` object. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a complete JavaScript program that uses `XMLHttpRequest` to send a POST request with a JSON payload to `https://api.example.com/students` and display the server response in a `
`. Assume the server returns the created record with HTTP status `201`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Lifecycle of an Asynchronous HTTP Request (XHR)** The complete lifecycle of an XHR request consists of **five operational stages**: 1. **Object Instantiation** — A new `XMLHttpRequest` object is created in JavaScript memory, backed by the browser's networking engine. 2. **Configuration (`open`)** — The HTTP method (GET/POST), target URL, and asynchronous flag (always `true` for async) are configured. 3. **Header Setup** — Optional request headers such as `Content-Type` and `Authorization` are appended. 4. **Dispatch (`send`)** — The request is fired to the server via a background thread managed by the browser's Web APIs. The main JavaScript thread is **not blocked**. 5. **Response Handling** — The `onreadystatechange` event handler is invoked at each `readyState` transition. When `readyState === 4` and `status === 200` (or `201`), the response data in `responseText` is parsed and the DOM is updated. ``` [Creation] → [open] → [setRequestHeader] → [send] → [readyState 0→1→2→3→4] → [Callback fires] ``` > The browser's **Event Loop** ensures the user interface remains interactive throughout, because only the callback runs on the main thread — the actual network I/O occurs on a separate thread. --- **(b) Complete JavaScript Program** ```javascript // ===================================================== // COMPLETE WORKING CODE — XHR POST WITH JSON // ===================================================== // STEP 1: Build the payload as a JavaScript object const studentData = { name: "Meera Nair", rollNo: "TVE21CS045", branch: "CSE", semester: 6 }; // STEP 2: Create the XHR object const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest(); // STEP 3: Define the callback for state changes xhr.onreadystatechange = function () { // Wait until the request is FULLY done if (xhr.readyState === 4) { if (xhr.status === 201) { // SUCCESS — server created the resource const created = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText); // Inject the response into the DOM document.getElementById("result").innerHTML = `

Student Created!

ID: ${created.id}

Name: ${created.name}

Status: ${created.status}

`; } else { // FAILURE — show the error code document.getElementById("result").innerHTML = `

Error ${xhr.status}: ${xhr.statusText}

`; } } }; // STEP 4: Configure as POST xhr.open("POST", "https://api.example.com/students", true); // STEP 5: Set the Content-Type header — mandatory for JSON xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/json"); // STEP 6: Serialize the JS object into a JSON string and send xhr.send(JSON.stringify(studentData)); ``` **Line-by-Line Justification (for valuation):** | Code Line | Why It Matters | |-----------|----------------| | `new XMLHttpRequest()` | Creates the request-handler object | | `xhr.open("POST", url, true)` | Sets verb, URL, and async mode | | `setRequestHeader("Content-Type", …)` | Tells server to expect JSON | | `JSON.stringify(studentData)` | Converts JS object → string (required for network) | | `xhr.send(payload)` | Dispatches the request | | `readyState === 4` | Means transfer is COMPLETE | | `status === 201` | Means resource CREATED (correct for POST) | | `JSON.parse(xhr.responseText)` | Converts JSON string → JS object | | `getElementById("result").innerHTML` | Updates the DOM with the response | --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — ALTERNATIVE** **(a)** Compare `XMLHttpRequest` and the modern `Fetch API` in terms of syntax, return type, error handling, and browser support. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a complete JavaScript program using **async/await** that fetches a list of products from `https://fakestoreapi.com/products`, displays the first 5 products' titles and prices in a `
    `, and gracefully handles network errors. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Comparison Table — XHR vs Fetch** | Criterion | `XMLHttpRequest` | `Fetch API` | |-----------|------------------|-------------| | **Release Era** | 1999 (Legacy) | 2015 (ES6 / Modern) | | **Return Type** | None (event-driven) | A `Promise` | | **Syntax Style** | Verbose; uses properties like `xhr.responseText` | Concise; chains `.then() / .catch()` | | **Error Handling** | Distinguishes HTTP errors from network errors | Does **NOT** reject on HTTP errors — must check `response.ok` | | **Async Pattern** | Callback-based via `onreadystatechange` | Promise-based, supports `async/await` | | **Streaming** | Limited | Supports `response.body.getReader()` | | **Aborting Requests** | `xhr.abort()` | `AbortController` + `signal` | | **Browser Support** | All browsers including legacy IE | Modern browsers only (no IE) | | **Progress Events** | `xhr.upload.onprogress` (works for uploads) | Limited; needs `ReadableStream` | | **Cookies** | Sent automatically | Must set `credentials: 'include'` for cross-origin | > **Conclusion:** For new KTU-relevant projects, **Fetch API + async/await** is the preferred modern standard. `XMLHttpRequest` is still used when progress tracking for file uploads is required. --- **(b) Complete Program — `async/await` Product Fetch** ```javascript // ===================================================== // COMPLETE WORKING CODE — async/await with error handling // ===================================================== async function displayProducts() { // The URL of the public fake store API const URL = "https://fakestoreapi.com/products"; try { // Await the initial network response const response = await fetch(URL); // CRITICAL: fetch() doesn't throw on 4xx/5xx — check manually if (!response.ok) { throw new Error(`HTTP error! Status: ${response.status}`); } // Await the JSON parsing const products = await response.json(); // Take only the first 5 products const top5 = products.slice(0, 5); // Build the HTML list const listItems = top5 .map(p => `
  • ${p.title} — ₹${p.price}
    • `) .join(""); // Inject into the DOM document.getElementById("productList").innerHTML = listItems; } catch (error) { // Graceful error handling document.getElementById("productList").innerHTML = `
  • Failed to load products: ${error.message}
    • `; } } // Invoke the async function displayProducts(); ``` **Program Walkthrough (for KTU evaluation):** 1. The function is declared with `async`, enabling use of `await` inside. 2. The first `await fetch(URL)` suspends this function until the headers arrive. 3. `response.ok` is checked — this is the **Fetch-specific** step students often miss. 4. The second `await response.json()` deserializes the body. 5. `Array.slice(0, 5)` limits to the first 5 products. 6. `Array.map` + `join` build a single HTML string. 7. The `try/catch` block guarantees the UI shows a message even on failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Tip:** When writing async/await code, ALWAYS show the `try/catch` block. Examiners allocate **at least 1 mark** specifically for error handling. --- ## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > **Use this as your last-minute cheat sheet the night before the exam.** ### ✅ Definitions - [ ] **AJAX** = technique/pattern, not a technology - [ ] **Async Request** = non-blocking HTTP call from the browser - [ ] **Promise** = proxy for a future value (3 states: pending, fulfilled, rejected) - [ ] **CORS** = server policy allowing cross-origin requests - [ ] **Same-Origin Policy** = browser default blocking cross-origin requests ### ✅ APIs - [ ] `XMLHttpRequest` — legacy, event-based, supports upload progress - [ ] `Fetch API` — modern, Promise-based, requires manual `response.ok` check - [ ] `async/await` — syntactic sugar over Promises (ES2017+) ### ✅ Lifecycle (XHR) - [ ] `readyState` values: **0, 1, 2, 3, 4** (UNSENT, OPENED, HEADERS_RECEIVED, LOADING, DONE) - [ ] Always check `readyState === 4` **AND** `status === 200` before reading data - [ ] JSON payload requires `JSON.stringify()` to send and `JSON.parse()` to receive ### ✅ HTTP Essentials - [ ] GET (no body, idempotent) vs POST (with body, creates resource) - [ ] Status code categories: 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error - [ ] `401` = unauthenticated; `403` = unauthorized (permission denied) ### ✅ Fetch API Gotchas - [ ] Does **NOT** throw on HTTP errors — must check `response.ok` - [ ] Cookies are **NOT** sent cross-origin by default — set `credentials: 'include'` - [ ] POST with JSON requires `headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" }` ### ✅ Code Patterns to Memorize - [ ] XHR setup: `new → open → setRequestHeader → send → onreadystatechange` - [ ] Fetch chain: `fetch().then(r => r.json()).then(data).catch(err)` - [ ] async/await: `try { await fetch … } catch (e) { … }` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Mantra:** *"AJAX is a pattern. XHR is the old vehicle. Fetch is the modern vehicle. Promises are the engine. The Event Loop keeps the page alive."*

topics.title: Complete Control over AJAX
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Complete Control over AJAX

# Complete Control over AJAX — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** is a web development technique that enables a web page to communicate with a server in the **background** without requiring a full page reload. The term "**Complete Control over AJAX**" refers to the low-level, programmatic manipulation of the **`XMLHttpRequest` (XHR) object** — the foundational browser API that empowers developers to fine-tune every aspect of asynchronous communication, including HTTP verb selection, headers, request bodies, timeouts, response parsing, and event lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 PECST742 syllabus (Module 2)**, "Complete Control over AJAX" emphasizes mastering the **properties, methods, ready states, and event handling** of the native `XMLHttpRequest` object (and optionally the modern `fetch` API as an evolution) — without relying on high-level jQuery wrappers like `$.ajax()`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala State IT Mission / Industry Relevance:** Every dynamic feature in services like Kerala e-Governance portals (e.g., SPOT, K-Smart), IRCTC booking, and Gmail's inbox relies on the same XHR/fetch lifecycle being exercised with *complete control* to ensure security, speed, and reliability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter Model

Imagine you are dining at a busy restaurant:

| Actor | Real-World Role | AJAX Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **You (Customer)** | Place an order for soup | JavaScript initiates a `request` |
| **Waiter** | Takes order, brings soup, tells you the status | The `XMLHttpRequest` object |
| **Kitchen** | Prepares the soup | The **Web Server** (e.g., Node.js, PHP, Python) |
| **Order Slip** | Defines the dish, spice level, table number | **HTTP Method, URL, Headers, Body** |
| **Waiter Report-Back** | "Soup is ready, here it is." | The `onreadystatechange` event firing with `readyState = 4` |
| **Leaving the Table** | Other customers can still order while you wait | **Asynchronous** behavior — the UI is **not blocked** |

In a **non-AJAX** (traditional) model, *you* would have to walk to the kitchen, wait for the soup, and return before doing anything else. With AJAX, the **waiter (XHR)** handles everything in parallel while you continue reading the menu — that is **"Complete Control"**: you dictate exactly how the waiter operates.

## 1.3 Why "Complete Control" Matters

| Control Aspect | Why It Matters (Engineering Justification) |
|---|---|
| **HTTP Verb Selection** | RESTful design demands `GET` for reads, `POST` for creation, `PUT/PATCH` for updates, `DELETE` for removals. |
| **Custom Headers** | Carrying JWT tokens, CSRF tokens, content-type negotiation. |
| **Timeouts** | Prevents the UI from hanging forever on a dead server (resilience). |
| **Response Type** | Choosing between `text`, `JSON`, `document`, `blob`, or `arraybuffer`. |
| **Synchronous vs. Asynchronous** | Synchronous requests freeze the UI — useful only in legacy `Worker` threads. |
| **Progress Tracking** | `xhr.upload.onprogress` for upload bars, `xhr.onprogress` for downloads. |
| **Abort Capability** | `xhr.abort()` cancels in-flight requests when a user navigates away — saves bandwidth and CPU. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stat Box (Industry Metric):** The median payload of an XHR call in modern SPAs (Single-Page Applications like React/Vue) is **~8 KB**, with round-trip times under **150 ms** on broadband. Complete control is what keeps these numbers tight.

## 1.4 The XHR Object — The Heart of Complete Control

The `XMLHttpRequest` object is a **built-in browser constructor** (no library needed) that exposes the entire HTTP transaction to JavaScript. It is supported by **all evergreen browsers** (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari) — making it the bedrock of cross-browser AJAX.

```javascript
// Step 1: Instantiate the "waiter"
const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
```

This single line creates the conduit through which **all** configuration, sending, and response handling flows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask: *"What is the role of the XMLHttpRequest object in AJAX?"* — Answer: **It is the browser-provided API that enables JavaScript to send HTTP/HTTPS requests to a server and process the response without reloading the page, providing complete programmatic control over request configuration, transmission, and response handling.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Lifecycle of an AJAX Request (Conceptual Logic)

The "Complete Control" workflow is best understood as a strict sequence of phases. Skipping a phase or mis-ordering them is a common source of bugs.

1. **Instantiation Phase** — `new XMLHttpRequest()` creates the object.
2. **Configuration Phase** — `xhr.open(method, url, async, user, pass)` *initializes* (but does not send) the request.
3. **Header Phase (Optional but critical for POST/PUT)** — `xhr.setRequestHeader(name, value)` adds HTTP headers.
4. **Dispatch Phase** — `xhr.send(body)` transmits the request to the server.
5. **Response Phase (Asynchronous)** — The `readystatechange` event fires repeatedly as `readyState` evolves from `0 → 4`.

> [!NOTE]
> The `open()` method only **prepares** the request. The actual network activity begins **only** when `send()` is called — a frequent interview/KTU question.

## 2.2 The `readyState` Machine — Five Discrete States

| `readyState` Value | Name | Meaning (What Happened) | Developer Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | `UNSENT` | Object created, `open()` not yet called | Configure request |
| **1** | `OPENED` | `open()` has been called; headers can be set | Add headers, prepare payload |
| **2** | `HEADERS_RECEIVED` | `send()` called; response headers available | Inspect `getAllResponseHeaders()` |
| **3** | `LOADING` | Response body is downloading in chunks | Stream processing (rare) |
| **4** | `DONE` | Operation complete — success or failure | Validate `status` and parse `responseText` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`readyState === 4` is necessary but not sufficient** for success. You **must also** check `xhr.status === 200` to confirm a successful HTTP response. A `readyState = 4` with `status = 404` means the server replied *that the page was not found*.

## 2.3 The `xhr.open(method, url, async)` Method — Request Initialization Signature

The full signature is:

```javascript
xhr.open(method, url, async, user, pass);
```

| Parameter | Type | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|---|
| `method` | String | HTTP verb: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `PATCH`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS` | **None — required** |
| `url` | String | Target endpoint (relative or absolute) | **None — required** |
| `async` | Boolean | `true` = asynchronous (recommended), `false` = synchronous (blocks UI) | `true` |
| `user`, `pass` | String | Optional HTTP basic auth credentials | `null` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Synchronous requests (`async = false`) are **deprecated** in the main thread. The W3C/XHR2 spec states they may throw an `InvalidAccessError` in future browsers. KTU examiners may mark answers using `false` for `async` in a UI context as incorrect.

## 2.4 The `xhr.send(body)` Method — Dispatch Signature

| `body` Type | Use Case | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `null` (or omitted) | `GET`/`HEAD` requests | `xhr.send()` |
| `String` | URL-encoded form data, JSON | `xhr.send('name=Anu&age=21')` |
| `FormData` | File uploads, multi-part forms | `xhr.send(formData)` |
| `Blob` / `ArrayBuffer` | Binary uploads | `xhr.send(blob)` |
| `URLSearchParams` | Modern URL-encoded | `xhr.send(new URLSearchParams({...}))` |

## 2.5 Key Properties of the `XMLHttpRequest` Object

| Property | Description | Type | Read/Write |
|---|---|---|---|
| `readyState` | Current state (0–4) | Number | R |
| `status` | HTTP status code (200, 404, 500, etc.) | Number | R |
| `statusText` | Status text ("OK", "Not Found") | String | R |
| `responseText` | Raw response as string | String | R |
| `responseXML` | Response parsed as XML `Document` | Document | R |
| `response` | Modern unified response body | Various | R |
| `responseType` | Declares expected type: `''`, `text`, `json`, `blob`, `arraybuffer`, `document` | String | R/W |
| `responseURL` | Final URL after redirects | String | R |
| `withCredentials` | Whether to send cookies / auth headers cross-origin | Boolean | R/W |
| `timeout` | Time in milliseconds before auto-abort | Number | R/W |
| `upload` | `XMLHttpRequestUpload` object for upload events | Object | R |
| `onreadystatechange` | Legacy event handler | Function | R/W |
| `onload`, `onerror`, `onabort`, `ontimeout` | Modern event handlers | Function | R/W |
| `onprogress` | Download progress | Function | R/W |

## 2.6 Key Methods of the `XMLHttpRequest` Object

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `open(method, url, async, user, pass)` | Initialize the request |
| `send(body)` | Dispatch the request |
| `abort()` | Cancel the in-flight request |
| `setRequestHeader(name, value)` | Add a custom HTTP header |
| `getResponseHeader(name)` | Retrieve a specific response header |
| `getAllResponseHeaders()` | Retrieve all response headers as a CRLF-separated string |
| `overrideMimeType(mime)` | Force interpretation of response as a specific MIME type |

## 2.7 The HTTP Status Code Cheat Sheet (KTU High-Yield)

| Range | Category | Examples | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx** | Informational | 100, 101 | Request received, continuing |
| **2xx** | Success | **200 OK**, 201 Created, 204 No Content | Request succeeded |
| **3xx** | Redirection | 301 Moved, 304 Not Modified | Further action required |
| **4xx** | Client Error | **400 Bad Request**, **401 Unauthorized**, **403 Forbidden**, **404 Not Found** | Problem with the request |
| **5xx** | Server Error | **500 Internal Server Error**, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable | Server-side failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** **"2 Good, 4 You, 5 Server"** — 2xx = success, 4xx = client's fault, 5xx = server's fault.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How Complete AJAX Control Is Used |
|---|---|
| **IRCTC Tatkal Booking** | Custom `POST` with session cookie, JSON body, abort on timeout |
| **Google Search Autocomplete** | `GET` with debounce, `responseType = 'json'`, `abort()` stale requests |
| **Facebook Live Video Upload** | `FormData` with `xhr.upload.onprogress` for the progress bar |
| **Stock Trading Dashboard** | `setInterval` polling with `timeout` of 5s, `onerror` retry logic |
| **Online Code Editors (CodeSandbox, Replit)** | Auto-save using `PUT` with `If-Match` ETag headers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Complete AJAX Workflows

## 3.1 Derivation of the Canonical GET-Request Pattern

We will derive, line by line, the **complete** code for a fully-controlled AJAX GET request.

**Step 1 — Instantiate the XHR object.**

```javascript
const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
```

*Reasoning:* A new XHR object starts in `readyState = 0` (UNSENT). No network activity has occurred.

**Step 2 — Attach the state-change listener (BEFORE `open()` is best practice, but the spec allows it any time).**

```javascript
xhr.onreadystatechange = function() {
    if (xhr.readyState === 4) {
        if (xhr.status === 200) {
            console.log("Server replied:", xhr.responseText);
        } else {
            console.error("HTTP error:", xhr.status, xhr.statusText);
        }
    }
};
```

*Reasoning:* The handler fires on **every** `readyState` transition. We narrow to `4` (DONE), then verify HTTP `200` to confirm success.

**Step 3 — Initialize the request with `open()`.**

```javascript
xhr.open('GET', 'https://api.kerala.gov.in/data.json', true);
```

*Reasoning:* `open()` transitions the object to `readyState = 1` (OPENED). No data is sent yet. The `true` flag confirms asynchronous behavior.

**Step 4 — (Optional) Set request headers.**

```javascript
xhr.setRequestHeader('Accept', 'application/json');
xhr.setRequestHeader('X-Requested-With', 'XMLHttpRequest');
```

*Reasoning:* Headers can **only** be set after `open()` and **before** `send()`. The `X-Requested-With` header is a server-side convention to detect AJAX requests in frameworks like Laravel and Django.

**Step 5 — Dispatch the request.**

```javascript
xhr.send();
```

*Reasoning:* For `GET` requests, the body is `null` (or omitted). This transitions the object to `readyState = 2`, then `3` (as data streams in), then finally `4` (DONE).

**Final Canonical Block (Compiled):**

```javascript
const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();

xhr.onreadystatechange = function() {
    if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200) {
        const data = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText);
        console.log(data);
    } else if (xhr.readyState === 4) {
        console.error("Failed with status:", xhr.status);
    }
};

xhr.open('GET', 'https://api.kerala.gov.in/data.json', true);
xhr.setRequestHeader('Accept', 'application/json');
xhr.send();
```

## 3.2 Derivation of a Fully-Controlled POST with JSON Body

```javascript
const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();

xhr.open('POST', '/api/students', true);

xhr.setRequestHeader('Content-Type', 'application/json; charset=UTF-8');
xhr.setRequestHeader('Authorization', 'Bearer ' + token);

xhr.onload = function() {
    if (xhr.status >= 200 && xhr.status < 300) {
        const result = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText);
        console.log("Saved:", result);
    } else {
        console.error("Server rejected:", xhr.status);
    }
};

xhr.onerror = function() {
    console.error("Network failure — server unreachable.");
};

xhr.timeout = 5000;          // 5-second timeout
xhr.ontimeout = function() {
    console.warn("Request timed out after 5s.");
};

const payload = JSON.stringify({ name: "Anu", cgpa: 9.2 });
xhr.send(payload);
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. `open('POST', '/api/students', true)` — prepares a POST to the local API.
2. `setRequestHeader('Content-Type', ...)` — **mandatory** for JSON; the server uses this to parse the body.
3. `setRequestHeader('Authorization', ...)` — sends the JWT/OAuth token.
4. `onload` — fires only on `readyState = 4` AND a successful response. **Modern alternative** to the `readyState`/`status` double-check.
5. `onerror` — fires on network failure (DNS error, CORS rejection, server down).
6. `timeout = 5000` + `ontimeout` — sets a hard ceiling on request duration.
7. `JSON.stringify(...)` — converts the JS object to a JSON string (required because XHR sends text, not objects).
8. `send(payload)` — transmits the JSON string as the request body.

## 3.3 Derivation of File Upload with Progress Tracking

```javascript
const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
const formData = new FormData();
formData.append('resume', fileInput.files[0]);
formData.append('rollNo', 'S2024CS042');

xhr.open('POST', '/upload', true);

xhr.upload.onprogress = function(e) {
    if (e.lengthComputable) {
        const pct = (e.loaded / e.total) * 100;
        progressBar.value = pct;
        progressLabel.textContent = pct.toFixed(1) + '%';
    }
};

xhr.upload.onload = function() {
    console.log("Upload complete — server is now processing.");
};

xhr.onload = function() {
    if (xhr.status === 200) console.log("Server response:", xhr.responseText);
};

xhr.send(formData);
```

**Reasoning chain:**

- `FormData` automatically sets the correct `Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=...` header — we must **not** set it manually.
- `xhr.upload` is a sub-object exposing upload-side events. `onprogress` fires repeatedly as bytes travel.
- `e.loaded` = bytes sent so far, `e.total` = total bytes to send, `e.lengthComputable` = `true` if the server sent `Content-Length`.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right HTTP Method

| Intended Operation | HTTP Method | Idempotent? | Safe? | Body? | Example Endpoint |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Read data | **GET** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | `GET /students` |
| Create new resource | **POST** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `POST /students` |
| Replace existing | **PUT** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `PUT /students/42` |
| Partial update | **PATCH** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `PATCH /students/42` |
| Delete | **DELETE** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | `DELETE /students/42` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box — "Idempotent":** A request is **idempotent** if performing it multiple times produces the same result as performing it once. `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE` are idempotent; `POST` and `PATCH` are not.

## 3.5 Derivation — The "Abort Stale Request" Pattern (Search Autocomplete)

```javascript
let activeXhr = null;

function searchSuggestions(query) {
    if (activeXhr) activeXhr.abort();      // cancel previous in-flight request
    
    activeXhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
    activeXhr.open('GET', '/search?q=' + encodeURIComponent(query), true);
    
    activeXhr.onload = function() {
        if (activeXhr.status === 200) {
            renderSuggestions(JSON.parse(activeXhr.responseText));
        }
    };
    
    activeXhr.send();
}
```

**Why this matters:** Without `abort()`, typing "Kerala" might fire 6 requests (K, Ke, Ker, ...) and the **slowest** could overwrite the **fastest**, displaying stale results. Aborting stale requests guarantees only the latest result is shown.

## 3.6 The `onload` vs. `onreadystatechange` Decision Matrix

| Aspect | `onreadystatechange` | `onload` |
|---|---|---|
| Fires on | **Every** state change (0→1→2→3→4) | Only when `readyState = 4` |
| Need to check `readyState`? | Yes | No |
| Need to check `status`? | Yes (status may be 0 during cross-origin redirects) | Yes (status may still be 4xx/5xx) |
| Verbosity | More code | Cleaner code |
| Browser support | Universal | IE10+, all modern |
| KTU exam preference | Older style, often seen in textbook examples | Modern best practice |

## 3.7 The `responseType` Decision Matrix

| `responseType` Value | What `xhr.response` Contains | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| `''` or `'text'` | String | HTML fragments, plain text |
| `'json'` | Parsed JS object | REST APIs returning JSON |
| `'document'` | XML/HTML `Document` | RSS feeds, SVG manipulation |
| `'blob'` | `Blob` (binary) | Images, PDFs, audio downloads |
| `'arraybuffer'` | Fixed-length binary buffer | Low-level binary processing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If `responseType = 'json'` is set, `xhr.response` is already parsed — **do not** call `JSON.parse()` again. This is a common error.

## 3.8 CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) and `withCredentials`

When the AJAX endpoint is on a **different domain** than the page, the browser enforces **CORS**. Three pillars govern the policy:

1. **Server must send** `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: <origin>` (or `*`).
2. **Browser sends a preflight** `OPTIONS` request for non-simple requests (custom headers, `PUT`/`DELETE`, JSON body).
3. **Credentials** (cookies, HTTP auth) require `xhr.withCredentials = true` **AND** the server must explicitly allow the origin (no wildcard `*`).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students set `withCredentials = true` but forget that the server **must** echo the exact origin (not `*`) in `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`. The browser will silently drop the response otherwise.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The AJAX Request–Response Lifecycle (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User Browser
    participant J as JavaScript Engine
    participant X as XHR Object
    participant N as Network Layer
    participant S as Web Server
    participant DB as Database

    U->>J: Triggers Event (click, timer)
    J->>X: new XMLHttpRequest()
    Note over X: readyState = 0 (UNSENT)
    J->>X: xhr.open(method, url, true)
    Note over X: readyState = 1 (OPENED)
    J->>X: xhr.setRequestHeader(...)
    J->>X: xhr.send(body)
    Note over X: readyState = 2 (HEADERS_RECEIVED)
    X->>N: Transmit HTTP request
    N->>S: TCP/HTTPS handshake + data
    S->>DB: Query / process
    DB-->>S: Result set
    S-->>N: HTTP response (status, headers, body)
    N-->>X: Bytes arrive
    Note over X: readyState = 3 (LOADING)
    X->>J: onreadystatechange fires
    Note over X: readyState = 4 (DONE)
    X->>J: onload / onreadystatechange
    J->>J: Validate xhr.status === 200
    J->>J: Parse xhr.responseText (JSON.parse)
    J->>U: Update the DOM
    U-->>U: User sees fresh data (no reload)
```

## 4.2 The `readyState` State Machine (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UNSENT : new XMLHttpRequest()
    UNSENT --> OPENED : xhr.open()
    OPENED --> HEADERS_RECEIVED : xhr.send()
    HEADERS_RECEIVED --> LOADING : Response body streaming
    LOADING --> DONE : Transfer complete
    DONE --> [*]
    UNSENT --> DONE : xhr.abort()
    OPENED --> DONE : xhr.abort()
    HEADERS_RECEIVED --> DONE : xhr.abort()
    LOADING --> DONE : xhr.abort()
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete AJAX Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Interface Layer] --> B[Event Listener / Trigger]
    B --> C[Validation and Data Sanitization]
    C --> D{Abort Previous Request?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Call xhr.abort on Stale XHR]
    D -- No --> F[Instantiate New XMLHttpRequest]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Configure: xhr.open method, url, async true]
    G --> H[Set Headers: Content-Type, Auth Token, CSRF]
    H --> I[Register Event Handlers: onload, onerror, onprogress, ontimeout]
    I --> J[Dispatch: xhr.send body]
    J --> K{Server Response}
    K -- Status 2xx --> L[Parse xhr.response or responseText]
    K -- Status 4xx --> M[Client Error Handler: Show Validation Message]
    K -- Status 5xx --> N[Server Error Handler: Retry or Alert]
    K -- Network Failure --> O[onerror Handler: Offline Notification]
    L --> P[Update DOM / Re-render Component]
    P --> Q[User Sees Updated Interface]
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XHR[XMLHttpRequest Object]
    XHR --> H1[onreadystatechange]
    XHR --> H2[onload]

    XHR --> H5[ontimeout]
```
