# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the `XMLHttpRequest` object in AJAX. List any four properties of the XHR object.**

**Model Answer:**

The **`XMLHttpRequest` (XHR) object** is a built-in browser API that enables JavaScript to send HTTP/HTTPS requests to a server **asynchronously** and process the response without requiring a full page reload. It is the foundational mechanism behind AJAX, providing developers with complete programmatic control over the request–response lifecycle.

Four key properties of the XHR object:

1. **`readyState`** — A numeric value (0 to 4) indicating the current state of the request (e.g., `4` = DONE).
2. **`status`** — The HTTP status code returned by the server (e.g., `200` for success, `404` for not found).
3. **`responseText`** — The response body as a raw string (often JSON or HTML).
4. **`responseType`** — Declares the expected type of the response (`text`, `json`, `blob`, `document`, `arraybuffer`).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous AJAX requests. When is each appropriate?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Synchronous (`async = false`) | Asynchronous (`async = true`) |
|---|---|---|
| **UI behavior** | Freezes the entire browser tab until the response arrives | UI remains responsive; user can interact freely |
| **Code location** | `xhr.send()` blocks the next line | `xhr.send()` returns immediately; response handled in callback |
| **Use case** | Legacy systems, `Worker` threads, Node.js-style blocking I/O | Almost all modern web applications (default) |
| **Browser support** | Deprecated in the main thread; throws `InvalidAccessError` warnings | Universally supported, recommended |
| **Risk** | Poor user experience, frozen UI, missed clicks | Slightly more complex callback logic |

**Appropriate use:** Use **asynchronous** for all browser-based user-facing applications. Use **synchronous** only in `Web Worker` contexts where blocking one worker thread does not affect the UI.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete lifecycle of an AJAX request** using the `XMLHttpRequest` object, including all `readyState` values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete JavaScript program that uses XHR to **send a POST request with a JSON body** to the endpoint `/api/enroll`, handles both success and error scenarios, and updates the DOM with the server's response. The request must include a Bearer token in the `Authorization` header and must time out after 4 seconds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The complete lifecycle comprises **five phases**, each corresponding to a `readyState` value:

| Phase | `readyState` | State Name | What Happens | Developer Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **0** | `UNSENT` | Object created via `new XMLHttpRequest()` | Configure the request |
| 2 | **1** | `OPENED` | `xhr.open(method, url, true)` called | Set headers, prepare body |
| 3 | **2** | `HEADERS_RECEIVED` | `xhr.send()` called; response headers received | Inspect `getAllResponseHeaders()` |
| 4 | **3** | `LOADING` | Response body is being downloaded in chunks | Stream processing (rare) |
| 5 | **4** | `DONE` | Operation complete (success or failure) | Validate `status`, parse `responseText`, update DOM |

**Diagrammatic representation (also produced in SECTION_4.2):**

```
UNSENT → OPENED → HEADERS_RECEIVED → LOADING → DONE
   (0)     (1)         (2)              (3)     (4)
            ↓ (any state) → DONE (via xhr.abort())
```

The `onreadystatechange` event fires on **every** transition. At `readyState = 4`, the developer must verify `xhr.status === 200` to confirm a successful HTTP response — `readyState = 4` alone is insufficient.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head><title>AJAX POST Demo</title></head>
<body>
    <h2>Course Enrollment</h2>
    <button id="enrollBtn">Enroll Now</button>
    <p id="output"></p>

    <script>
        document.getElementById('enrollBtn').addEventListener('click', enrollStudent);

        function enrollStudent() {
            const xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
            const token = 'eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.demo.jwt';
            
            xhr.open('POST', '/api/enroll', true);
            
            // Headers — must be set AFTER open() and BEFORE send()
            xhr.setRequestHeader('Content-Type', 'application/json; charset=UTF-8');
            xhr.setRequestHeader('Authorization', 'Bearer ' + token);
            
            // Timeout configuration
            xhr.timeout = 4000;  // 4 seconds
            
            xhr.ontimeout = function() {
                document.getElementById('output').textContent = 
                    'Request timed out. Please try again.';
            };
            
            xhr.onerror = function() {
                document.getElementById('output').textContent = 
                    'Network error. Check your connection.';
            };
            
            xhr.onload = function() {
                if (xhr.status >= 200 && xhr.status < 300) {
                    const result = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText);
                    document.getElementById('output').textContent = 
                        'Success! Enrolled in ' + result.courseName;
                } else if (xhr.status === 401) {
                    document.getElementById('output').textContent = 
                        'Unauthorized. Please log in again.';
                } else {
                    document.getElementById('output').textContent = 
                        'Server error: ' + xhr.status + ' ' + xhr.statusText;
                }
            };
            
            const payload = JSON.stringify({
                studentId: 'S2024CS042',
                courseId: 'PECST742',
                semester: 7
            });
            
            xhr.send(payload);
        }
    </script>
</body>
</html>
```

**Explanation of key lines:**

- `xhr.open('POST', '/api/enroll', true)` — initializes an asynchronous POST request.
- `setRequestHeader('Content-Type', 'application/json; ...)` — **mandatory** for the server to parse the body as JSON.
- `setRequestHeader('Authorization', ...)` — sends the JWT bearer token for authentication.
- `xhr.timeout = 4000` — the request will be auto-aborted after 4 seconds.
- `onload` — modern event that fires when `readyState = 4`; we still check `status` for HTTP-level errors.
- `JSON.stringify(...)` — converts the JS object to a string before sending.
- `xhr.send(payload)` — dispatches the request with the JSON string as the body.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** List and explain any **six important properties** of the `XMLHttpRequest` object. **(6 marks)**

**(b)** Develop a complete JavaScript program that uses XHR to **upload a file with a progress bar**. The progress bar must update in real time as the file uploads. Also implement an **abort button** that cancels the upload. **(8 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Six important properties of the `XMLHttpRequest` object:

1. **`readyState`** *(Number, Read-only)* — Returns the current state of the request as an integer from `0` (UNSENT) to `4` (DONE). Developers poll this property inside the `onreadystatechange` handler to determine when the response is complete.

2. **`status`** *(Number, Read-only)* — Returns the HTTP status code from the server, such as `200` (OK), `404` (Not Found), or `500` (Server Error). It is only meaningful once `readyState === 4`.

3. **`statusText`** *(String, Read-only)* — Returns the textual status message accompanying the numeric code, e.g., `"OK"` for `200` or `"Not Found"` for `404`.

4. **`responseText`** *(String, Read-only)* — Returns the response body as a raw string. If the server sends JSON, the developer must call `JSON.parse(responseText)` to convert it to a JavaScript object.

5. **`responseType`** *(String, Read/Write)* — Declares the expected type of the response. Setting it to `'json'` makes `xhr.response` return a parsed object instead of a string. Other values include `'text'`, `'blob'`, `'arraybuffer'`, and `'document'`.

6. **`timeout`** *(Number, Read/Write)* — Specifies the maximum time (in milliseconds) the request is allowed to run before being auto-aborted. If exceeded, the `ontimeout` event handler fires.

7. **`withCredentials`** *(Boolean, Read/Write)* — When set to `true`, the request will include cookies and HTTP authentication headers even for cross-origin requests, subject to server CORS policy.

8. **`upload`** *(Object, Read-only)* — Returns an `XMLHttpRequestUpload` object that exposes progress events for the upload phase (`onprogress`, `onload`, `onerror`, `onabort`).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>File Upload with Progress</title>
    <style>
        #progressBar { width: 400px; height: 20px; }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <h2>Upload Resume</h2>
    <input type="file" id="fileInput"><br><br>
    <progress id="progressBar" value="0" max="100"></progress>
    <span id="progressLabel">0%</span>
    <p id="status"></p>
    <button id="uploadBtn">Upload</button>
    <button id="abortBtn">Cancel</button>

    <script>
        let currentXhr = null;

        document.getElementById('uploadBtn').addEventListener('click', startUpload);
        document.getElementById('abortBtn').addEventListener('click', cancelUpload);

        function startUpload() {
            const file = document.getElementById('fileInput').files[0];
            if (!file) {
                document.getElementById('status').textContent = 'Please select a file first.';
                return;
            }

            const formData = new FormData();
            formData.append('resume', file);
            formData.append('rollNo', 'S2024CS042');

            currentXhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
            currentXhr.open('POST', '/upload', true);

            // Progress tracking
            currentXhr.upload.onprogress = function(e) {
                if (e.lengthComputable) {
                    const pct = (e.loaded / e.total) * 100;
                    document.getElementById('progressBar').value = pct;
                    document.getElementById('progressLabel').textContent = pct.toFixed(1) + '%';
                }
            };

            currentXhr.upload.onload = function() {
                document.getElementById('status').textContent = 
                    'Upload complete — server processing now.';
            };

            currentXhr.onload = function() {
                if (currentXhr.status === 200) {
                    document.getElementById('status').textContent = 
                        'Server response: ' + currentXhr.responseText;
                } else {
                    document.getElementById('status').textContent = 
                        'Server returned error: ' + currentXhr.status;
                }
            };

            currentXhr.onerror = function() {
                document.getElementById('status').textContent = 'Upload failed (network error).';
            };

            currentXhr.onabort = function() {
                document.getElementById('status').textContent = 'Upload cancelled by user.';
                document.getElementById('progressBar').value = 0;
                document.getElementById('progressLabel').textContent = '0%';
            };

            currentXhr.send(formData);
            document.getElementById('status').textContent = 'Uploading...';
        }

        function cancelUpload() {
            if (currentXhr) {
                currentXhr.abort();
                currentXhr = null;
            }
        }
    </script>
</body>
</html>
```

**Explanation of key lines:**

- `FormData` is used because it automatically constructs the correct `multipart/form-data` body and headers — we do **not** call `setRequestHeader` for the content type.
- `xhr.upload.onprogress` provides real-time byte-level feedback via `e.loaded` and `e.total`.
- `e.lengthComputable` is `false` when the server does not send `Content-Length`; the check prevents division-by-zero.
- `currentXhr.abort()` cancels the request, triggers the `onabort` handler, and resets the UI.
- Storing the XHR object in a module-level variable (`currentXhr`) makes it reachable from the cancel button.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check `xhr.status === 200` after `readyState === 4`** — losing **2 marks** in nearly every question.
> 2. **Setting `Content-Type` manually when using `FormData`** — the browser sets it automatically with the correct boundary; setting it manually *breaks* the upload. **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing `responseText` (always a string) with `response` (respects `responseType`)** — a common error in JSON-handling questions.
> 4. **Using `async = false`** in a UI context — explicitly disallowed in modern XHR; KTU will mark this down.
> 5. **Not preventing default form submission** when XHR is wired to a form's submit button — the page will reload, defeating the purpose of AJAX.
> 6. **Forgetting `encodeURIComponent()` on query-string parameters** — leads to malformed URLs and silent server errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary for last-minute revision:**

- ✅ **AJAX = Asynchronous JavaScript and XML** — though modern apps mostly use **JSON**, the name persists.
- ✅ The **core engine** is the `XMLHttpRequest` object, instantiated via `new XMLHttpRequest()`.
- ✅ The **two key methods** are `xhr.open(method, url, async)` and `xhr.send(body)`.
- ✅ `open()` *configures* the request; `send()` *dispatches* it — **no network activity occurs until `send()` is called**.
- ✅ The **five ready states** are `UNSENT (0)`, `OPENED (1)`, `HEADERS_RECEIVED (2)`, `LOADING (3)`, `DONE (4)`.
- ✅ **Always check both** `readyState === 4` **AND** `status === 200` for true success.
- ✅ Use `onreadystatechange` (legacy) or `onload` (modern, cleaner) for the success callback.
- ✅ Use `onerror` for network failures, `ontimeout` for slow responses, `onabort` for cancelled requests.
- ✅ For `POST`/`PUT` with JSON, you **must** set `Content-Type: application/json` and call `JSON.stringify()` on the payload.
- ✅ For file uploads, use `FormData` — **do not** manually set `Content-Type`.
- ✅ Use `xhr.upload.onprogress` for upload progress bars; use `xhr.onprogress` for download progress.
- ✅ `xhr.abort()` cancels in-flight requests — essential for autocomplete and "Cancel" buttons.
- ✅ `responseType = 'json'` makes `xhr.response` a parsed object — **do not double-parse** with `JSON.parse`.
- ✅ `xhr.timeout` (in ms) + `xhr.ontimeout` provides a hard ceiling on request duration.
- ✅ For CORS, the server must send `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`; `withCredentials = true` requires the server to echo the **exact origin** (no `*`).
- ✅ HTTP status ranges: **2xx = success**, **4xx = client error**, **5xx = server error**.
- ✅ **Idempotent verbs** (`GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) can be retried safely; **non-idempotent** (`POST`, `PATCH`) cannot.
- ✅ The XHR object exposes both **request-side events** (`onload`, `onerror`) and **upload-side events** (`xhr.upload.onprogress`).
- ✅ Modern alternative: the `fetch()` API (Promise-based) — but the KTU 2024 syllabus still emphasizes XHR for foundational understanding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Configure with `open`, customize with `setRequestHeader`, dispatch with `send`, and react in `onreadystatechange` or `onload` — but never forget the `status` check!"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, JavaScript Library: jQuery, jQuery Foundations, Including jQuery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, JavaScript Library: jQuery, jQuery Foundations, Including jQuery # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) ### Formal Definition **Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)** is a browser security mechanism, standardized by the **W3C**, that relaxes the **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** by allowing web pages served from one **Origin** to request resources (data, fonts, scripts, styles, images) from a *different* Origin, provided the target server explicitly permits it via specific **HTTP response headers**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Origin (per RFC 6454)** = `Scheme` + `Host` + `Port`. Two URLs share the same origin **only** if all three match. For example, `https://ktu.ac.in:443/page1` and `https://ktu.ac.in:443/page2` are same-origin, but `http://ktu.ac.in/page1` differs in **scheme**. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Embassy Visa Counter" Imagine a country **Kerala** (your origin) and a country **Tamil Nadu** (the API server). By default, the **Same-Origin Policy** is like a strict border — no foreigner can just walk in. **CORS** is the *visa counter*: if the foreign server (Tamil Nadu) stamps your passport with the right **Access-Control-Allow-Origin** header (visa), then the browser (immigration officer) allows the response to enter your page. If the server doesn't give permission, the browser *blocks* the response, even though the request was physically delivered. ### The Same-Origin Policy (SOP) — The Default Lock SOP is a *defense-in-depth* security feature in browsers that **prevents a document/script loaded from one origin** from reading or interacting with resources from another origin. Without SOP, a malicious script on `evil.com` could freely read your bank data on `bank.com` while you are logged in. CORS is the **controlled, opt-in relaxation** of this policy. > [!NOTE] > **Key Metric:** Browsers enforce SOP at the *client side*. CORS does **not** make a server "more secure" — it only tells the *browser* it is safe to expose the response to the calling page. ### Why CORS is needed - **AJAX / Fetch / XMLHttpRequest** calls are blocked by SOP by default across origins. - **Web Fonts (WOFF2)**, **Canvas textures**, and **WebGL textures** loaded cross-origin need CORS to be usable. - **Third-party APIs** (e.g., OpenWeather, GitHub) must explicitly opt-in via CORS to be consumed from any web page. --- ## 1.2 JavaScript Library: jQuery ### Formal Definition **jQuery** is a fast, lightweight, feature-rich **JavaScript library** (released in **August 2006** by **John Resig**) that simplifies client-side scripting of HTML, handles DOM manipulation, event handling, animation, and AJAX interactions using a single, consistent API that works uniformly across all major browsers. > [!NOTE] > jQuery's slogan: *"Write less, do more."* It abstracts away browser-specific quirks (especially the pre-HTML5 era of IE6/IE7/IE8) and exposes a unified interface. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Remote" Think of plain JavaScript as having to manually wire an electronic circuit for every TV brand. jQuery is the **universal remote** — one set of buttons (`$()`, `.click()`, `.fadeIn()`) operates every TV (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) consistently. You press *one* button, and jQuery figures out which underlying native API to call. ### Core Capabilities of jQuery 1. **DOM Selection & Traversal** — CSS-selector based element finding. 2. **DOM Manipulation** — Content, attributes, CSS, classes. 3. **Event Handling** — Unified `on()`/`click()`/`bind()`. 4. **Effects & Animations** — Built-in fade, slide, custom animate. 5. **AJAX Abstraction** — `$.ajax()`, `$.get()`, `$.post()`, `$.getJSON()`. 6. **Utility Functions** — `$.each()`, `$.extend()`, `$.grep()`, `$.trim()`. --- ## 1.3 jQuery Foundations ### Formal Definition **jQuery Foundations** refer to the *fundamental building blocks* of jQuery programming: the **`$` alias**, the **jQuery factory function**, **selector engine (Sizzle)**, **method chaining**, **ready event**, **implicit iteration**, and the **no-conflict mode** — the conceptual bedrock on which all jQuery code is written. > [!IMPORTANT] > **`window.jQuery === window.$`** is `true` by default. `$` is merely a *shortcut* alias to the global `jQuery` function object. This is why `$` may be reassigned via `jQuery.noConflict()`. ### The `$()` Factory Function `$(selector)` is the **entry point** to almost every jQuery operation. It accepts: - A **CSS selector string** → `$( "div.item" )` - An **HTML string** → `$( "

Hello

" )` - A **DOM element** → `$( document.body )` - A **function** → `$( function() { ... } )` ← shorthand for `$( document ).ready()` ### jQuery Object vs DOM Element `$("p")` returns a **jQuery wrapper object** (array-like collection) — **not** the raw DOM element. You must call methods on the wrapper: `$("p").text(...)`. To get the raw DOM element: `$("p")[0]` or `$("p").get(0)`. --- ## 1.4 Including jQuery ### Formal Definition **Including jQuery** is the act of loading the jQuery library file (`jquery.min.js` or `jquery.js`) into an HTML page so that its functions become available to the page's JavaScript. This can be done via a **CDN (Content Delivery Network) reference** or by **downloading the file locally** and referencing it with a relative path inside a `` | Production sites with internet; cache sharing | | **Local Download** | `` | Offline dev, intranet, strict firewalls | > [!NOTE] > **Recommended placement:** Just before the closing `` tag, OR in `` with the `defer` attribute. The `defer` attribute guarantees the script executes **after** the HTML is fully parsed, avoiding "element not found" errors. ### Intuition — "The Pre-installed App" Using a CDN is like using an app **already installed on every visitor's phone** (via browser HTTP cache from a prior visit to *any* site using the same CDN). A local file is like bringing your own copy — works without internet, but bigger initial download for first-time users. --- ## 1.5 Quick Synoptic View > [!IMPORTANT] > **SOP** blocks cross-origin reads. **CORS** is the *opt-in relaxation* using HTTP headers. **jQuery** is the *simplification layer* over vanilla JavaScript. Its **foundations** are the `$` function, selectors, and chaining. **Inclusion** is via CDN or local `` | | 2 | **CDN** — jQuery official | `` | | 3 | **CDN** — cdnjs | `` | | 4 | **Local download** | Download `jquery-3.7.1.min.js` from jquery.com → save in `js/` folder | | 5 | **Local include** | `` | | 6 | **NPM (modern)** | `npm install jquery` then `import $ from 'jquery';` | | 7 | **Verify load** | `console.log(typeof jQuery);` → expect `"function"` | | 8 | **Fallback** | `` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **jQuery 3.7.1** is the current stable line (slim build: `jquery-3.7.1.slim.min.js` excludes `ajax`/`effects` modules). Always include **only one** jQuery on a page to avoid version conflicts. ### The `.min` vs Unminified Trade-off - `.min.js` → whitespace/stripped; **production**. - `.js` (unminified) → readable; **development/debugging** only. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Derivations ## 3.1 CORS — A Complete Request-Response Walkthrough ### Walkthrough 1: Blocking (SOP kicks in, no CORS) > A page at `https://app.ktu.in` tries to fetch from `https://api.ktu.in/users`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Browser evaluates: are origins different?} \\ & \text{Origin}_{\text{page}} = \texttt{https://app.ktu.in} \quad (443,\ \text{scheme https},\ \text{host app.ktu.in}) \\ & \text{Origin}_{\text{API}} = \texttt{https://api.ktu.in} \quad (443,\ \text{scheme https},\ \text{host api.ktu.in}) \\ & \text{Hosts differ} \implies \text{Cross-Origin.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Request is "not simple" because it uses \texttt{Authorization} header.} \\ & \text{Browser issues preflight \texttt{OPTIONS} request.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Server responds with no \texttt{Access-Control-Allow-Origin} header.} \\ & \text{Browser aborts the actual \texttt{GET} request before sending it.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Console shows:} \\ & \texttt{Access to fetch at 'https://api.ktu.in/users' from origin} \\ & \texttt{'https://app.ktu.in' has been blocked by CORS policy:} \\ & \texttt{No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present...} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Walkthrough 2: Successful CORS (simple GET, `*`) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Cross-origin GET to } \texttt{https://api.ktu.in/ping} \\ & \text{No custom headers, method is GET} \implies \text{Simple request.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Browser sends request with \texttt{Origin: https://app.ktu.in}} \\ & \text{Server replies with \texttt{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Browser compares Origin header to allow-list.} \\ & \texttt{*} \text{ matches any origin (but excludes credentials mode).} \\ & \text{Response is released to JavaScript.} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Walkthrough 3: Preflight with custom header (full derivation) > Page at `https://app.ktu.in` PUTs JSON with header `X-API-Key` to `https://api.ktu.in/tasks`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Browser checks "simple" criteria.} \\ & \text{Method = PUT} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Not in \{GET, HEAD, POST\}} \\ & \text{Content-Type = application/json} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Not in allowed simple types} \\ & \text{Custom header X-API-Key} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Not in allowed simple headers} \\ & \text{Conclusion: NOT simple. Preflight is required.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2: Browser sends preflight.} \\ & \texttt{OPTIONS /tasks HTTP/1.1} \\ & \texttt{Host: api.ktu.in} \\ & \texttt{Origin: https://app.ktu.in} \\ & \texttt{Access-Control-Request-Method: PUT} \\ & \texttt{Access-Control-Request-Headers: content-type, x-api-key} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 3: Server grants permission (or refuses).} \\ & \text{If granted, it returns e.g. \texttt{HTTP/1.1 204}} \\ & \texttt{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.ktu.in} \\ & \texttt{Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE} \\ & \texttt{Access-Control-Allow-Headers: content-type, x-api-key} \\ & \texttt{Access-Control-Max-Age: 3600} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 4: Browser caches the preflight for 3600 s, then sends real request.} \\ & \texttt{PUT /tasks HTTP/1.1} \\ & \texttt{Origin: https://app.ktu.in} \\ & \texttt{Content-Type: application/json} \\ & \texttt{X-API-Key: abc123} \\ & \texttt{\{"title":"Submit notes"\} } \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 5: Server replies to real request, again echoing CORS headers.} \\ & \texttt{HTTP/1.1 201 Created} \\ & \texttt{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.ktu.in} \\ & \texttt{Content-Type: application/json} \\ & \texttt{\{"id": 7, "status":"created"\} } \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **CORS errors never happen on the server.** The server *receives* and *processes* the request; the browser *hides the response* from JavaScript if CORS headers are missing/wrong. --- ## 3.2 CORS — Node.js (Express) Server-Side Snippet ```js const express = require("express"); const app = express(); // CORS middleware app.use((req, res, next) => { res.header("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "https://app.ktu.in"); res.header("Access-Control-Allow-Methods", "GET,POST,PUT,DELETE,OPTIONS"); res.header("Access-Control-Allow-Headers", "Content-Type, X-API-Key"); res.header("Access-Control-Allow-Credentials", "true"); if (req.method === "OPTIONS") { return res.sendStatus(204); // short-circuit preflight } next(); }); app.get("/users", (req, res) => res.json([{id:1, name:"Krishna"}])); app.listen(3000); ``` --- ## 3.3 jQuery — A Foundations Walkthrough ### Walkthrough 4: Selectors and Method Chaining (full derivation) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{HTML contains: } \\ & \texttt{
  • Apple
  • Banana
  • Cherry
} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{JS selects all
  • elements and logs their count.} \\ & \texttt{var $items = \$("li");} \\ & \texttt{console.log(\$items.length);} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Chain three operations: highlight odd items, fade in.} \\ & \texttt{\$items.filter(":odd").css("background","yellow").fadeIn(400);} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Index 1 (Banana) — the second
  • in zero-based indexing — turns yellow.} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Walkthrough 5: Event Handling — `.on()` vs `.click()` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{\$("button").click(function()\{ ... \})} \\ & \text{Equivalent shorthand: } \texttt{\$("button").on("click", function()\{ ... \})} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Delegated event (works for dynamically added elements):} \\ & \texttt{\$(document).on("click", ".btn", function()\{ alert("Clicked!"); \});} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > In jQuery 3.x, `.click()`, `.bind()`, `.delegate()` are still functional but **deprecated for new code**; prefer `.on()` and `.off()`. --- ### Walkthrough 6: AJAX via jQuery (full derivation) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{\$.ajax(\{ url: "/api/users", method: "GET", dataType: "json" \})} \\ & \text{.done( function(data) \{ console.log(data); \})} \\ & \text{.fail( function(xhr, status, err) \{ console.error(err); \})} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Promise-style chaining with .then():} \\ & \texttt{\$.getJSON("/api/users").then(users => render(users));} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Including jQuery — The Exact Sequence $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Decide CDN vs Local.} \\ & \text{CDN benefits: cache, geographic speed, no bandwidth on your server.} \\ & \text{Local benefits: works offline, no third-party dependency.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Place } \\ & \texttt{ } \\ & \texttt{} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Verify in browser DevTools console: \texttt{typeof jQuery === "function"} \Rightarrow \text{true}.} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Implement a CDN-fallback (defensive loading):} \\ & \texttt{} \\ & \texttt{} \end{aligned} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CORS — End-to-End Request Flow (Simple vs Preflight) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Page at https://app.ktu.in] --> B{Cross-origin?} B -- No --> C[Use response normally] B -- Yes --> D{Simple request?} D -- Yes --> E[Send real request with Origin header] D -- No --> F[Send OPTIONS preflight] F --> G{Server allows?} G -- No --> H[Browser blocks - CORS error in console] G -- Yes --> I[Cache preflight per Access-Control-Max-Age] I --> J[Send real request] E --> K{Response has Access-Control-Allow-Origin matching?} J --> K K -- No --> H K -- Yes --> L[Release response to JavaScript] ``` ## 4.2 CORS — Header Direction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ClientToServer[Request Headers - Browser to Server] H1[Origin] H2[Access-Control-Request-Method - preflight only] H3[Access-Control-Request-Headers - preflight only] end subgraph ServerToClient[Response Headers - Server to Browser] R1[Access-Control-Allow-Origin] R2[Access-Control-Allow-Methods] R3[Access-Control-Allow-Headers] R4[Access-Control-Allow-Credentials] R5[Access-Control-Expose-Headers] R6[Access-Control-Max-Age] end ClientToServer --> ServerToClient ``` ## 4.3 jQuery Object Lifecycle & Method Chaining ```mermaid flowchart LR A[HTML Document] --> B[$ selector] B --> C[jQuery Object - wrapped set of DOM nodes] C --> D[.css method] D --> E[jQuery Object returned] E --> F[.fadeIn method] F --> G[jQuery Object returned] G --> H[.on method - bind event] H --> I[Side effect on DOM] ``` ## 4.4 jQuery Selector Engine Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[$ selector string] --> B{Browser supports querySelectorAll?} B -- Yes --> C[Native querySelectorAll - fast path] B -- No --> D[Sizzle selector engine - polyfill] C --> E[Array-like jQuery object] D --> E E --> F[Implicit iteration on .method calls] F --> G[DOM mutations / events / animations] ``` ## 4.5 Including jQuery — Loading Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose jQuery Loading Method] --> B{Need offline support?} B -- Yes --> C[Download jquery-3.7.1.min.js] C --> D[Reference as script src equals js/jquery-3.7.1.min.js] B -- No --> E{Cache reuse priority?} E -- Yes --> F[Use major CDN: code.jquery.com or cdnjs] E -- No --> G[Use local copy in js folder] F --> H[Add CDN-fallback with window.jQuery check] G --> H H --> I[Verify typeof jQuery equals function] ``` ## 4.6 jQuery Effects Pipeline (queue-based animation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[.animate opacity 0 to 1, 400ms] --> Q[fx queue] A2[.animate height 0 to 200, 400ms] --> Q A3[.delay 200] --> Q A4[.slideUp 400] --> Q Q --> DOM[DOM updates frame by frame] DOM --> Done[.promise or callback fires] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS). Explain the role of the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header in a CORS response.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)** is a **W3C-standardized browser security mechanism** that relaxes the **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** by allowing a web page served from one **Origin** to make controlled, asynchronous requests to a *different* origin, provided that the target server explicitly grants permission through specific HTTP headers in its response. > > The header **`Access-Control-Allow-Origin`** is the *single most important* response header in CORS. It tells the browser which origin(s) are allowed to read the response body in JavaScript. Its value can be: > - A **specific origin** — e.g. `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.ktu.in` (most secure, supports credentials). > - A **wildcard** — `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` (allows any origin, but **forbids** the use of cookies/auth unless `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` is also set, in which case the origin must be explicit). > > If this header is **absent** or **does not match** the requesting origin, the browser blocks JavaScript from reading the response and throws a CORS error in the console — even though the request was successfully delivered to the server. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any four advantages of using jQuery. Write the syntax to include jQuery in an HTML page using a CDN.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Four advantages of jQuery (any four, 2 marks):** > 1. **Cross-browser compatibility** — One unified API works across Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari, and older IE versions. > 2. **Concise DOM manipulation** — Tasks that take many lines of vanilla JS become single jQuery calls. > 3. **Powerful selectors** — CSS3 + jQuery-specific pseudo-selectors (`:has`, `:contains`, `:input`). > 4. **Built-in effects and animations** — `fadeIn`, `slideUp`, `animate` ready to use. > 5. **Simplified AJAX** — `$.ajax`, `$.get`, `$.post` with Promise support. > 6. **Rich plugin ecosystem** — Thousands of community plugins (sliders, validation, datepickers). > > **CDN inclusion syntax (1 mark):** > ```html > > ``` > Place this tag just before the closing `` tag (or in `` with `defer`). The file is fetched from the global CDN and the `jQuery` (and `$`) global becomes available. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the Same-Origin Policy (SOP) and its necessity. Describe with a diagram how CORS relaxes SOP. List and explain the major CORS request and response headers. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With a neat example, explain how a **preflight request** works in CORS. How does the browser distinguish between a *simple* request and a *preflight* request? **(7 marks)** #### (a) Model Solution (7 marks) **1. Same-Origin Policy (SOP) — Definition & Necessity (2 marks):** SOP is a **browser-enforced security policy** that restricts how a document or script loaded from one **Origin** can interact with resources from a *different* Origin. By **Origin**, the RFC 6454 definition applies: $$ \text{Origin} = \text{Scheme} + \text{Host} + \text{Port} $$ SOP is necessary to **prevent malicious scripts** on one site (e.g. `evil.com`) from reading or manipulating sensitive data on another site (e.g. `bank.com`) where the user is authenticated. It applies to DOM access, `XMLHttpRequest`/`fetch`, `Web Storage`, and `cookies`. **2. How CORS Relaxes SOP (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Page at Origin A] -->|XHR or fetch| B{Browser SOP check} B -->|Same origin| C[Direct access - allowed] B -->|Cross origin| D{Server returns CORS headers?} D -- No --> E[Block response - CORS error] D -- Yes --> F[Allow JS to read response] ``` CORS does **not disable** SOP; it provides an **opt-in, server-controlled exception**. The server is the *final authority*; if it sends the right headers, the browser permits the cross-origin read. **3. Major CORS Headers (3 marks):** *(List any 6 with one-line purpose; full table is in Section 2 above.)* - `Origin` (request) - `Access-Control-Request-Method` (request, preflight) - `Access-Control-Request-Headers` (request, preflight) - `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` (response) - `Access-Control-Allow-Methods` (response) - `Access-Control-Allow-Headers` (response) - `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` (response) - `Access-Control-Max-Age` (response) #### (b) Model Solution (7 marks) **1. Preflight Example (4 marks):** A page at `https://app.ktu.in` wants to PUT JSON with an `Authorization` header to `https://api.ktu.in/tasks`. ```http # Step 1 — Preflight (browser → server) OPTIONS /tasks HTTP/1.1 Host: api.ktu.in Origin: https://app.ktu.in Access-Control-Request-Method: PUT Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization, content-type ``` ```http # Step 2 — Server grants (or denies) HTTP/1.1 204 No Content Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.ktu.in Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT Access-Control-Allow-Headers: authorization, content-type Access-Control-Max-Age: 3600 ``` ```http # Step 3 — Actual request (browser → server) PUT /tasks HTTP/1.1 Host: api.ktu.in Origin: https://app.ktu.in Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGc... Content-Type: application/json {"title":"Submit notes"} ``` **2. Simple vs Preflight Distinction (3 marks):** A request is **simple** (no preflight) **only if all** the following are true: - Method ∈ { **GET, HEAD, POST** } - `Content-Type` ∈ { `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, `multipart/form-data`, `text/plain` } (or absent) - No custom request headers other than: `Accept`, `Accept-Language`, `Content-Language`, `Content-Type`, `Range` - No `ReadableStream` body, no `XMLHttpRequestUpload` listeners If **any** of these constraints is violated, the browser **must** send an `OPTIONS` preflight. Non-simple triggers include: `PUT`, `DELETE`, `PATCH` methods, `Content-Type: application/json`, custom headers like `X-API-Key`, and certain media types. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** What is jQuery? List and explain the various ways to include jQuery in a web page. Write a program to demonstrate jQuery selectors, events, and effects. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the jQuery foundations: the `$` factory function, implicit iteration, method chaining, and the `$(document).ready()` event. Write a jQuery AJAX example to fetch and display JSON data from a server. **(7 marks)** #### (a) Model Solution (7 marks) **1. jQuery Definition (1 mark):** jQuery is a fast, lightweight JavaScript library (created by John Resig in 2006) that simplifies HTML DOM manipulation, event handling, animation, and AJAX using a single consistent API across all browsers. **2. Ways to Include jQuery (3 marks):** | Method | Syntax | |---|---| | **CDN — jQuery official** | `` | | **CDN — Google** | `` | | **CDN — cdnjs** | `` | | **Local download** | Download from jquery.com → `` | | **NPM** | `npm install jquery` then `import $ from 'jquery';` | **3. Program demonstrating selectors, events, effects (3 marks):** ```html jQuery Demo

    KTU Subjects

    • Web Programming
    • Data Structures
    • Operating Systems
    ``` #### (b) Model Solution (7 marks) **1. `$` factory function (1.5 marks):** `$` is an alias for the global `jQuery` function. Calling `$(selector)` returns a *jQuery object* — a wrapped, array-like collection of DOM elements matching the CSS-selector. Example: `var $paras = $("p");` — `$paras` has methods like `.css()`, `.text()`, `.hide()` that operate on the entire set. **2. Implicit iteration (1 mark):** When a jQuery method is called on a collection, it **automatically applies to every element** in the collection. `$( "li" ).hide()` hides *all* `
  • `s without an explicit loop. **3. Method chaining (1.5 marks):** Almost every jQuery method returns the jQuery object, allowing chains: ```js $("#box") .css("background", "crimson") .slideUp(400) .delay(200) .slideDown(400) .text("Done!"); ``` **4. `$(document).ready()` event (1 mark):** `$(function(){ ... })` is shorthand for `$(document).ready(handler)`. It runs *after* the DOM is fully constructed but before images load — preventing "element not found" errors. **5. AJAX JSON example (2 marks):** ```js $(function () { $("#loadBtn").on("click", function () { $.ajax({ url: "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users", type: "GET", dataType: "json" }) .done(function (users) { var html = "
      "; $.each(users, function (i, u) { html += "
    • " + u.name + " — " + u.email + "
      • "; }); html += "
    "; $("#output").html(html).hide().fadeIn(600); }) .fail(function (xhr, status, err) { console.error("Failed:", err); }); }); }); ``` --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Confusing SOP with CORS:** Many answers state *"CORS is a security policy"* — wrong. **SOP is the policy**; **CORS is the relaxation mechanism.** > 2. **Wrong origin of the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` + credentials combination:** The wildcard `*` is **invalid** when `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` is set; the response *must* echo the exact origin. > 3. **Thinking CORS is enforced on the server:** CORS is enforced **only by the browser**. A non-browser client (Postman, cURL, server-to-server) ignores CORS headers entirely. > 4. **Forgetting the `defer` attribute / placing jQuery in `` without it:** Code runs before DOM is parsed, leading to `$("p")` returning 0 elements — but most students think jQuery is broken. > 5. **Confusing `$` with `jQuery`:** `$` is a **variable/alias**; `jQuery` is the function. `$ === jQuery` only when `noConflict()` has not been called. > 6. **Mixing jQuery versions:** Including two different jQuery versions on the same page silently breaks plugins — a frequently penalized mistake. > 7. **Confusing simple and preflight:** A `POST` with `Content-Type: application/json` is **NOT** simple — it always preflights. Students often claim it does not. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **SOP** = same `Scheme + Host + Port`; blocks cross-origin reads by default. - [x] **CORS** = opt-in, header-based, **browser-enforced** relaxation of SOP. - [x] **Simple request** = `GET`/`HEAD`/`POST` + restricted headers + simple `Content-Type`. - [x] **Preflight** = `OPTIONS` request; caches per `Access-Control-Max-Age`. - [x] **`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`** works **only without** credentials. - [x] **CORS does not protect the server**; it protects the **browser's user**. - [x] **jQuery** is a JS library, *not* a framework; written by John Resig (2006). - [x] **`$` is an alias** for `jQuery`; identical unless `noConflict()` is called. - [x] **`$(selector)`** returns a **jQuery object**, never a raw DOM element. - [x] **Implicit iteration** removes the need for `for` loops on jQuery collections. - [x] **Method chaining** works because jQuery methods return the jQuery object. - [x] **`$(function(){...})`** is the canonical "DOM ready" handler. - [x] **Include jQuery** via CDN (`code.jquery.com`) or local (`js/jquery-3.7.1.min.js`). - [x] Place the `
