> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** jQuery Selectors # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Web Programming (PECST742)*, **jQuery Selectors** are formally defined as **expressions that use a CSS-like syntax to identify, locate, and match a set of DOM (Document Object Model) elements** from the HTML document tree, so that jQuery methods can subsequently be applied to the matched set. The jQuery library exposes its entire selection engine through the global factory function: $$ \$(selector, context) $$ where: - **$** is the alias for the jQuery factory function (equivalent to `jQuery`). - **selector** is a string containing the CSS-style selection expression. - **context** is an optional DOM element, jQuery object, or document; it scopes the search. The function returns a **jQuery wrapped collection** (a pseudo-array) of all matching DOM nodes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST742 – Module 2):** > "jQuery – Selectors – Form Selectors – Document Object Model (DOM) manipulation with jQuery." > Selectors are the **entry point** to all jQuery operations: events, animations, AJAX, and DOM traversal begin with selecting elements. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine a **massive library** (the HTML document) containing thousands of **books on shelves** (the DOM elements). When you want to do something with a book — read it, highlight it, move it — you must first **locate it**. A **jQuery selector is like a smart librarian** who, the moment you state a rule (e.g., *"all red-covered novels on shelf 3"*), instantly walks over and **hands you a stack of exactly those books**, neatly bundled together in a "jQuery wrapper box". You don't have to walk through the aisles yourself. - *"Red-covered books"* → `$( ".novel.red" )` (class selector) - *"Book on shelf 3"* → `$( "#shelf3" )` (id selector) - *"All books"* → `$( "*" )` (universal selector) - *"All novels in the science section"* → `$( "#science .novel" )` (descendant selector) Just as the librarian understands both natural language rules and the library catalog, jQuery selectors understand **CSS rules, attribute filters, position filters, and form-specific rules** — all in a single, unified grammar. > [!NOTE] > A single jQuery selector often combines **multiple conditions** (tag + class + attribute + position) to pinpoint exactly the right element(s), exactly like a librarian combining multiple rules to find the precise book. ## 1.3 Why Selectors Matter in KTU Examinations In KTU's outcome-based assessment for PECST742, questions on selectors are testing **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Develop interactive web pages using client-side scripting languages."* You are expected to: 1. Write selectors that match a given HTML snippet. 2. Predict **which elements** a particular selector will match. 3. Apply jQuery methods to selected elements. 4. Differentiate between **CSS selectors** and **jQuery-specific pseudo-selectors** (`:eq()`, `:first`, `:has()`, etc.). # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The jQuery Selector Engine — How It Works The jQuery selector engine (internally **Sizzle**, prior to jQuery 3.0 — and later integrated) executes the selection in this sequence: 1. **Tokenization** — The selector string is split into atomic tokens (tag, id, class, attribute, pseudo). 2. **Combination** — Tokens are combined using combinators (space, `>`, `+`, `~`, comma). 3. **Right-to-Left Matching (Compiling)** — Sizzle compiles the rightmost selector first for performance. 4. **Filtering** — Each candidate DOM element is checked against ALL parts of the compound selector. 5. **Return** — A jQuery wrapper object containing the matched elements is returned. > [!IMPORTANT] > The returned object is **not** a native array. It is a **jQuery object** with a numeric index (0-based) and a `length` property. This is why the syntax `$("p")[0]` returns the raw DOM element, while `$("p").eq(0)` returns another jQuery object. ## 2.2 Master Classification of jQuery Selectors ### A. Basic CSS-Style Selectors | Category | Syntax | Description | KTU Weight | |---|---|---|---| | Element | `$("p")` | Selects all `

` elements | High | | ID | `$("#login")` | Selects the element with `id="login"` | High | | Class | `$(".btn")` | Selects all elements with `class="btn"` | High | | Universal | `$("*")` | Selects every element | Medium | | Compound | `$("p.lead")` | `

` tags that also have class `lead` | High | | Grouped (multiple) | `$("h1, h2, h3")` | All h1, h2, and h3 elements | High | ### B. Hierarchy / Combinator Selectors | Combinator | Syntax | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Descendant | `A B` | B anywhere inside A | `$("div p")` | | Child | `A > B` | B is a **direct** child of A | `$("ul > li")` | | Adjacent Sibling | `A + B` | B immediately follows A | `$("h2 + p")` | | General Sibling | `A ~ B` | B is a later sibling of A | `$("h2 ~ p")` | ### C. Attribute Selectors (Critical for KTU) | Syntax | Matches | |---|---| | `[attr]` | Element has the attribute | | `[attr="value"]` | Exact value match | | `[attr!="value"]` | Value does NOT equal (or attr absent) | | `[attr^="value"]` | Value **starts with** | | `[attr$="value"]` | Value **ends with** | | `[attr*="value"]` | Value **contains** | | `[attr~="word"]` | Value contains the word (space-separated list) | | `[attr\|="value"]` | Value equals OR starts with `value-` | ### D. jQuery-Specific Pseudo-Selectors (NOT in CSS) | Pseudo | Behaviour | Example | |---|---|---| | `:first` | First matched element only | `$("li:first")` | | `:last` | Last matched element only | `$("li:last")` | | `:even` | 0-indexed even positions (0, 2, 4…) | `$("tr:even")` | | `:odd` | 0-indexed odd positions (1, 3, 5…) | `$("tr:odd")` | | `:eq(n)` | Element at index n (0-based) | `$("p:eq(2)")` | | `:gt(n)` | Index greater than n | `$("li:gt(3)")` | | `:lt(n)` | Index less than n | `$("li:lt(2)")` | | `:animated` | Currently being animated | `$(":animated")` | | `:hidden` | Display:none, hidden inputs, zero dimensions | `$(":hidden")` | | `:visible` | Has layout (non-zero dimensions) | `$(":visible")` | | `:contains(text)` | Contains the literal text | `$("p:contains('Hi')")` | | `:has(sel)` | Contains a descendant matching sel | `$("div:has(p)")` | | `:empty` | Has no children (no text, no elements) | `$("td:empty")` | | `:parent` | Has at least one child (text or element) | `$("td:parent")` | | `:nth-child(n)` | CSS-style nth-child filter | `$("li:nth-child(2)")` | ### E. Form Selectors (Explicitly in KTU Module 2 Syllabus) | Selector | Matches | |---|---| | `:input` | All ``, `

topics.title: Common Element Manipulations in jQuery
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Common Element Manipulations in jQuery # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **jQuery Element Manipulation** refers to the set of cross-browser-compatible, chainable methods provided by the jQuery library that allow a developer to **read, write, replace, clone, and remove** content, attributes, properties, classes, and CSS styles of DOM (Document Object Model) elements selected through a jQuery wrapper object `$()`. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic falls under **Client-Side Scripting using jQuery**, where the `$()` factory function returns a *jQuery object* — a wrapper around zero or more DOM nodes — exposing a unified, simplified API that abstracts away native JavaScript inconsistencies across browsers (the so-called *"Write Less, Do More"* paradigm). | jQuery Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Wrapper Set** | The collection of DOM elements returned by a jQuery selector | | **Getter Pattern** | A method called with **no arguments** → returns the *current* value of the first matched element | | **Setter Pattern** | A method called with **one or more arguments** → sets the value on **all** matched elements | | **Implicit Iteration** | Setter methods automatically loop over every element in the wrapper set | | **Chainability** | Most jQuery methods return a jQuery object, allowing `$('p').addClass('x').fadeIn();` | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal TV Remote" 🖥️📺 Imagine your web page is a **living room full of appliances** (headings, paragraphs, images, divs — these are your *DOM elements*). Without jQuery, you'd have to walk up to each appliance, learn its specific quirky controls, and operate them one by one (this is the *raw JavaScript / DOM API*). **jQuery is a single universal remote control.** The remote has labelled buttons: - `text()`, `html()`, `val()` → *"Show me what the screen is displaying / Change the screen text"* - `attr()`, `prop()` → *"Check the brand label / Replace the brand label"* - `addClass()`, `removeClass()`, `toggleClass()` → *"Pin / Unpin / Flip a sticker on the device"* - `css()` → *"Change the colour, size, or position"* - `append()`, `prepend()`, `before()`, `after()` → *"Insert new items into the shelf above, below, in front, or behind"* - `remove()`, `empty()` → *"Throw the device away / Empty the device's drawer"* - `width()`, `height()`, `offset()` → *"Measure the device"* > [!NOTE] > The remote **does not operate the device itself** — it just sends standardised *signals* (method calls) that the jQuery engine translates into the right native DOM instructions for the current browser. This is why jQuery manipulations are inherently **cross-browser safe**. ## 1.3 Categories of Element Manipulation (KTU Taxonomy) The KTU syllabus groups jQuery manipulation methods into **five functional families**: 1. **Content Manipulation** — `text()`, `html()`, `val()`, `replaceWith()`, `replaceAll()` 2. **Attribute & Property Manipulation** — `attr()`, `removeAttr()`, `prop()`, `removeProp()` 3. **Class & Style Manipulation** — `addClass()`, `removeClass()`, `toggleClass()`, `hasClass()`, `css()` 4. **DOM Insertion / Removal** — `append()`, `prepend()`, `before()`, `after()`, `wrap()`, `unwrap()`, `remove()`, `empty()`, `detach()`, `clone()` 5. **Dimension / Position Manipulation** — `width()`, `height()`, `innerWidth()`, `outerWidth()`, `offset()`, `position()`, `scrollTop()` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In the 2024 scheme, questions on jQuery manipulations are frequently asked in *Part A* (definitions) and *Part B* (write a complete jQuery script that performs a multi-step DOM operation). You must know the **getter/setter duality** of every method cold. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Getter–Setter Duality (Conceptual Foundation) Almost every jQuery manipulation method follows the **"No-Argument = Read, With-Argument = Write"** contract: $$ \text{Method State} = \begin{cases} \text{Getter (Read)} & \text{if arguments.length} = 0 \\[4pt] \text{Setter (Write)} & \text{if arguments.length} \geq 1 \end{cases} $$ - **Getter** returns the value of the *first* matched element. - **Setter** assigns the value to *every* matched element (implicit iteration). - A setter call **returns the jQuery object** → enabling chaining. ## 2.2 Family-Wise Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Content Manipulation - **`text()`** — Gets/sets the **plain text** (HTML tags are escaped). - **`html()`** — Gets/sets the **inner HTML** (tags are interpreted). - **`val()`** — Gets/sets the **value** of form elements (``, `

topics.title: Event Handling in jQuery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Event Handling in jQuery ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Event Handling in jQuery** refers to the mechanism of capturing, responding to, and managing user interactions (or system-generated occurrences) on DOM elements using jQuery's unified cross-browser API. An *event* represents an action detected by the browser — such as a mouse click, key press, form submission, or page load — while *handling* denotes the binding of an event listener (a callback function) to a specific element so that predefined logic executes whenever the event fires. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** jQuery's event model abstracts the inconsistencies of native DOM Level 0 (`element.onclick = ...`) and DOM Level 2 (`addEventListener`) event handling, providing a uniform, chainable interface that works identically across all major browsers. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorbell and Reception Desk" Imagine a busy office building. The **building** is your webpage, the **rooms** are DOM elements, and the **visitors** are user actions (clicks, key presses). Without a system, every action would be chaos. A **doorbell** (the *event listener*) is installed on specific rooms. When a visitor presses the bell, a **signal** is sent to the **reception desk** (the *event handler function*), which then decides what action to take — open the door, deliver a message, or call security. jQuery is essentially a universal doorbell installation company that wires up bells on any room with a single, simple command. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology for Board Exams:** > - **Event Source:** The DOM element where the event originated. > - **Event Type:** The category of the occurrence (e.g., `click`, `keydown`, `submit`). > - **Event Handler / Listener:** The JavaScript function invoked when the event occurs. > - **Event Object:** A structured data object passed to the handler carrying details about the event. ### 1.3 Why jQuery Event Handling? (KTU Board Perspective) | Native JavaScript Drawback | jQuery Solution | |---|---| | `addEventListener` not supported in IE < 9 | Unified `.on()` works across all browsers | | Verbose syntax: `document.getElementById(...).addEventListener(...)` | Concise: `$('#btn').click(...)` | | Manual `removeEventListener` cleanup | Simple `.off()` method | | Inconsistent `event` object across browsers | Normalized `event` object with helpers | | Difficult event delegation | Native `.on()` delegation syntax | --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The jQuery Event Lifecycle jQuery event handling follows a strict three-phase lifecycle: 1. **Binding Phase** — An event listener is attached to one or more selected DOM elements using methods like `.on()`, `.click()`, `.bind()`. 2. **Triggering Phase** — A user action (or programmatic call) generates the event. jQuery normalizes the event object and dispatches it to the bound handler. 3. **Resolution Phase** — The handler executes its logic. Optionally, the event may bubble up the DOM tree (propagation) or be explicitly cancelled. ### 2.2 Core jQuery Event Methods — The High-Yield Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **mandatory knowledge** for KTU board exams. Memorize the syntax patterns, not just the names. | Method | Syntax Pattern | Purpose | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | `.on()` | `$('selector').on(event, childSel, data, handler)` | Modern, recommended way to bind events | Supports delegation | | `.off()` | `$('selector').off(event, handler)` | Removes event handlers | Undoes `.on()` | | `.click()` | `$('sel').click(handler)` | Shorthand for `on('click', handler)` | No delegation | | `.bind()` | `$('sel').bind(event, handler)` | Legacy direct binding | Deprecated since 3.0 | | `.delegate()` | `$('parent').delegate(sel, event, handler)` | Legacy delegation method | Superseded by `.on()` | | `.live()` | `$('sel').live(event, handler)` | Obsolete | Removed in jQuery 1.9+ | | `.one()` | `$('sel').one(event, handler)` | Fires handler exactly once | Auto-unbinds after execution | | `.trigger()` | `$('sel').trigger(event, data)` | Programmatically invoke handlers | Can pass extra parameters | | `.triggerHandler()` | `$('sel').triggerHandler(event)` | Trigger without default browser action | Does not bubble | | `$.Event()` | `var e = $.Event('click')` | Create a custom event object | Used for testing | ### 2.3 The Event Object — Standardized Properties jQuery wraps the native `event` object and adds cross-browser-safe properties. The handler receives this object as its first argument. | Property / Method | Type | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | `event.type` | String | Identifies which event fired (e.g., `"click"`) | | `event.target` | DOM Element | The originating element (where event started) | | `event.currentTarget` | DOM Element | The element whose listener is currently executing | | `event.delegateTarget` | DOM Element | The element where the `.on()` delegate is attached | | `event.data` | Object | Custom data passed via `.on(event, data, handler)` | | `event.result` | Any | The value returned by the last handler | | `event.namespace` | String | Event namespace, e.g., `"click.myPlugin"` | | `event.preventDefault()` | Function | Stops default browser action (e.g., form submit reload) | | `event.stopPropagation()` | Function | Halts event bubbling to ancestor elements | | `event.stopImmediatePropagation()` | Function | Stops bubbling AND remaining handlers on same element | | `event.isDefaultPrevented()` | Function | Boolean check if `preventDefault` was called | | `event.pageX` / `event.pageY` | Number | Mouse coordinates relative to document | | `event.which` | Number | Normalized key/button code | ### 2.4 Event Propagation — Bubbling vs. Capturing > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Board Favorite:** Questions on event propagation appear frequently. Understand both directions. Event propagation in the DOM has **three phases** as defined by the W3C DOM Level 2 specification: 1. **Capturing Phase (Trickle Down):** Event travels from `document` → `html` → `body` → ... → target. jQuery does not natively support capturing-phase listeners (it binds to the bubbling phase). 2. **Target Phase:** Event reaches the actual target element. 3. **Bubbling Phase (Bubble Up):** Event travels from target → ancestors → ... → `document`. **This is the default jQuery behavior.** ### 2.5 Event Delegation — The Engineering Concept **Event Delegation** is a powerful pattern where a single event handler is attached to a **parent element** to manage events for all current and future children matching a selector. This works because of event bubbling. **Why is it critical for engineering applications?** - **Performance:** Attaching 1000 listeners to 1000 table rows is slow; one listener on `` handles all rows. - **Dynamic Content:** Newly added elements (via AJAX) automatically inherit the delegated handler — no re-binding needed. - **Memory Efficiency:** Fewer closures, less GC pressure. **The Master Formula:** $$ \text{Delegated Binding} = \text{Parent Selector} + \text{`.on(event, childSelector, handler)`} $$ --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Code Walkthroughs ### 3.1 Evolution of Event Binding — Step-by-Step Since this is a **Computer Science (Web Programming)** course, code derivation is essential. Let us trace how jQuery event binding evolved. **Step 1 — jQuery 1.0: The `.bind()` Era (Direct Binding)** The original jQuery method bound handlers directly to every element matched by the selector at the time of the call. This is the foundational pattern: ```javascript // Binds click handler to all

elements present at execution time $('p').bind('click', function(event) { console.log('Paragraph clicked: ' + event.target.textContent); }); ``` **Logical Analysis:** - The selector `$('p')` scans the DOM immediately and produces a static set of elements. - For each element in that set, jQuery internally invokes `addEventListener('click', wrappedHandler)`. - **Limitation:** Any `

` added to the DOM *after* this call will NOT have the handler. **Step 2 — jQuery 1.3: The `.live()` Attempt (Document-Level Delegation)** ```javascript // Binds to document; checks target on every event $('p').live('click', function() { alert('A paragraph was clicked!'); }); ``` **Logical Analysis:** - jQuery attaches ONE listener to the `document` root. - On any click anywhere on the page, the handler checks if `event.target` is a `

` (or descendant). - **Limitation:** Performance suffers because the event traverses the entire DOM before being matched. `.live()` was **removed in jQuery 1.9**. **Step 3 — jQuery 1.4.2: The `.delegate()` Method (Scoped Delegation)** ```javascript // Attaches handler to #container; filters for dynamic .item descendants $('#container').delegate('.item', 'click', function() { $(this).toggleClass('active'); }); ``` **Logical Analysis:** - Handler is attached to `#container` (an ancestor you control). - When a click bubbles up, jQuery checks if the original target matches `.item`. - **Advantage:** Smaller propagation path → better performance than `.live()`. - **Advantage:** Works for dynamically created `.item` elements. **Step 4 — jQuery 1.7+: The Unified `.on()` Method (Modern Standard)** The `.on()` method unified `.bind()`, `.live()`, and `.delegate()` into a single API. The selector argument's *presence* determines the mode: ```javascript // Mode A: Direct binding (replaces .bind()) $('p').on('click', function() { console.log('Bound directly'); }); // Mode B: Delegated binding (replaces .delegate()) $('#container').on('click', '.item', function() { $(this).toggleClass('active'); }); ``` **The Decision Rule (derive this — high-yield):** $$ \text{If a child selector is passed to } .on() \Rightarrow \text{Delegation Mode} $$ $$ \text{If NO child selector is passed} \Rightarrow \text{Direct Binding Mode} $$ ### 3.2 Custom Data Passing — Full Derivation jQuery allows passing custom data into a handler, which is then exposed via `event.data`. The mathematical model: $$ \text{Handler Signature} = \texttt{function(event)} \quad \text{where } \texttt{event.data} = \text{Passed Object} $$ ```javascript var userData = { userId: 42, role: 'admin' }; // Pass extra data as the 2nd argument to .on() $('#loginBtn').on('click', userData, function(event) { console.log('User ID:', event.data.userId); // Output: 42 console.log('Role:', event.data.role); // Output: admin }); ``` **Step-by-step Execution Trace:** 1. `$('#loginBtn')` selects the element with `id="loginBtn"`. 2. `.on('click', userData, handler)` registers the click listener. 3. The `userData` object is stored in jQuery's internal event cache, keyed to this binding. 4. When clicked, jQuery constructs an `event` object and copies `userData` into `event.data`. 5. The handler executes with full access to the user data without relying on closures. ### 3.3 Namespaced Events — The Plugin Developer's Tool Namespacing allows multiple handlers on the same event and enables surgical removal of specific handlers. ```javascript // Binding with namespaces (separated by a dot) $('#btn').on('click.myPlugin', handlerA); $('#btn').on('click.analytics', handlerB); // Remove ONLY the myPlugin handler $('#btn').off('click.myPlugin'); // Remove ALL click handlers (no namespace) $('#btn').off('click'); ``` **Derivation of the pattern:** $$ \text{Event Name} = \texttt{type.namespace} \quad \text{where} \quad \texttt{type} \in \text{String}, \quad \texttt{namespace} \in \text{String} $$ ### 3.4 Programmatic Triggering — `.trigger()` vs `.triggerHandler()` ```javascript $('#myInput').on('change', function() { console.log('Change detected'); }); // Method 1: trigger() — fires handler AND default browser action $('#myInput').trigger('change'); // Method 2: triggerHandler() — fires handler ONLY, no default action $('#myInput').triggerHandler('change'); ``` | Aspect | `.trigger()` | `.triggerHandler()` | |---|---|---| | Bubbles up DOM | Yes | No (stops at matched element) | | Default browser action | Executes | Suppressed | | Returns | jQuery object (chainable) | Return value of last handler | | Operates on multiple elements | Yes (all matched) | Yes (only first matched) | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 jQuery Event Lifecycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Action: Click, Keypress, etc.] --> B{Event Captured by Browser} B --> C[Event Travels to Target Element] C --> D{Target Phase: Handler Executes} D --> E{Bubbling Phase Active?} E -- Yes --> F[Event Bubbles to Ancestors] F --> G{Ancestor Has Handler?} G -- Yes --> H[Ancestor Handler Executes] G -- No --> I[Continue Bubbling Up] I --> J[Reaches document Root] H --> J D --> K{stopPropagation Called?} K -- Yes --> L[Bubbling Halted] K -- No --> E J --> M[Event Lifecycle Complete] L --> M ``` ### 4.2 Event Delegation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph DOMTree[Document Object Model] Doc[document] Cont[#container Parent] Row1[.item Row 1] Row2[.item Row 2] Row3[.item Row 3 NEW Dynamic] Doc --> Cont Cont --> Row1 Cont --> Row2 Cont --> Row3 end Bind[$'#container'.on click, '.item', handler] Bind -. Attaches 1 listener .-> Cont UserClick[User Clicks Row 3] --> Row3 Row3 -->|Bubbles Up| Cont Cont -->|Matches .item selector| Handler[Handler Executes on Row 3] Cont -->|Bubbles Up| Doc style Bind fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style Handler fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745 style Row3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 ``` ### 4.3 Event Binding Method Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR Start[Need to Bind Event?] --> Q1{Element Already
in DOM?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need Delegation
for Future Elements?} Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use .on with
Delegation Mode] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3 Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use .on Direct Mode
or .click shorthand] Q3 --> End[Code: $'parent'.on event, 'child', handler] Q4 --> End2[Code: $'selector'.on event, handler] style Start fill:#e1f5ff style End fill:#d4edda style End2 fill:#d4edda ``` ### 4.4 The jQuery Event Object Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD EventObj[jQuery Event Object] EventObj --> Meta[Metadata] EventObj --> Targets[Target References] EventObj --> Controls[Control Methods] EventObj --> Coords[Coordinate Data] Meta --> M1[type: String] Meta --> M2[namespace: String] Meta --> M3[timestamp: Number] Meta --> M4[data: Object] Targets --> T1[target: Original Element] Targets --> T2[currentTarget: Listener Element] Targets --> T3[delegateTarget: Delegation Parent] Controls --> C1[preventDefault] Controls --> C2[stopPropagation] Controls --> C3[stopImmediatePropagation] Controls --> C4[isDefaultPrevented] Coords --> Co1[pageX, pageY] Coords --> Co2[which] style EventObj fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style Meta fill:#d1ecf1 style Targets fill:#f8d7da style Controls fill:#d4edda style Coords fill:#e2e3e5 ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define event handling in jQuery. List any three jQuery event methods with their syntax.** **Model Answer:** Event handling in jQuery is the process of defining functions (handlers) that execute in response to user actions or browser-triggered events on selected DOM elements, using jQuery's simplified, cross-browser API. The three jQuery event methods are: 1. **`.on()`** — The modern, unified method for binding events. Syntax: `$('selector').on('event', [childSelector], [data], handler);` 2. **`.click()`** — Shorthand for binding a click event. Syntax: `$('selector').click(handler);` 3. **`.off()`** — Removes an event handler previously bound with `.on()`. Syntax: `$('selector').off('event', handler);` --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain the concept of event delegation in jQuery. Why is it preferred over direct binding?** **Model Answer:** Event delegation is a technique in jQuery where a single event handler is attached to a *parent* element to manage events for its *current and future* children, leveraging the DOM event bubbling mechanism. **Syntax:** ```javascript $('#parent').on('click', '.child', function() { // handler logic }); ``` **Advantages over direct binding:** 1. **Handles Dynamic Elements:** Newly added children automatically receive the delegated handler without re-binding. 2. **Better Performance:** A single handler on a parent is more efficient than binding hundreds of handlers to individual children. 3. **Lower Memory Footprint:** Fewer event listener objects stored in memory. 4. **Cleaner Code:** Centralizes event logic at a common ancestor. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024] **(a)** With a suitable example, explain the different ways of binding event handlers in jQuery. Compare `.bind()`, `.live()`, `.delegate()`, and `.on()` methods. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is the jQuery event object? Explain any five important properties and methods of the event object with examples. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** jQuery provides multiple methods to bind event handlers to DOM elements. The evolution of these methods reflects the need for better performance and dynamic content support. **1. `.bind()` Method (Direct Binding — jQuery 1.0):** ```javascript $('button').bind('click', function() { alert('Button clicked'); }); ``` This binds the click handler to all ` Event Delegation Demo

Dynamic List

  • Initial Item 1
  • Initial Item 2
``` **Explanation — How `.on()` Achieves Delegation:** 1. **Binding Location:** `$('#itemList').on('click', '.item', handler)` attaches a **single** event listener to the `
    ` parent element, NOT to each `
  • ` individually. 2. **Selector Matching:** The second argument `'.item'` is the *child filter*. When a click event fires anywhere inside `#itemList`, jQuery checks whether `event.target` matches `.item` (or is a descendant of one). 3. **Bubbling Mechanism:** When a user clicks a `
  • `, the click event bubbles up to the `
      `. The `
        `'s handler runs, identifies the matching `.item` element, and invokes the callback with `this` set to the matched `
      • `. 4. **Dynamic Element Support:** Because the listener is on the parent (which never changes), any new `
      • ` added via `$('#addBtn')` click is automatically covered. No re-binding is required. 5. **Performance Benefit:** Even with 1000 dynamically added items, only ONE event listener exists in memory, vastly outperforming 1000 individual `.click()` bindings. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Confusing `event.target` and `event.currentTarget`** — Students frequently lose 1–2 marks. Remember: `target` = where event *started*; `currentTarget` = where listener is *attached*. > 2. **Forgetting to call `event.preventDefault()`** in form submit handlers — causes page reload, breaking AJAX logic. Examiners will deduct marks. > 3. **Using deprecated `.live()` or `.bind()`** in answers without mentioning they are obsolete — shows outdated knowledge. Always prefer `.on()`. > 4. **Mixing up `.trigger()` and `.triggerHandler()`** — `trigger()` runs default browser actions, `triggerHandler()` does not. Examiners test this distinction. > 5. **Not mentioning event delegation** when asked about dynamic content — this is the canonical use case and earns full marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **jQuery event handling** provides a unified, cross-browser API replacing inconsistent native DOM events. - ⭐ The **modern standard** for binding is `.on()`; use `.off()` to remove handlers. - ⭐ `.click()`, `.hover()`, `.submit()` etc. are **shorthand methods** — internally they call `.on()`. - ⭐ `.bind()` and `.live()` are **deprecated/removed** — never use them in KTU answers. - ⭐ **Event delegation** uses bubbling: bind to parent, filter by child selector → `$(parent).on(event, childSel, handler)`. - ⭐ **Event bubbling** is the default; events flow target → ancestors → document. jQuery does NOT support capturing phase natively. - ⭐ The **event object** is passed as the first argument to handlers and contains normalized properties. - ⭐ `event.target` = originating element; `event.currentTarget` = element with the listener; `event.delegateTarget` = delegation anchor. - ⭐ `event.preventDefault()` blocks default browser actions (form submits, link navigation). - ⭐ `event.stopPropagation()` stops bubbling; `event.stopImmediatePropagation()` also stops other handlers on the same element. - ⭐ `.trigger(event, data)` programmatically invokes handlers AND default actions; `.triggerHandler()` does NOT trigger defaults. - ⭐ **Namespaced events** (`click.myPlugin`) allow surgical removal of specific handlers via `.off('click.myPlugin')`. - ⭐ `.one(event, handler)` fires the handler **exactly once** then auto-removes it. - ⭐ Custom data can be passed via `.on(event, dataObject, handler)` and accessed via `event.data`. - ⭐ For **dynamic AJAX-injected elements**, ALWAYS use delegated `.on()` binding on a stable parent. ---

topics.title: Node.js - The Architecture of Node.js, Working with Node.js, Adding Express to Node.js
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Node.js - The Architecture of Node.js, Working with Node.js, Adding Express to Node.js

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Node.js — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Node.js** is an **open-source, cross-platform, back-end JavaScript runtime environment** that executes JavaScript code **outside a web browser**. It is built on **Google's V8 JavaScript engine** and uses an **event-driven, non-blocking I/O architecture**, making it ideal for building highly scalable, data-intensive, real-time network applications.

Node.js was created by **Ryan Dahl in 2009** and has since become the de-facto standard for server-side JavaScript development. It unifies web application development around a single programming language (JavaScript) for both client-side and server-side.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Node.js is a *runtime*, **not** a framework or a programming language. The language is JavaScript; the runtime is Node.js.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Model

Imagine a busy restaurant:

- **Traditional (Blocking) Server (like Apache + PHP):** A waiter takes an order from Table 1, then walks to the kitchen and *stands there* until the food is cooked. Only then does the waiter serve Table 1 and move to Table 2. One customer at a time.
- **Node.js (Non-Blocking) Server:** A waiter takes the order from Table 1, hands the chit to the kitchen, and **immediately moves to Table 2** to take another order. When Table 1's food is ready, the kitchen calls the waiter back. This single waiter can handle **dozens of tables efficiently**.

This is the essence of Node.js's **event-driven, non-blocking I/O model** — a **single thread** can manage thousands of concurrent connections.

---

## 1.3 Express.js — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Express.js** (or simply **Express**) is a **minimal, fast, and flexible Node.js web application framework** that provides a robust set of features for building **single-page, multi-page, and hybrid web applications** and **RESTful APIs**. It is the *de-facto* standard server framework for Node.js.

---

## 1.4 Key Architectural Metrics

| Metric | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Initial Release** | **May 27, 2009** |
| **Written In** | **C, C++, JavaScript** |
| **Engine** | **V8 (Google Chrome's JS Engine)** |
| **License** | **MIT License** |
| **Package Manager** | **npm (Node Package Manager)** |
| **Default Version Manager** | **nvm** |
| **Latest Stable Line (2024)** | **Node.js 20.x / 22.x LTS** |

---

## 1.5 Where Node.js is Used (Real-World Industry Map)

> [!NOTE]
> Node.js powers **36+ million websites** globally (per W3Techs 2024 statistics), including **Netflix, LinkedIn, Uber, PayPal, NASA, and Walmart**.

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Real-time chat** | WhatsApp Web clones, Slack integrations |
| **Streaming** | Netflix's UI layer |
| **IoT** | Sensor data ingestion at massive scale |
| **Microservices / REST APIs** | Backend for SPAs (React, Angular, Vue) |
| **Serverless** | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions runtimes |
| **DevOps Tooling** | Build tools like Webpack, Vite, npm itself |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of Node.js — Component Breakdown

Node.js architecture is a **layered stack** that sits on top of the operating system. Understanding each layer is *critical* for KTU exam answers.

### Layer 1: The V8 JavaScript Engine (The Brain)

- **Developed by:** Google (for Chrome)
- **Role:** Compiles JavaScript directly to **machine code** (not interpreted) using **Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation**.
- **Why it matters:** Provides **C++-level execution speed** for JavaScript.

```
JS Source Code  →  Parser  →  AST  →  Ignition (Interpreter)  →  TurboFan (Optimizing JIT)  →  Machine Code
```

### Layer 2: libuv (The Heart of Async I/O)

- **libuv** is a **C library** that provides:
  - The **Event Loop**
  - **Asynchronous I/O** operations (file system, network, DNS)
  - A **Worker Thread Pool** (default size: **4 threads**, configurable via `UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE`)
  - **Cross-platform abstraction** (works on Linux, macOS, Windows)

### Layer 3: Node.js Bindings (C++ Glue Layer)

- Exposes low-level OS features (file system, sockets, threads) to JavaScript as native modules (e.g., `fs`, `net`, `crypto`).

### Layer 4: Node.js Standard Library (The API)

- JavaScript modules: `http`, `fs`, `path`, `events`, `os`, `crypto`, `stream`, `util`, etc.

---

## 2.2 The Event Loop — The Core Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Event Loop** is what makes Node.js *non-blocking*. It is a **continuously running loop** that picks up completed asynchronous tasks and pushes their callbacks onto the call stack.

### Event Loop Phases (KTU High-Yield)

The Event Loop has **6 distinct phases**, executed in **strict order, repeatedly**:

| Phase | Name | What Happens |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Timers** | Executes callbacks scheduled by `setTimeout()` and `setInterval()`. |
| 2 | **Pending Callbacks** | Executes I/O callbacks deferred from the previous loop iteration. |
| 3 | **Idle, Prepare** | Internal use only (not user-facing). |
| 4 | **Poll** | Retrieves new I/O events; executes their callbacks. **Most application code runs here.** |
| 5 | **Check** | Executes callbacks scheduled by `setImmediate()`. |
| 6 | **Close Callbacks** | Executes `close` event handlers (e.g., `socket.on('close')`). |

### Process.nextTick() and Microtasks

- **`process.nextTick()`** callbacks run **after the current operation**, **before** the Event Loop continues. They have **higher priority** than Promises.
- **Promise `.then()` microtasks** run after the next tick, before the Event Loop proceeds to the next phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The order of execution is: **Synchronous code → process.nextTick() → Promise microtasks → Event Loop phases**. This is a **favourite 7-mark question**.

---

## 2.3 Node.js Single-Threaded Model — Clarification

- Node.js has a **single main thread** for executing JavaScript.
- **Heavy/CPU-bound work** is delegated to the **libuv thread pool** (default: **4 threads**).
- For truly CPU-intensive tasks, the **Worker Threads module** (`worker_threads`) is used.

---

## 2.4 Working with Node.js — Core Concepts

### A. The REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop)

Launch by typing `node` in the terminal. It supports:
- **Read:** Reads user input
- **Eval:** Evaluates JS expression
- **Print:** Prints the result
- **Loop:** Loops back to Read

Useful REPL commands: `.help`, `.exit`, `.load`, `.save`, `_` (last result).

### B. Node.js Module Systems

**1. CommonJS (Default, Server-side standard)**

```javascript
// math.js (Module file)
function add(a, b) { return a + b; }
module.exports = { add };
```

```javascript
// app.js (Consumer file)
const math = require('./math');
console.log(math.add(5, 3));   // 8
```

**2. ES Modules (Modern, Standardized)**

```javascript
// math.mjs
export function add(a, b) { return a + b; }
```

```javascript
// app.mjs
import { add } from './math.mjs';
console.log(add(5, 3));   // 8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU expects students to know that **module.exports** is the CommonJS export object, while **exports** is a shorthand reference to it (do not reassign `exports` directly).

### C. The `npm` (Node Package Manager) Ecosystem

| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `npm init` | Initialize a new `package.json` |
| `npm init -y` | Initialize with defaults |
| `npm install <pkg>` | Install a package locally (adds to `dependencies`) |
| `npm install -g <pkg>` | Install globally |
| `npm install --save-dev <pkg>` | Install as dev dependency |
| `npm uninstall <pkg>` | Remove a package |
| `npm start` | Run the `start` script from `package.json` |
| `npm run <script>` | Run a custom script |

**`package.json`** is the heart of every Node project — it holds metadata, dependencies, and scripts.

### D. The Built-in `http` Module (Foundation for Express)

```javascript
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  res.statusCode = 200;
  res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/plain');
  res.end('Hello from Node.js');
});
server.listen(3000, () => console.log('Server running on port 3000'));
```

### E. The `fs` (File System) Module

Supports both **synchronous** (`fs.readFileSync`) and **asynchronous** (`fs.readFile`) variants. KTU expects you to use callbacks or Promises.

### F. The `events` Module & EventEmitter

> [!NOTE]
> **Much of Node.js is built on the Observer Pattern.** Many objects (like `http.Server`, streams) extend `EventEmitter` and emit named events that listeners consume.

```javascript
const EventEmitter = require('events');
const emitter = new EventEmitter();
emitter.on('greet', (name) => console.log(`Hello, ${name}!`));
emitter.emit('greet', 'KTU Student');   // Output: Hello, KTU Student!
```

---

## 2.5 Adding Express to Node.js — Core Concepts

### Why Express over plain `http`?

| Feature | Plain `http` | Express |
|---|---|---|
| Routing | Manual URL parsing | Built-in router |
| Middleware | None | Powerful middleware pipeline |
| Request body parsing | Manual stream handling | `express.json()`, `express.urlencoded()` |
| Static files | Manual | `express.static()` |
| Error handling | Manual try/catch everywhere | Centralized error middleware |
| Code readability | Verbose | Concise and declarative |

### Installation

```bash
npm init -y
npm install express
```

### Minimal Express Server

```javascript
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const PORT = 3000;

app.get('/', (req, res) => res.send('Hello from Express!'));

app.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Server running on http://localhost:${PORT}`));
```

### The Request (`req`) and Response (`res`) Objects

- **`req.params`** — Route parameters (e.g., `/users/:id` → `req.params.id`)
- **`req.query`** — URL query string (e.g., `?name=John` → `req.query.name`)
- **`req.body`** — Parsed request body (requires `express.json()` middleware)
- **`req.headers`** — HTTP headers
- **`res.send()`** — Send a response (auto-sets Content-Type)
- **`res.json()`** — Send a JSON response
- **`res.status()`** — Set HTTP status code
- **`res.redirect()`** — Redirect to another URL

### Routing in Express

```javascript
app.get('/users', (req, res) => { /* List all users */ });
app.post('/users', (req, res) => { /* Create user */ });
app.put('/users/:id', (req, res) => { /* Update user */ });
app.delete('/users/:id', (req, res) => { /* Delete user */ });
```

**`app.all()`** matches all HTTP methods; **`app.use()`** mounts middleware.

### Middleware — The Heart of Express

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Middleware functions** are functions that have access to the `request` object (`req`), the `response` object (`res`), and the **next** middleware function in the application's request-response cycle. They can:
> - Execute any code
> - Modify `req` and `res` objects
> - End the request-response cycle
> - Call the next middleware in the stack

**Middleware execution order:**

```
Client Request → Middleware 1 → Middleware 2 → ... → Route Handler → Response
```

```javascript
// Application-level middleware
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  console.log(`${req.method} ${req.url} - ${new Date().toISOString()}`);
  next();
});

// Built-in middleware
app.use(express.json());
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true }));
app.use(express.static('public'));

// Third-party middleware
app.use(morgan('dev'));   // Logging
app.use(cors());          // CORS handling
```

### Error-Handling Middleware

Must have **4 parameters** (the `err` argument signals it as an error handler):

```javascript
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  console.error(err.stack);
  res.status(500).json({ error: 'Something broke!' });
});
```

### Router Module (For Modular Routing)

```javascript
// routes/users.js
const router = express.Router();
router.get('/', (req, res) => res.send('All users'));
router.get('/:id', (req, res) => res.send(`User ${req.params.id}`));
module.exports = router;

// app.js
app.use('/users', userRouter);
```

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Value / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Event Loop Phases** | **6** (Timers → Pending → Idle/Prepare → Poll → Check → Close) | Cyclical |
| **Default Thread Pool Size** | **4** | Set via `UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE` |
| **Middleware Parameters** | `(req, res, next)` for normal; `(err, req, res, next)` for error | Arity matters |
| **`module.exports` vs `exports`** | `exports` is a **reference** to `module.exports` | Reassigning `exports` breaks the link |
| **CommonJS import** | `const x = require('./file')` | Synchronous, cached |
| **ES Module import** | `import x from './file.mjs'` | Static, must be top-level |
| **npm install flags** | `-g` (global), `--save-dev` (devDep), `-D` (short for devDep) | Updates `package.json` |
| **`res.json()` vs `res.send()`** | `json()` sets `Content-Type: application/json` and calls `JSON.stringify` | `send()` infers type |
| **HTTP methods in Express** | `app.get/post/put/delete/patch/all()` | `all()` matches any method |
| **`next()` argument** | `next()` → next middleware; `next('route')` → skip remaining in route; `next(err)` → jump to error handler | Critical distinction |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Node.js + Express is the **MERN / MEAN stack backbone**:
- **M**ongoDB (database)
- **E**xpress (server framework)
- **R**eact / **A**ngular (frontend)
- **N**ode.js (runtime)

This stack enables **JavaScript end-to-end**, drastically reducing context switching for engineering teams and powering modern SaaS products.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tracing a Request Through the Node.js Event Loop

Consider the following code block (a classic KTU-style tracing question):

```javascript
console.log('1. Start');

setTimeout(() => console.log('2. setTimeout'), 0);

setImmediate(() => console.log('3. setImmediate'));

process.nextTick(() => console.log('4. nextTick'));

Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('5. Promise'));

console.log('6. End');
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1: V8 compiles the script.**
The V8 engine parses the entire file, builds the AST, and begins execution from top to bottom on the main thread (Call Stack).

**Step 2: `console.log('1. Start')` is pushed onto the call stack.**
It executes **synchronously** and prints `1. Start`. Output buffer receives `"1. Start\n"`.

**Step 3: `setTimeout(..., 0)` is encountered.**
The Node.js bindings delegate the timer to **libuv**. A timer is registered in the **Timers phase** queue with a 0 ms delay. The main thread is **not blocked**; it proceeds.

**Step 4: `setImmediate(...)` is encountered.**
libuv registers this callback in the **Check phase** queue. The main thread proceeds.

**Step 5: `process.nextTick(...)` is encountered.**
This callback is added to the **`nextTickQueue`**, which has **higher priority** than microtasks and Event Loop phases. The main thread proceeds.

**Step 6: `Promise.resolve().then(...)` is encountered.**
The `.then` callback is added to the **microtask queue**. The main thread proceeds.

**Step 7: `console.log('6. End')` executes synchronously.**
Output: `6. End`.

**Step 8: Synchronous code complete. Microtask & nextTick processing.**
The Call Stack is now empty. Node.js **drains the nextTick queue first**, then **the microtask queue**, before returning to the Event Loop.

**Step 9: nextTick queue drained.**
Output: `4. nextTick`.

**Step 10: Microtask queue drained.**
Output: `5. Promise`.

**Step 11: Event Loop — Timers phase.**
The 0 ms timer has expired. Its callback runs.
Output: `2. setTimeout`.

**Step 12: Event Loop — Check phase.**
The `setImmediate` callback runs.
Output: `3. setImmediate`.

### Final Output

```
1. Start
6. End
4. nextTick
5. Promise
2. setTimeout
3. setImmediate
```

### Key Derivation Rule

$$\text{Output Order} = \text{Sync} \rightarrow \text{nextTick} \rightarrow \text{Microtasks} \rightarrow \text{Timers} \rightarrow \text{...} \rightarrow \text{Check}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Middleware Chain Execution

Given this Express app:

```javascript
app.use((req, res, next) => { console.log('MW1'); next(); });
app.use((req, res, next) => { console.log('MW2'); next(); });
app.get('/test', (req, res, next) => { console.log('Route'); next(); });
app.use((req, res, next) => { console.log('MW3'); next(); });
app.use((err, req, res, next) => { console.log('Error'); });
```

A `GET /test` request yields:

**Step 1:** Request enters Express's internal router.
**Step 2:** `MW1` runs → logs `MW1` → calls `next()`.
**Step 3:** `MW2` runs → logs `MW2` → calls `next()`.
**Step 4:** Route handler matches `/test` → logs `Route` → calls `next()`.
**Step 5:** `MW3` runs → logs `MW3` → calls `next()`.
**Step 6:** No more handlers; Express sends default `200 OK` response.

### Output

```
MW1
MW2
Route
MW3
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Scenario | Best Node.js Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Build REST API quickly | **Express** | Mature, huge ecosystem |
| Real-time bidirectional comms | **ws / Socket.IO** | WebSocket support |
| Heavy CPU computation | **`worker_threads`** | Avoid blocking main loop |
| File uploads | **Multer** | Express middleware for `multipart/form-data` |
| Template rendering (server-side) | **EJS / Pug / Handlebars** | View engines for Express |
| Database (NoSQL) | **Mongoose (MongoDB)** | Schema-based ODM |
| Authentication | **Passport.js / JWT** | Standard auth middlewares |
| Validation | **Joi / Zod / express-validator** | Schema validation |
| Logging | **Morgan / Winston** | HTTP & app-level logging |
| Testing | **Jest / Mocha / Supertest** | Unit + integration tests |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Building a CRUD Endpoint (Step-by-Step)

**Goal:** Create a user-management API with `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE` routes.

**Step 1: Project Initialization**

```bash
mkdir user-api && cd user-api
npm init -y
npm install express
```

**Step 2: Create `app.js`**

```javascript
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const PORT = 3000;

// Built-in middleware
app.use(express.json());

// In-memory data store (for demo)
let users = [
  { id: 1, name: 'Alice' },
  { id: 2, name: 'Bob' }
];

// GET /users — list all
app.get('/users', (req, res) => {
  res.json(users);
});

// GET /users/:id — fetch one
app.get('/users/:id', (req, res) => {
  const user = users.find(u => u.id === parseInt(req.params.id));
  if (!user) return res.status(404).json({ error: 'User not found' });
  res.json(user);
});

// POST /users — create
app.post('/users', (req, res) => {
  const newUser = { id: users.length + 1, name: req.body.name };
  users.push(newUser);
  res.status(201).json(newUser);
});

// PUT /users/:id — update
app.put('/users/:id', (req, res) => {
  const user = users.find(u => u.id === parseInt(req.params.id));
  if (!user) return res.status(404).json({ error: 'User not found' });
  user.name = req.body.name;
  res.json(user);
});

// DELETE /users/:id — remove
app.delete('/users/:id', (req, res) => {
  const index = users.findIndex(u => u.id === parseInt(req.params.id));
  if (index === -1) return res.status(404).json({ error: 'User not found' });
  users.splice(index, 1);
  res.status(204).send();
});

// Centralized error handler
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  console.error(err.stack);
  res.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal Server Error' });
});

app.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Server on http://localhost:${PORT}`));
```

**Step 3: Run and Test**

```bash
node app.js
# In another terminal:
curl http://localhost:3000/users
curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"name":"Charlie"}' http://localhost:3000/users
```

**Derivation of the request flow for `POST /users`:**

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP POST}} \text{Express} \xrightarrow{\text{express.json()}} \text{req.body parsed} \xrightarrow{\text{app.post('/users')}} \text{Handler} \xrightarrow{\text{res.status(201).json()}} \text{Client}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the event-driven, non-blocking I/O architecture of Node.js. Why is it suited for I/O-intensive applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Node.js uses an **event-driven, non-blocking I/O architecture** built on the **libuv** library. When a request arrives, it is dispatched to the appropriate handler and the **main thread is not blocked** while waiting for I/O. Instead, the I/O operation runs in the background (in the libuv thread pool or via OS-level async APIs), and a **callback** is invoked once the operation completes.

This is suited for **I/O-intensive applications** such as chat servers, streaming services, and REST APIs because:
1. A **single thread** can handle **thousands of concurrent connections**, reducing memory overhead.
2. **No context-switching cost** between threads for typical web workloads.
3. The **Event Loop** efficiently manages the lifecycle of asynchronous operations.

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks for explanation + 1.5 marks for suitability.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between `module.exports` and `exports` in Node.js.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `module.exports` | `exports` |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The actual export object returned by `require()`. | A **local reference** (alias) to `module.exports`. |
| **Reassignment** | Safe; always reflects the export. | **Dangerous**; breaks the link with `module.exports`. |
| **Usage** | `module.exports = { add, sub }` | `exports.add = add; exports.sub = sub` |
| **Precedence** | Takes precedence if both are set. | Ignored if `module.exports` is reassigned. |

Example pitfall:
```javascript
// Wrong
exports = function() { return 'hello'; };
// Correct
module.exports = function() { return 'hello'; };
```

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [Choose either A or B]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of Node.js**. Describe the role of the **V8 engine** and the **libuv library** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Event Loop** in Node.js. List and briefly describe its **six phases** in the order of execution. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Node.js Architecture (7 marks)**

Node.js has a layered architecture that sits on top of the operating system:

**1. V8 JavaScript Engine (2 marks):**
- Developed by Google, originally for Chrome.
- Performs **Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation** of JavaScript to native machine code.
- Comprises the **Parser**, **Ignition** (interpreter), and **TurboFan** (optimizing compiler).
- Provides C++-level performance for JavaScript.

**2. libuv Library (2 marks):**
- A C library that is the **heart of Node.js async I/O**.
- Provides the **Event Loop**, **thread pool** (default 4 threads), and **cross-platform async I/O abstractions**.
- Handles file system, network, DNS, and child process operations asynchronously.

**3. Node.js Bindings (1 mark):**
- C++ glue layer exposing OS-level features as JavaScript modules (`fs`, `net`, `os`).

**4. Standard Library (1 mark):**
- Built-in JS modules: `http`, `events`, `stream`, `util`, `crypto`, `path`, etc.

**5. Application Code (1 mark):**
- User-written JavaScript that uses the standard library API.

*(Include the layered diagram from Section 4.1 for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Event Loop & Its Phases (7 marks)**

The **Event Loop** is a continuously running loop in libuv that orchestrates the execution of asynchronous callbacks. It has **six phases** that execute in strict order, repeatedly:

| # | Phase | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Timers** | Executes `setTimeout` and `setInterval` callbacks whose threshold has expired. |
| 2 | **Pending Callbacks** | Runs I/O callbacks deferred from the previous iteration. |
| 3 | **Idle, Prepare** | Internal libuv use; not user-accessible. |
| 4 | **Poll** | Retrieves new I/O events; executes their callbacks. This is where most application code runs. |
| 5 | **Check** | Executes `setImmediate` callbacks. |
| 6 | **Close Callbacks** | Runs `close` event handlers (e.g., `socket.on('close')`). |

**Additional Priority Queues (1 mark):**
- **`process.nextTick()`** callbacks run **after the current operation**, **before** the Event Loop continues.
- **Promise microtasks** run after nextTick, before Event Loop phases.

**Execution Order Formula (1 mark):**
$$\text{Sync} \rightarrow \text{nextTick} \rightarrow \text{Microtasks} \rightarrow \text{Timers} \rightarrow \text{Poll} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Close} \rightarrow \text{(repeat)}$$

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** What is **Express.js**? Explain its advantages over the plain Node.js `http` module. Demonstrate the creation of a basic Express server with proper routing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **middleware** in Express.js. Explain the different **types of middleware** (application-level, router-level, built-in, third-party, error-handling) with suitable code examples. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Express.js & Its Advantages (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Express.js is a minimal, fast, and flexible Node.js web framework that provides robust features for building web applications and RESTful APIs.

**Advantages over plain `http` module (3 marks):**
1. **Simplified routing** with `app.get()`, `app.post()`, etc.
2. **Middleware pipeline** for request processing.
3. **Built-in body parsing** via `express.json()` and `express.urlencoded()`.
4. **Static file serving** via `express.static()`.
5. **Centralized error handling** using 4-argument middleware.
6. **Better code organization** with `express.Router()`.

**Basic Server Code (3 marks):**

```javascript
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const PORT = 3000;

app.use(express.json());

app.get('/', (req, res) => res.send('Home Page'));

app.get('/about', (req, res) => res.send('About Page'));

app.get('/users/:id', (req, res) => {
  res.json({ userId: req.params.id, name: 'Sample User' });
});

app.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Server on port ${PORT}`));
```

---

**(b) Middleware in Express.js (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Middleware is a function that has access to `req`, `res`, and the `next` function. It can execute code, modify request/response, end the cycle, or call the next middleware.

**Types of Middleware (6 marks — 1.2 each):**

**1. Application-level Middleware:**
```javascript
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  console.log('Time:', Date.now());
  next();
});
```

**2. Router-level Middleware:**
```javascript
const router = express.Router();
router.use((req, res, next) => {
  console.log('Router middleware');
  next();
});
```

**3. Built-in Middleware:**
```javascript
app.use(express.json());
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true }));
app.use(express.static('public'));
```

**4. Third-party Middleware:**
```javascript
const morgan = require('morgan');
const cors = require('cors');
app.use(morgan('dev'));
app.use(cors());
```

**5. Error-handling Middleware:**
```javascript
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  console.error(err.stack);
  res.status(500).send('Something broke!');
});
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 4-argument signature** for error-handling middleware. Express identifies it by **arity** (function length), NOT by the parameter name. `app.use((req, res, next) => {...})` will **NEVER** be treated as an error handler.
> 2. **Forgetting to call `next()`** in middleware — the request will hang and timeout, costing 2–3 marks in tracing/output questions.
> 3. **Confusing `next('route')` vs `next(err)`** — `next('route')` skips to the next matching route in the same router; `next(err)` jumps directly to error-handling middleware.
> 4. **Mixing up `res.send()` and `res.json()`** — `res.send()` infers Content-Type; `res.json()` forces `application/json` and stringifies the object.
> 5. **Reassigning `exports`** instead of mutating it — silently breaks CommonJS module exports.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **Node.js is a runtime, not a framework or language.** JavaScript is the language; Node.js is the runtime built on **V8 + libuv**.
- 🔁 **Event Loop = 6 phases** (Timers → Pending → Idle/Prepare → Poll → Check → Close), executing in a **strict, repeating cycle**.
- 📋 **Execution Priority:** `Sync → process.nextTick → Promise microtasks → Event Loop phases`.
- 🧵 **Single-threaded main thread** for JS execution; **libuv thread pool (default 4)** for heavy I/O; **`worker_threads`** for CPU-bound work.
- 📦 **`npm`** is the standard package manager; **`package.json`** is the project manifest.
- 🧩 **CommonJS** uses `require` / `module.exports`; **ES Modules** use `import` / `export`.
- 🚂 **Express** is a *minimal* framework on top of Node's `http` module, adding **routing**, **middleware**, and **utility methods**.
- 🛠️ **Middleware** = `(req, res, next)` for normal; `(err, req, res, next)` for error handling. **Arity is the discriminator**.
- 🛣️ **Route parameters** → `req.params`; **Query strings** → `req.query`; **Request body** → `req.body` (requires `express.json()`).
- 🔌 **`express.Router()`** enables **modular, mountable route handlers** — use it for clean architecture.
- ❌ **Error handling** is centralized via the 4-argument middleware; always call `next(err)` from async error points.
- 🌍 **Industry context:** Node.js + Express powers the **MERN/MEAN stack**, used by Netflix, LinkedIn, Uber, and PayPal.
- 📚 **Key npm commands** to memorize: `npm init`, `npm install`, `npm install -g`, `npm install --save-dev`, `npm start`, `npm run`.
- 🧪 **Tracing question mantra:** Synchronous first → nextTick → microtasks → Timers → Check.
- 🏷️ **HTTP status codes to remember:** `200` (OK), `201` (Created), `204` (No Content), `400` (Bad Request), `404` (Not Found), `500` (Server Error).
- 💡 **The single most-tested concept:** The order of execution of `setTimeout`, `setImmediate`, `process.nextTick`, and `Promise.then` in a given snippet. Master this for sure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rendering HTML: React - ReactJS Foundations: The Philosophy of React, What Is a Component, Built-in Components, User-Defined Components - Types of Components, Function Components, Differences Between Function and Class Components # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of React **React** is a declarative, component-based, open-source JavaScript library developed by **Meta (Facebook)** in 2013, used for building user interfaces (UIs), particularly for single-page applications (SPAs). It is officially described by the React Team as *"a JavaScript library for building user interfaces"* and uses a **Virtual DOM** to efficiently update and render only the components whose state has changed. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3** > React is **NOT a framework**. It is a *library* focused purely on the *view layer* (the "V" in MVC). It does not dictate routing, HTTP, or state management — those are handled by companion libraries (e.g., React Router, Axios, Redux). ## 1.2 The Philosophy of React The philosophy of React can be summarized in **Four Pillars** that every KTU student must memorize: | # | Pillar | Meaning | |---|--------|---------| | 1 | **Declarative** | You describe *what* the UI should look like for a given state; React figures out *how* to update the DOM. | | 2 | **Component-Based** | UIs are built by composing small, isolated, reusable pieces called *components*. | | 3 | **Learn Once, Write Anywhere** | The same React mental model applies to web, mobile (React Native), VR, and server-side rendering. | | 4 | **Unidirectional Data Flow** | Data in React flows in one direction: from parent components down to child components via *props*. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Declarative vs Imperative — The Heart of React's Philosophy** > - *Imperative* (vanilla JS): You manually write `document.getElementById(...).innerHTML = "..."` and explicitly tell the browser *every step*. > - *Declarative* (React): You simply write `return

Hello

` and React *reconciles* the UI with the desired state automatically. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "React is a Restaurant Waiter" Imagine you are at a restaurant: - **You (the customer)** = The Component describing the desired UI state. - **The Waiter (React)** = Takes your order (your JSX) and goes to the kitchen. - **The Kitchen (React's Reconciliation Engine + DOM)** = Cooks only what has changed on your plate. - **The Final Plate (the rendered HTML on your screen)** = The actual visible output. You don't walk into the kitchen to cook your food — you just *declare* what you want. React works the same way: you *declare* the UI, and React *imperatively* (and efficiently) updates the DOM for you. ## 1.4 What Is a Component? A **Component** is the *fundamental building block* of a React application. It is a **self-contained, reusable, independent piece of UI** that can be thought of as a *custom, encapsulated HTML element* which also carries its own logic and styling. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Board-Exam Standard)** > A React component is a **JavaScript function or class** that optionally accepts inputs (called *props*) and returns a **React element** describing what should appear on the screen. The conceptual equation is: $$ \text{Component} \;=\; f(\text{props},\ \text{state}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{React Element (JSX)} $$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet ## 2.1 Breakdown of the React Component Model The React component model operates on a structured, layered pipeline: - **Step 1 — Define:** A developer writes a component as a JavaScript function or class. - **Step 2 — Invoke:** The component is rendered (either by another component or by `ReactDOM.createRoot().render()`). - **Step 3 — Accept Inputs:** The component receives `props` (properties) — read-only data passed from the parent. - **Step 4 — Maintain State:** A component may maintain internal `state` — mutable data that drives re-renders. - **Step 5 — Return JSX:** The component returns a **JSX (JavaScript XML)** expression, which is syntactic sugar for `React.createElement(...)` calls. - **Step 6 — Render:** React's *reconciler* diffs the new virtual DOM with the previous one and applies minimal changes to the *real* DOM. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Core "Why" — Why does React use Components?** > Components promote **reusability**, **separation of concerns**, **testability**, and **composability**. Instead of one giant HTML file, a React app is a *tree* of small, manageable UI blocks. ## 2.2 Classification of Components — The Master Taxonomy React components are classified into **two broad categories**: ### A. Built-in Components Built-in components are the **primitive elements provided by React** itself. They map directly to standard HTML tags but are written in **lower-case** (camelCase) to distinguish them from user-defined ones. The most common built-in components include: | Built-in Component | Equivalent HTML | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `
` | `
` | Generic container | | `` | `` | Inline container | | `

` ... `

` | Same | Headings | | `

` | Same | Paragraph | | `` | Same | Image | | `` | Same | Anchor/Hyperlink | | `` | Same | Form input | | `

); } ``` **Line-by-Line Analytical Breakdown:** - **Line 1:** Imports the `useState` Hook from the `react` package. - **Line 3:** Declares a function component named `Counter`. Note the **PascalCase** convention. - **Line 4:** Calls `useState(0)` to declare a state variable. The `0` is the **initial value**. The Hook returns a **2-element array** that is destructured: `count` (current value) and `setCount` (updater function). - **Line 6:** The function **directly returns JSX** — no `render()` method, no `return` from inside a class method. - **Line 9:** The button's `onClick` handler calls `setCount(count + 1)`. React **schedules a re-render**. The new `count` value is available on the next render. ### 3.2.2 Class Component (Legacy) ```jsx import React from 'react'; class Counter extends React.Component { constructor(props) { super(props); this.state = { count: 0 }; this.handleClick = this.handleClick.bind(this); } handleClick() { this.setState({ count: this.state.count + 1 }); } render() { return (

Count: {this.state.count}

); } } ``` **Line-by-Line Analytical Breakdown:** - **Line 1:** `React` must be explicitly imported (in the classic JSX transform). - **Line 3:** Declares a class that **extends `React.Component`**. Inheritance is mandatory because `React.Component` provides the `setState`, `forceUpdate`, and lifecycle machinery. - **Lines 4–8:** The **constructor** receives `props` and **must call `super(props)`** before using `this`. This is a hard rule of ES6 classes when extending another class. - **Line 6:** Initializes the state object. State **must be an object** in class components (unlike Hooks, which can be any type). - **Line 7:** **Manual binding** of the event handler. Without this line, `this` inside `handleClick` would be `undefined` when the button is clicked — a notorious source of bugs. - **Line 10:** Event handler. Calls `this.setState({...})` to schedule a re-render. **Never** mutate `this.state` directly (e.g., `this.state.count = 5`). - **Lines 14–23:** The **mandatory `render()` method** returns JSX. ### 3.2.3 Side-by-Side Concept Matrix | Concept | Function Component | Class Component | |---|---|---| | Import statement | `import { useState } from 'react'` | `import React from 'react'` | | Declaration | `function Counter() { ... }` | `class Counter extends React.Component { ... }` | | State declaration | `const [count, setCount] = useState(0);` | `this.state = { count: 0 }` | | State update | `setCount(count + 1)` | `this.setState({ count: this.state.count + 1 })` | | Event binding | Lexical scope — no binding needed | Manual `.bind(this)` in constructor | | Output | Direct JSX return | JSX inside `render()` method | | Total lines (approx.) | **~10** | **~22** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Insight — "Why do Function Components have less code?"** > Because there is **no class instantiation, no `this` binding, no `render()` boilerplate, and no constructor**. Hooks consolidate state + lifecycle logic into simple function calls. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level React Component Architecture (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **hierarchical tree structure** of a typical React application, where built-in and user-defined components coexist. ```mermaid graph TD A[App Root Component]:::user B[Header]:::user C[NavBar]:::user D[MainContent]:::user E[ArticleList]:::user F[ArticleCard]:::user G[Footer]:::user H[div built in]:::builtin I[img built in]:::builtin J[h1 built in]:::builtin K[button built in]:::builtin L[p built in]:::builtin A --> B A --> D A --> G B --> C B --> H B --> I B --> J D --> E E --> F F --> H F --> J F --> K F --> L G --> H G --> L classDef user fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef builtin fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - **Blue nodes** (PascalCase) = **User-defined components** (custom React components). - **Yellow nodes** (lowercase) = **Built-in components** (native HTML elements). - **Arrows** = Parent-to-child composition (the *unidirectional data flow*). ## 4.2 React Rendering Pipeline (Sequential Flow) This diagram shows the **end-to-end rendering flow** that was derived in Section 3.1. ```mermaid graph LR A1[JSX Source Code]:::stage A2[Babel Transpiler]:::stage A3[React createElement Call]:::stage A4[React Element JS Object]:::stage A5[Virtual DOM Tree]:::stage A6[Reconciler Diff Algorithm]:::stage A7[Mutations Patch List]:::stage A8[Real DOM in Browser]:::stage A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 classDef stage fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Function vs Class Component — Internal Structure This block diagram compares the **internal anatomy** of both component types. ```mermaid graph TD subgraph FN["FUNCTION COMPONENT ANATOMY"] F1[Function Name PascalCase]:::fn F2[Props Argument]:::fn F3[useState Hook]:::fn F4[useEffect Hook Optional]:::fn F5[Direct JSX Return]:::fn F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> F5 end subgraph CL["CLASS COMPONENT ANATOMY"] C1[Class Name extends React.Component]:::cls C2[Constructor super props]:::cls C3[this.state Object]:::cls C4[Event Handler with bind]:::cls C5[Lifecycle Methods]:::cls C6[Mandatory render Method]:::cls C7[JSX Return from render]:::cls C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6 --> C7 end classDef fn fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cls fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Key Observation from the Diagram:** - The function component has a **linear, simple structure** — 5 elements. - The class component has a **layered, complex structure** — 7 elements with a mandatory `render()` method. ## 4.4 Component Composition — Parent-Child Data Flow ```mermaid graph TD P[Parent Component App]:::parent CH[Child Component Welcome]:::child DOM[Real DOM h1 element]:::dom P -- props name=KTU --> CH CH -- returns JSX --> DOM classDef parent fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef child fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dom fill:#fde68a,stroke:#78350f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip — Direction of Arrows** > In React data-flow diagrams, the arrow **always goes from parent to child** for *props*. State updates are triggered inside the child (e.g., via a button click), but the *state lives* in whichever component declared it. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define a React component. List any two built-in components of React.** **Model Answer:** A React component is a reusable, self-contained piece of user interface defined as a JavaScript function or class that accepts inputs called **props** and returns a **React element** (JSX) describing what should appear on the screen. Two built-in components of React are: 1. **`
`** — a generic container, equivalent to the HTML `
` tag. 2. **``** — a special React component that groups multiple children without adding an extra node to the DOM. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two built-in components = 3 marks total)* --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State any three differences between function components and class components in React.** **Model Answer:** | # | Function Component | Class Component | |---|---|---| | 1 | Declared as a plain JavaScript function using `function` keyword or arrow syntax. | Declared as an ES6 class that **extends `React.Component`**. | | 2 | State is managed using **Hooks** (e.g., `useState`). | State is stored in a **`this.state`** object and updated via **`this.setState()`**. | | 3 | Does **not** use the `this` keyword. | Uses the `this` keyword extensively, often requiring manual binding of event handlers. | *(1 mark per difference = 3 marks total)* --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **KTU ESE Internal Choice Format (2024 Scheme)** > Each question has two parts — (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024] **(a)** Explain the **philosophy of React** in detail. Discuss any **four key principles** that guide React's design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **classification of React components**. Differentiate between **built-in** and **user-defined** components. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Philosophy of React — 7 Marks** React's design philosophy is built on four foundational principles: 1. **Declarative Paradigm (2 marks):** React follows a declarative approach where developers describe the *desired state* of the UI, and React handles the underlying DOM manipulations. This contrasts with imperative programming where the developer manually writes every DOM operation. Example: writing `

Hello

` is declarative, while `document.getElementById('h1').innerText = 'Hello'` is imperative. 2. **Component-Based Architecture (2 marks):** React encourages breaking the UI into small, isolated, reusable pieces called *components*. Each component encapsulates its own structure, logic, and styling. This promotes modularity, easier debugging, parallel team development, and code reuse. 3. **Unidirectional (One-Way) Data Flow (2 marks):** Data in React flows in a single direction — from parent components to child components via *props*. This makes the data flow predictable and easier to trace compared to two-way binding frameworks like AngularJS. 4. **Learn Once, Write Anywhere (1 mark):** React's component mental model is portable across web (ReactJS), mobile (React Native), server-side rendering (Next.js), and even desktop apps (Electron + React). Developers learn one paradigm and apply it everywhere. **(b) Classification of React Components — 7 Marks** React components are classified into two main categories: 1. **Built-in Components (3 marks):** These are the primitive elements that React provides out-of-the-box, directly mirroring standard HTML tags.
