topics.title: SPA - Basics, AngularJS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** SPA - Basics, AngularJS # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Single Page Application (SPA)? A **Single Page Application (SPA)** is a web application or website that interacts with the user by dynamically rewriting the current web page with new data fetched from the web server, instead of the default method of the browser loading entire new pages from the server. The page does not reload at any point in the process, nor does control transfer to another page. (KTU 2024 Syllabus — PECST742 Module 4) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition (Verbatim-grade):** "A Single Page Application is a web application that fits on a single web page with the goal of providing a more fluid user experience analogous to a desktop application. It avoids the interruption of loading a completely new page, achieving fluidity through AJAX and HTML5." ### Conceptual Analogy — The Television Remote Analogy Imagine you are watching a TV connected to a streaming service like **Netflix**. - The TV screen **never goes black** or reloads. - When you press a button to switch from one movie to another, the **content changes instantly**, but the *same* screen, the *same* remote, and the *same* TV remain in place. - Internally, the TV is fetching new video data packets in the background, but from your perspective, the page (screen) never refreshed. **A SPA behaves exactly like this TV:** the browser never performs a full refresh; JavaScript quietly fetches JSON data from the server in the background and rewrites the relevant portions of the DOM (Document Object Model). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Trade-off of SPAs:** SPAs trade higher initial load size for faster, near-instant subsequent interactions. The first load is heavier because the entire application shell (HTML + CSS + JavaScript bundle) is downloaded upfront. --- ## 1.2 What is AngularJS? **AngularJS** is an open-source, JavaScript-based, front-end web application framework maintained by **Google** and a community of individual developers and corporations. It is written entirely in **JavaScript** and aims to simplify both the development and the testing of such applications by providing a framework for client-side **Model–View–Controller (MVC)** and **Model–View–ViewModel (MVVM)** architectures, along with components commonly used in rich internet applications. (Released: 2010, by Miško Hevery) > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** AngularJS (**version 1.x**, JavaScript-based) is **NOT** the same as Angular (**version 2+**, TypeScript-based). The KTU 2024 PECST742 syllabus explicitly names **AngularJS**, meaning the student must answer using the JavaScript-based 1.x API — directives like `ng-model`, `ng-app`, and `ng-controller` (not `[(ngModel)]` square brackets from Angular 2+). ### Conceptual Analogy — The Theatre Director Analogy Think of AngularJS as a **theatre director** standing between the **actors (JavaScript data)** and the **stage (HTML view)**. - The director has a **scriptbook (Scope)** where he notes what each actor is supposed to do. - If a phone call comes in (a controller updates a variable), the director **instantly** signals the actor and the stage to update their performance together — no shouting, no re-running the entire play. - This is **two-way data binding**: any change in the model reflects in the view, and any change in the view reflects in the model, all orchestrated by AngularJS. --- ## 1.3 Why SPA + AngularJS? — Syllabus Highlights > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Module 4 High-Yield Points** > 1. SPAs reduce server load (only data is exchanged, not full markup). > 2. SPAs provide better user experience (no flicker, no white flash). > 3. AngularJS extends HTML vocabulary using **Directives** like `ng-app`, `ng-model`, `ng-bind`, `ng-repeat`, `ng-controller`, `ng-click`, `ng-show`, `ng-hide`, `ng-if`, `ng-src`, `ng-href`. > 4. AngularJS provides **two-way data binding**, **dependency injection**, **routing**, **filters**, **services**, and **modules** out of the box. > 5. Standard SPA frameworks include **AngularJS, React, Vue.js, Ember.js, Backbone.js**. --- ## 1.4 Real-World Examples of SPAs (with framework) | Application | Underlying SPA Framework | |---|---| | Gmail | Internal Google closure + AngularJS (early versions) | | Google Maps | Custom JS SPA architecture | | Facebook (news feed) | React + Relay | | Twitter (X) | React | | PayPal (my account) | AngularJS | | LinkedIn Mobile Web | React + GraphQL | # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Traditional (Multi-Page) Application vs. SPA | Aspect | Multi-Page Application (MPA) | Single Page Application (SPA) | |---|---|---| | Page reload on every request | **Yes** (full HTTP round-trip) | **No** (only data via AJAX) | | Server load | **High** (renders HTML server-side each time) | **Low** (serves JSON/data only) | | Initial load time | **Faster** (one small page) | **Slower** (entire shell downloaded) | | Subsequent navigation | **Slow** (network + parsing) | **Near-instant** (client-side routing) | | SEO friendliness | **Excellent** by default | Requires extra effort (SSR / Prerender) | | Backed by | Server-side templating (JSP, PHP, Django) | Client-side rendering (React/Angular/Vue) | | URL handling | Real file paths | **Hash (`#/route`)** or **HTML5 History API** | | State management | Server session / cookies | Client-side state (in-memory) | | Examples | Wikipedia, news portals | Gmail, Trello, Google Docs | --- ## 2.2 SPA — Core Operational Principles The SPA lifecycle can be broken into the following **sequential logic steps**: 1. **Initial Bootstrapping** — The user types a URL. The browser downloads a single `index.html` shell along with all bundled CSS and JavaScript files. 2. **Framework Initialization** — A SPA framework (e.g., AngularJS) boots up, scans the DOM, and registers listeners. 3. **Route Resolution** — The framework examines the URL fragment and determines which "view" (component/template) to display. 4. **Data Fetching (AJAX / Fetch / Axios)** — Instead of reloading the page, an asynchronous HTTP call (`XMLHttpRequest` or `fetch()`) is made to a REST API endpoint. 5. **JSON Response Parsing** — The server returns a lightweight **JSON** payload (no HTML markup). 6. **DOM Patching** — The framework updates only the relevant portions of the in-memory DOM tree. 7. **History API Update** — `window.history.pushState()` is invoked so the URL bar reflects the current logical page (enables Back/Forward). 8. **User Interaction Loop** — The user clicks a link/button → step 3 repeats (no full page reload). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why `pushState` matters:** Without the HTML5 History API, the user pressing the browser's "Back" button would *exit* the SPA. `pushState` injects a synthetic entry into the browser history stack, mimicking true page navigation. --- ## 2.3 AngularJS — Architectural Building Blocks | # | Building Block | Purpose | KTU-Exam Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **`ng-app`** | Auto-bootstraps the AngularJS application on a DOM element | **Very High** | | 2 | **`ng-controller`** | Attaches a controller class to a DOM subtree | **Very High** | | 3 | **`ng-model`** | Two-way binds an input/select/textarea to a scope variable | **Very High** | | 4 | **`ng-bind`** | One-way binds a scope variable to innerHTML of an element | **High** | | 5 | **`ng-init`** | Initializes scope variables inline | **Medium** | | 6 | **`ng-repeat`** | Iterates over a collection and renders a template per item | **Very High** | | 7 | **`ng-click / ng-dblclick / ng-mouseenter`** | Binds DOM events to scope functions | **High** | | 8 | **`ng-show / ng-hide / ng-if`** | Conditional rendering by toggling `display` or removing DOM | **High** | | 9 | **`ng-src / ng-href`** | Defers loading of `src`/`href` until binding is evaluated | **Medium** | | 10 | **`ng-class / ng-style`** | Dynamically applies CSS classes / inline styles | **Medium** | | 11 | **`{{ expression }}`** | Interpolation syntax (one-way binding to text) | **Very High** | | 12 | **`$scope`** | The glue object connecting View and Controller | **Very High** | | 13 | **Module (`angular.module`)** | Container for controllers, services, directives | **High** | | 14 | **Filters (`\| currency, \| uppercase, \| filter`)** | Transform data displayed in the view | **High** | | 15 | **Services / Factories** | Reusable singleton business logic | **High** | | 16 | **Routing (`$routeProvider`)** | Maps URL → Template + Controller | **Very High** | | 17 | **Dependency Injection (DI)** | Components declare their dependencies; injector resolves them | **Medium** | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — AngularJS Mnemonics | Mnemonic / Symbol | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | `{{ }}` | Interpolation (one-way) | `{{ student.name }}` | | `ng-model` | Two-way data binding | `` | | `ng-bind` | One-way binding to innerHTML | `` | | `ng-repeat` | Loop directive | `ng-repeat="x in items"` | | `ng-click` | Click event binding | `ng-click="save()"` | | `ng-if` | Conditional render (removes DOM) | `ng-if="isLoggedIn"` | | `ng-show` | Show if truthy (CSS toggle) | `ng-show="count > 0"` | | `ng-hide` | Hide if truthy (CSS toggle) | `ng-hide="isLoading"` | | `ng-class` | Dynamic CSS class | `ng-class="{active: isActive}"` | | `ng-init` | Inline initialization | `ng-init="count=0"` | | `\| filter` | Built-in filter pipe | `{{ list \| filter:query }}` | | `\:number` | Number format filter | `{{ price | number:2 }}` | | `\$scope` | ViewModel glue object | `$scope.title = "KTU";` | | `\$routeProvider` | Routing configuration | `.when('/home', { templateUrl: ... })` | | `module()` | Module getter/creator | `angular.module('app', [])` | | `controller()` | Registers a controller | `.controller('Ctrl', function($scope){...})` | | `service()` / `factory()` | Registers a service | `.factory('DataSvc', function(){...})` | | `directive()` | Custom directive | `.directive('myDir', function(){...})` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion note for table cells:** In the table above, the filter symbol `\|` was escaped as a backslash only for the cheat-sheet rendering, but in **actual AngularJS code**, you write a single unescaped pipe, e.g., `{{ price | currency }}` (this is **filter chaining syntax**). --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **E-commerce:** Amazon-style product catalogues — instant filter, sort, and pagination without flicker. - **Project Management Tools:** Trello, Asana, Jira — drag-and-drop with optimistic UI updates. - **Dashboards:** Grafana, Kibana — real-time charts driven by WebSocket data within the same shell. - **Code Editors in Browser:** VS Code Web, Replit — heavy client-side logic + state preserved across "page" transitions. - **AngularJS Specific Use Cases:** Legacy enterprise apps (PayPal's checkout, DoubleClick advertising console, Lego.com's commerce UI during 2014–2020) — AngularJS 1.x is still maintained in **Long-Term Support** for these deployments. --- ## 2.6 Advantages & Disadvantages of SPAs (MUST-KNOW for KTU 2-marker) ### Advantages 1. **Rich, fluid user experience** — no white-flash between screens. 2. **Reduced server load** — only JSON data crosses the network after initial load. 3. **Decoupled front-end and back-end** — back-end can serve multiple clients (web, mobile, IoT). 4. **Offline support** — service workers can cache the shell. 5. **Easier to build mobile wrappers** — the same SPA can be wrapped by Cordova / Capacitor. ### Disadvantages 1. **SEO challenges** — search crawlers may not execute JS (mitigated by SSR / Prerender). 2. **Initial load is heavy** — entire bundle must download first. 3. **Memory leaks** — long-lived SPAs accumulate state in the browser. 4. **JavaScript required** — users with JS disabled see nothing. 5. **Security** — XSS attacks are more impactful since everything is rendered client-side. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Context Note:** PECST742 is a **Web Programming** course. Code snippets are the *primary examination artefact* and are **MANDATORY** here (the "no-code" rule applies only to non-CS subjects). All code below is exhaustive and runnable. --- ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — The AngularJS Bootstrapping Sequence The following is the **algorithmic derivation** of how AngularJS turns static HTML into a reactive SPA, broken into discrete evaluable steps: ### Step 1 — Document Load The browser parses the raw HTML and constructs the initial DOM. At this moment, no AngularJS has run yet; `{{ name }}` would literally display the curly braces. ### Step 2 — Script Tag Encounter When the browser encounters ``, it fetches and executes the AngularJS library. The global `angular` object is registered on `window`. ### Step 3 — `DOMContentLoaded` Event AngularJS listens for this event (or runs as soon as it sees `ng-app` if the script is at the bottom of the body). It scans the DOM for the **`ng-app`** directive. ### Step 4 — Module Resolution The string passed to `ng-app` (e.g., `ng-app="myApp"`) is looked up via `angular.module("myApp")`. If it returns a registered module, AngularJS uses that as the root injector. If it returns an *undefined* module, AngularJS throws `Error: [$injector:modulerr]`. ### Step 5 — `$compile` Service Invocation AngularJS traverses the entire subtree of the element marked by `ng-app`. For every directive it encounters (`ng-model`, `ng-repeat`, `ng-click`, custom directives), it calls the corresponding **compile function** once, then the **link function** per instance. ### Step 6 — `$scope` Creation For each element bearing `ng-controller`, AngularJS instantiates the controller, injects `$scope` (a new child of `$rootScope`), and executes the controller function. ### Step 7 — `$digest` Cycle Activation After linking, a **dirty-checking loop** runs. Every registered `watch` listener (auto-created by `ng-model`, `{{ }}`, etc.) is invoked. If a watched expression's value has changed, the corresponding DOM element is updated. The loop repeats until no changes are detected (max **10 iterations**, after which a "digest iterations limit reached" error fires). ### Step 8 — Event Listeners Attached `ng-click`, `ng-change`, etc., register native DOM event listeners. When the user interacts, the controller function runs, modifies `$scope`, and AngularJS automatically triggers a new `$digest` cycle to push the changes to the view (this is **two-way data binding**). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — "Hello, AngularJS!" (Exhaustive Walkthrough) **Problem statement:** Create a minimal AngularJS SPA that displays a name entered by the user in real-time. ### Step-by-Step Construction **Step A — HTML skeleton (`index.html`):** ```html Hello AngularJS

Welcome to AngularJS

Hello, ! Welcome to KTU Web Programming.

Course: {{ course }} | Year: {{ year }}

``` ### Step-by-Step Execution Logic 1. `angular.min.js` loads → `window.angular` is defined. 2. The browser parses HTML, sees `ng-app="helloApp"`. 3. AngularJS calls `angular.module('helloApp', [])` — this **defines** a new module (the empty `[]` array means dependencies). The `app` variable is held in memory. 4. `app.controller('MessageController', ...)` registers a controller factory. 5. The DOM is compiled: the `` with `ng-model="name"` creates a watcher on `$rootScope.name` (undefined initially → `""`). 6. The `` registers a one-way watcher. 7. The `MessageController` is instantiated when the `
` is compiled. It assigns four properties to its local `$scope` (a child of `$rootScope`). 8. The `{{ clickCount }}` interpolation shows `0` initially. 9. The user types "Alice" into the input: - The `input` event fires. - `ng-model`'s listener updates `name` on `$rootScope`. - The `$digest` loop runs: detects `name` changed → updates ``. - The user sees "Hello, Alice!" in real-time **without any page reload**. 10. The user clicks the button: - `ng-click` calls `$scope.increment()`. - `clickCount` goes from 0 → 1. - The `$digest` loop detects the change and rewrites the button text to "Clicked 1 times". This single static HTML file behaves like a complete SPA — every interaction stays on the same page. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — `ng-repeat` with Filtering (Exhaustive) **Problem statement:** Display a list of KTU students in a table with a search filter. ```html

KTU Student Directory

Roll No Name Branch
{{ s.roll }} {{ s.name }} {{ s.branch }}

Showing {{ (students | filter: query).length }} matching record(s).

``` ### Step-by-Step Logic Trace - The `students` array is bound to `$scope`. - The expression `students | filter: query` invokes AngularJS's built-in **`filter`** filter — it returns a new array containing only items where any property loosely matches `query`. - The `ng-repeat` directive iterates this filtered array and clones a `` for each item, replacing `s.roll`, `s.name`, `s.branch` with the current item's properties. - As the user types, `$scope.query` changes → `$digest` cycle → filter re-runs → DOM is re-rendered. - `(students | filter: query).length` re-evaluates on every digest to show the count. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Routing with `ngRoute` (Exhaustive) **Problem statement:** Build a 3-view SPA (Home / About / Contact) with the URL bar updating. **Step 1 — Include the routing module:** ```html ``` **Step 2 — Declare `ngRoute` as a dependency:** ```javascript var app = angular.module('spaApp', ['ngRoute']); ``` **Step 3 — Configure routes:** ```javascript app.config(function($routeProvider) { $routeProvider .when('/', { templateUrl: 'views/home.html', controller: 'HomeController' }) .when('/about', { templateUrl: 'views/about.html', controller: 'AboutController' }) .when('/contact', { templateUrl: 'views/contact.html', controller: 'ContactController' }) .otherwise({ redirectTo: '/' }); }); ``` **Step 4 — Inject the `ng-view` placeholder in the main shell:** ```html
Home | About | Contact
``` ### Step-by-Step Logic Trace 1. User clicks ``. 2. The hash in the URL changes to `#/about`. **No HTTP request** is made (because it's a fragment identifier). 3. AngularJS's `$location` service detects the hash change. 4. `$route` service looks up `/about` in the configured routes. 5. It asynchronously fetches `views/about.html` via `$http`. 6. The fetched HTML is compiled and injected into `
`. 7. The `AboutController` is instantiated, populating the new view's `$scope`. 8. The URL bar shows `index.html#/about`, the user perceives a "page change" — yet the browser never reloaded. # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — High-Level SPA Request/Response Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD user1[User clicks a link or button] browser1[Browser intercepts event via SPA framework router] ajax1[AJAX / fetch call to REST API endpoint] server1[Backend server processes request] json1[Returns lightweight JSON payload only] dom1[Framework updates relevant DOM nodes] history1[History API pushState updates URL bar] view1[User sees new content instantly - no reload] user1 --> browser1 browser1 --> ajax1 ajax1 --> server1 server1 --> json1 json1 --> dom1 dom1 --> history1 history1 --> view1 view1 -.loop.-> user1 ``` --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — AngularJS MVC / MVVM Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph V[View - HTML Template] tpl1[div ng-controller] tpl2[input ng-model name] tpl3[h1 ng-bind title or curly brace title curly brace] tpl1 --> tpl2 tpl1 --> tpl3 end subgraph VM[ViewModel - Scope] sc1[$scope name] sc2[$scope title] sc3[$scope methods] end subgraph M[Model - Plain JS objects or service] m1[student object with name, roll, branch] m2[array of students] end subgraph C[Controller] ctrl1[function with $scope injected] ctrl2[defines data and behaviour] end V -- ng-model writes to scope --> VM VM -- $digest pushes back to DOM --> V C -- initialises --> VM VM -- binds to --> M M -- $http loads --> VM ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The AngularJS `$digest` Cycle (Two-Way Binding) ```mermaid flowchart TD start([User types in input or clicks button]) evt[Native DOM event fires] listen[AngularJS event listener captures it] mutate[Scope variable is mutated by ng-model or controller function] digest[$digest cycle begins] watch{Any $watch listener reports dirty} update[DOM is updated in-place] loop[Repeat until all watchers clean or 10 iterations] done([UI is in sync with model]) start --> evt --> listen --> mutate --> digest --> watch watch -- Yes --> update --> loop --> digest watch -- No --> done ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — AngularJS Module Container Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB module[angular.module name myApp dependencies ngRoute] module --> ctrl1[controller HomeController] module --> ctrl2[controller AboutController] module --> svc1[service AuthService] module --> svc2[factory DataService] module --> dir1[directive customValidator] module --> filt1[filter capitalizeCustom] module --> cfg[config $routeProvider setup] module --> run[run block - global initialization] ``` --- ## 4.5 Diagram 5 — SPA vs MPA Network Traffic Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MPA[Multi-Page Application - 3 navigations] m1[Request 1: GET page1.html - 80KB] m2[Request 2: GET page2.html - 75KB] m3[Request 3: GET page3.html - 90KB] m1 --> m2 --> m3 end subgraph SPA[Single Page Application - 3 navigations] s1[Request 1: GET index.html + bundle.js - 250KB] s2[Request 2: GET api/data1 - 4KB JSON] s3[Request 3: GET api/data2 - 3KB JSON] s1 --> s2 --> s3 end ``` # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define Single Page Application (SPA). List any four advantages.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Single Page Application (SPA)** is a web application that loads a single HTML document and dynamically updates that page as the user interacts with the application, using AJAX and client-side rendering instead of full-page reloads. **Four advantages:** 1. **Better user experience** — no page flicker or white flash during navigation. 2. **Reduced server load** — server sends only JSON data, not entire HTML markup, after the initial load. 3. **Faster subsequent navigations** — once the shell is loaded, view changes occur instantly because they are rendered client-side. 4. **Clear front-end / back-end separation** — back-end becomes a pure REST API, reusable by mobile apps and other clients. *(Examiner's note: a clean one-sentence definition earns 1 mark; four well-explained advantages earn 0.5 each = 2 marks.)* --- ### **Q2. What is AngularJS? Explain the role of `ng-app` and `ng-model` directives.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **AngularJS** is an open-source JavaScript framework developed by Google that extends HTML vocabulary to build dynamic, single-page web applications using the MVC/MVVM pattern. - **`ng-app`** — This is the **root directive** that auto-bootstraps an AngularJS application. Placed on the ``, ``, or any container element, it tells AngularJS: *"Treat this subtree as an AngularJS-managed module."* The value (e.g., `ng-app="myApp"`) refers to the module name registered via `angular.module('myApp', [])`. - **`ng-model`** — This directive implements **two-way data binding** between an input element (``, `

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Real-World Web Software Design?

**Real-world web software design** is the disciplined engineering process of translating abstract user requirements (features, business rules, performance goals) into a concrete, layered, testable, and maintainable web application architecture. Unlike toy or academic examples, real-world web design must address **scalability, security, concurrency, maintainability, deployment, and continuous evolution**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), the syllabus frames this as a problem of *architectural decision-making* — choosing the right **layers, patterns, and testing strategy** to ensure the system remains correct, performant, and evolvable over its lifetime.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Web Application Design is the systematic application of software engineering principles — modularity, layering, design patterns, and quality assurance — to the construction of distributed, HTTP-based, multi-tier applications.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy: Building a Restaurant

Imagine you are opening a **restaurant** (your web app):

| Restaurant Element | Web Application Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Dining Hall & Menu | **Presentation Layer** (HTML, CSS, UI) |
| Waiter taking orders | **Controller / Router** (Spring, Express, Django) |
| Chef following recipes | **Business Logic Layer** (Services, Domain Models) |
| Cold storage / Pantry | **Data Access Layer** (Database, ORM, Repositories) |
| Health Inspector & Tasting | **Testing Layer** (Unit, Integration, E2E tests) |
| Franchise manual & recipe book | **Design Patterns & Layering Principles** |

Just as a restaurant separates *serving*, *cooking*, and *storing* food into distinct stations (so the chef never takes orders and the waiter never cooks), a well-designed web app separates concerns into layers. This makes the restaurant (app) **easier to scale, easier to test, and easier to change recipes (features)**.

## 1.2 The Principle of Layering

**Layering** is a core architectural principle in which a software system is decomposed into a hierarchy of stacked, loosely-coupled tiers, each with a clearly defined responsibility. Communication is strictly **top-down** (or downward invocation): a layer may call the layer directly below it, but never sideways or upward.

The canonical 3-tier (or n-tier) web architecture contains:

1. **Presentation Layer (UI Layer)** — Handles HTTP requests, renders HTML/JSON, manages user input.
2. **Business Logic Layer (Application / Domain Layer)** — Enforces business rules, orchestrates workflows, performs validation.
3. **Data Access Layer (Persistence Layer)** — Reads/writes to the database, manages transactions, abstracts storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The Principle of Layering enforces *separation of concerns (SoC)*. Each layer hides the complexity of the layer below it through well-defined *interfaces*. This is the bedrock of testability and maintainability.

## 1.3 Software Design Patterns in the Web Context

A **design pattern** is a reusable, proven solution to a recurring design problem. The KTU syllabus emphasizes web-context patterns, which are the Gang of Four (GoF) and enterprise patterns most commonly applied in modern web frameworks.

The three foundational web patterns are:

- **MVC — Model-View-Controller** (e.g., Spring MVC, Ruby on Rails, Django, Express)
- **MVP — Model-View-Presenter** (common in ASP.NET Web Forms legacy)
- **MVVM — Model-View-ViewModel** (e.g., Angular, Knockout.js, Vue.js, WPF)

Plus supporting patterns:
- **Front Controller** (single entry point for all requests — e.g., `DispatcherServlet` in Spring)
- **DAO / Repository** (encapsulate data access)
- **Singleton** (shared resources like DB connection pools, loggers)
- **Factory** (object creation abstracted)
- **Observer** (event-driven UI updates)

> [!TIP]
> Frameworks don't *invent* MVC — they *implement* it. Understanding the underlying pattern lets you reason about any framework (React, Angular, Vue, Spring) using the same mental model.

## 1.4 Testing

**Software testing** is the systematic process of evaluating a web application to verify that it meets specified requirements and to identify defects. The KTU 2024 syllabus distinguishes between:

- **Functional Testing** — does it do what it should? (Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance)
- **Non-Functional Testing** — does it do it *well*? (Performance, Load, Stress, Security, Usability)
- **Structural Testing** — has the code been *exercised*? (White-box, Code Coverage, Cyclomatic Complexity)

The **Test Pyramid** (Mike Cohn) is the canonical model: many unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and very few end-to-end tests at the top.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Metric:** A healthy web project targets **70–80% code coverage**, **< 10 cyclomatic complexity** per function, and a test pyramid ratio of approximately **70 : 20 : 10** (Unit : Integration : E2E).

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principle of Layering — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Why Layering Works (The "Why")

- **Change Localization:** A change in the database schema affects only the Data Layer. A UI redesign affects only the Presentation Layer.
- **Independent Testability:** Each layer can be unit-tested by *mocking* the layer below it.
- **Parallel Development:** Frontend and backend teams can work simultaneously against agreed interfaces.
- **Technology Substitution:** You can swap MySQL for PostgreSQL, or React for Vue, without rewriting business logic.

### 2.1.2 The "How" — Layer Communication Rules

1. **Strict Top-Down Calls Only** — Layer N may call Layer N-1. Never sideways, never upward.
2. **Interfaces Define Contracts** — Each layer exposes an interface; the implementation can change without breaking callers.
3. **No Leaky Abstractions** — A Presentation Layer component must not embed SQL.
4. **Statelessness Where Possible** — Middle and bottom tiers should be stateless to enable horizontal scaling.
5. **Cross-Cutting Concerns via AOP** — Logging, security, and transactions span all layers and are best handled by Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP) or middleware.

### 2.1.3 The 3-Tier vs. n-Tier Distinction

| Aspect | 3-Tier | n-Tier |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | 3 (UI, Business, Data) | 3+ (e.g., adds Cache, Message Queue, Search) |
| Physical Distribution | Often same process | Distributed across multiple servers |
| Scalability | Limited | High — each tier scales independently |
| Example | Monolithic Spring Boot app | Microservices with API Gateway + DB + Cache |

## 2.2 Design Patterns — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 MVC (Model-View-Controller)

- **Model** — Domain data + business rules. Knows nothing of UI.
- **View** — Renders the Model. In web: JSP, Thymeleaf, React component, JSON serializer.
- **Controller** — Receives HTTP request, updates Model, selects View.

**Flow:** `User Request → Controller → (updates) Model → (selects) View → HTML Response`

### 2.2.2 MVP (Model-View-Presenter)

- **Model** — Same as MVC.
- **View** — Passive. Forwards *every* user event to the Presenter.
- **Presenter** — Acts as the "middleman." Contains all UI logic. Calls Model, then directly updates the View.

**Key Difference from MVC:** The View is *passive*; the Presenter pushes data to it. Easier to unit-test because the View can be mocked.

### 2.2.3 MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel)

- **Model** — Domain data.
- **View** — Declarative template (HTML with bindings).
- **ViewModel** — An abstraction of the View. Exposes data and commands. **Two-way data binding** keeps View and ViewModel synchronized automatically.

**Key Difference from MVP:** No explicit Presenter call. The framework (Angular, Vue) handles synchronization via *reactive bindings* and *observers*.

### 2.2.4 Supporting Web Patterns

| Pattern | Purpose | Web Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Front Controller** | Single entry point | `DispatcherServlet` (Spring), `app.use()` (Express) |
| **DAO / Repository** | Encapsulate persistence | `UserRepository.findById(id)` |
| **Singleton** | One shared instance | DB connection pool, Logger |
| **Factory** | Abstract object creation | `ConnectionFactory.getConnection()` |
| **Observer** | Event-driven updates | RxJS Observables, DOM event listeners |
| **Decorator** | Add behavior dynamically | Express middleware, Spring AOP |

## 2.3 Testing — Theory and Metrics

### 2.3.1 Levels of Testing (V-Model)

1. **Unit Testing** — Tests a single function/method in isolation. Fast, cheap, numerous.
2. **Integration Testing** — Tests interaction between modules. Reveals interface mismatches.
3. **System Testing** — Tests the complete, integrated application.
4. **Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — Validates against business requirements. Done by users/clients.

### 2.3.2 Testing Types

- **White-Box (Structural)** — Tester knows the internals. Used for unit & integration tests.
- **Black-Box (Functional)** — Tester knows only inputs/outputs. Used for system & acceptance tests.
- **Grey-Box** — Hybrid; tester has partial knowledge of internals.
- **Regression Testing** — Re-running old tests after a change to ensure nothing broke.
- **Smoke Testing** — Quick subset of tests to verify the build is stable.
- **Performance Testing** — Load, stress, spike, soak, capacity tests.
- **Security Testing** — OWASP Top 10 (SQLi, XSS, CSRF, etc.).

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in 14-mark derivation questions!**

| # | Metric / Formula | Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Measures number of independent paths in source code |
| 2 | **Nodes $N$** | Statements + decision points + entry + exit | Building CFG |
| 3 | **Edges $E$** | Flow of control between nodes | Building CFG |
| 4 | **Connected Components $P$** | Number of disconnected subgraphs (usually 1) | Building CFG |
| 5 | **Alternate Form 1** | $V(G) = \text{Number of decision points} + 1$ | Quick estimate |
| 6 | **Alternate Form 2** | $V(G) = \text{Number of regions in CFG}$ | Geometric CFG check |
| 7 | **Number of Independent Paths** | Equals $V(G)$ | Minimum tests for branch coverage |
| 8 | **Line Coverage** $LC$ | $LC = \dfrac{L_e}{L_t} \times 100\%$ | % of lines executed |
| 9 | **Branch Coverage** $BC$ | $BC = \dfrac{B_e}{B_t} \times 100\%$ | % of branches executed |
| 10 | **Function Coverage** $FC$ | $FC = \dfrac{F_e}{F_t} \times 100\%$ | % of functions called |
| 11 | **Path Coverage** $PC$ | $PC = \dfrac{P_e}{P_t} \times 100\%$ | % of paths executed |
| 12 | **Statement Coverage** $SC$ | $SC = \dfrac{S_e}{S_t} \times 100\%$ | % of statements executed |
| 13 | **Defect Density** $DD$ | $DD = \dfrac{D}{K} \ \text{defects/KLOC}$ | Quality indicator |
| 14 | **Mean Time To Failure** $MTTF$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{T_{\text{operational}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Reliability |
| 15 | **Test Effectiveness** $TE$ | $TE = \dfrac{\text{Defects found by tests}}{\text{Total defects}} \times 100\%$ | Test suite quality |
| 16 | **Boundary Value Analysis** | Test at $min-1$, $min$, $min+1$, $max-1$, $max$, $max+1$ | Edge case detection |
| 17 | **Equivalence Partitioning** | Divide inputs into valid/invalid classes, pick one from each | Test minimization |
| 18 | **Defect Removal Efficiency** $DRE$ | $DRE = \dfrac{D_{\text{found before release}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Process quality |

> **Where:** $L_e$ = executed lines, $L_t$ = total lines, $B_e$ = executed branches, $D$ = defects, $K$ = KLOC (thousand lines of code), $KLOC$ stands for **Kilo Lines of Code**.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These metrics drive **SonarQube** dashboards, **JaCoCo** coverage reports, **ISTQB** certification exams, and **CI/CD quality gates** in tools like Jenkins, GitHub Actions, and GitLab CI. A web app with $V(G) > 10$ per function is flagged as high-risk for maintenance. A coverage below **70%** typically fails a quality gate.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Cyclomatic Complexity — Full Derivation

The **cyclomatic complexity** $V(G)$ of a program (McCabe, 1976) measures the number of linearly independent paths through its source code. It is derived from graph theory (Euler's formula for planar graphs):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V - E + F &= C + 1 \quad \text{(Euler's formula for connected planar graphs)} \\
\text{For a single connected graph: } C &= 1 \\
\Rightarrow V - E + F &= 2 \\
\text{For a Control Flow Graph (CFG): } V &= N, \ E = E, \ F = P \ \text{(regions, including outer)} \\
\Rightarrow N - E + P &= 2 \\
\text{Solving for regions } P: \quad P &= E - N + 2 \\
\text{Since } V(G) &= \text{Number of enclosed regions} + 1 \\
\text{Wait — McCabe's } V(G) &= E - N + 2P \ \text{(for } P \text{ connected components)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Standard KTU form (single connected program):** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$

For a single connected program ($P = 1$):

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2
$$

For a program with $P$ disconnected subgraphs (rare in practice):

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

### 3.1.1 Worked Example: Compute $V(G)$ for a Web Login Function

Consider this JavaScript function (typical web login logic):

```javascript
function login(user, pass) {
    if (user == null || pass == null) {   // 1 decision (|| splits into 2)
        return "Invalid";
    }
    if (user === "admin" && pass === "1234") {  // 1 decision
        return "Welcome Admin";
    } else {
        return "Access Denied";
    }
}
```

**Step 1 — Identify decision points (predicate nodes):**

- Decision 1: `user == null || pass == null` → 2 branches
- Decision 2: `user === "admin" && pass === "1234"` → 2 branches

**Step 2 — Count decision points $D$:**

$$
D = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply shortcut formula:**

$$
V(G) = D + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Verify using full CFG formula:**

Draw the Control Flow Graph:

```
       [1: Entry]
            |
       <2: user==null || pass==null>
       /            \
   [3: T]        [4: F]
     |              |
  "Invalid"     <5: user==="admin" && pass==="1234">
                    /         \
                [6: T]      [7: F]
                  |            |
            "Welcome"    "Access Denied"
                  \         /
                   [8: Exit]
```

- Nodes $N = 8$ (numbered 1 through 8)
- Edges $E = 9$ (1→2, 2→3, 2→4, 3→8, 4→5, 5→6, 5→7, 6→8, 7→8)
- Connected components $P = 1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
     &= 9 - 8 + 2(1) \\
     &= 1 + 2 \\
     &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Both formulas agree: $V(G) = 3$**

This means we need a **minimum of 3 independent test cases** for 100% branch coverage.

### 3.1.2 The Three Independent Test Paths

1. **Path 1:** Entry → null-check (true) → "Invalid" → Exit
2. **Path 2:** Entry → null-check (false) → admin-check (true) → "Welcome" → Exit
3. **Path 3:** Entry → null-check (false) → admin-check (false) → "Access Denied" → Exit

## 3.2 Code Coverage Calculation — Full Derivation

Suppose a web project's test run reports the following from a coverage tool like **JaCoCo** or **Istanbul**:

- Total lines $L_t = 1000$
- Lines executed $L_e = 820$
- Total branches $B_t = 200$
- Branches executed $B_e = 170$
- Total functions $F_t = 50$
- Functions called $F_e = 45$

### 3.2.1 Line Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
LC &= \frac{L_e}{L_t} \times 100\% \\
   &= \frac{820}{1000} \times 100\% \\
   &= 82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Branch Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
BC &= \frac{B_e}{B_t} \times 100\% \\
   &= \frac{170}{200} \times 100\% \\
   &= 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Function Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
FC &= \frac{F_e}{F_t} \times 100\% \\
   &= \frac{45}{50} \times 100\% \\
   &= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU 14-mark question asks: *"The branch coverage is 85% but line coverage is 82%. Explain the discrepancy."* The answer: **branch coverage is generally ≥ line coverage** because every line is reachable through *some* branch, but not every branch is necessarily exercised even if lines are touched (e.g., missing the `else` branch).

## 3.3 Defect Density — Full Derivation

Suppose a team delivers 15 KLOC and the QA team finds 45 defects before release. The post-release period reveals an additional 5 defects.

### 3.3.1 Pre-release Defect Density

$$
\begin{aligned}
DD_{\text{pre}} &= \frac{D_{\text{pre}}}{KLOC} \\
                &= \frac{45}{15} \\
                &= 3.0 \ \text{defects/KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Post-release Total Defect Density

$$
\begin{aligned}
DD_{\text{total}} &= \frac{D_{\text{pre}} + D_{\text{post}}}{KLOC} \\
                  &= \frac{45 + 5}{15} \\
                  &= \frac{50}{15} \\
                  &\approx 3.33 \ \text{defects/KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.3 Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \frac{D_{\text{pre}}}{D_{\text{pre}} + D_{\text{post}}} \times 100\% \\
    &= \frac{45}{50} \times 100\% \\
    &= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Industry Benchmark:** Top software organizations target DRE > 95%. SEI CMMi Level 5 organizations often achieve > 99%.

## 3.4 📊 Pattern Comparison Matrix (Analytical)

| **Inventor** | Trygve Reenskaug (1979, Xerox PARC) | Mike Potel (1996) | John Gossman (2005, Microsoft) |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Explain the three layers of a 3-tier web architecture with one example component per layer.

**Model Answer:**

A 3-tier web architecture decomposes the application into three logical layers, each with a distinct responsibility:

1. **Presentation Layer (UI Tier):** The user-facing interface that handles HTTP request rendering and user input. *Example: HTML pages, React components, JSP files, or Thymeleaf templates.*

2. **Business Logic Layer (Application Tier):** Contains the core business rules, validation, and workflow orchestration. It acts as the brain of the system. *Example: A `PaymentService` class that validates an order, calculates tax, and initiates a transaction.*

3. **Data Access Layer (Data Tier):** Manages all interactions with persistent storage. Abstracts away SQL/NoSQL specifics. *Example: A `UserRepository` class using JPA/Hibernate or a `userModel.js` Mongoose schema.*

The **principle of separation of concerns** is enforced: a change in the database (e.g., MySQL → PostgreSQL) only affects the Data Layer, and a UI redesign only affects the Presentation Layer. This **improves maintainability, testability, and scalability** — key KTU outcomes.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between White-Box and Black-Box testing. Give one example technique for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | White-Box Testing | Black-Box Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge Required** | Internal code structure known to tester | Only inputs and outputs known |
| **Also Called** | Structural / Glass-Box Testing | Functional / Behavioral Testing |
| **Performed By** | Developers | Testers / End Users |
| **Granularity** | Statement, branch, path level | Requirement / Specification level |
| **Example Technique** | Cyclomatic complexity, statement coverage | Equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis |
| **Tool Examples** | JaCoCo, Coverage.py, Istanbul | Selenium, Postman, QTP |
| **Limitation** | Misses missing requirements | Cannot detect untested code paths |

> *Example:* A developer using JUnit to verify that every `if-else` branch in a `login()` function is executed is doing **white-box** testing. A tester using Selenium to verify that a user can complete checkout with a valid credit card (without knowing the backend code) is doing **black-box** testing.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *layers* with *tiers*. Layers are **logical** (code organization); tiers are **physical** (deployment on separate machines).
> - **Do NOT** say "MVC is a design pattern only for Java." MVC is **language-agnostic**.
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention the *interface contract* when explaining layered architecture — that's where the marks for "loose coupling" come from.
> - For cyclomatic complexity derivations, **always show the CFG** — examiners award 3–4 marks just for the diagram.
> - When computing coverage, **state the units clearly** (e.g., "% of branches") — losing 1 mark for ambiguity is common.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Layered Architecture & Patterns*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Principle of Layering in web application design. With a neat diagram, describe the 3-tier architecture and explain how each layer communicates with the layer below it.

**Model Answer:**

The **Principle of Layering** is a foundational architectural pattern in software engineering where a system is organized into a hierarchy of stacked, loosely-coupled layers. Each layer has a single, well-defined responsibility and communicates **only with the layer immediately below it** through a well-defined *interface*. This is known as the **separation of concerns (SoC)**.

**The 3-Tier Architecture consists of:**

**1. Presentation Layer (Top Tier)**
- Manages the **user interface** and handles HTTP requests/responses.
- Examples: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, JSP, Thymeleaf, Angular templates.
- Validates user input (client-side) before forwarding to the next layer.

**2. Business Logic Layer (Middle Tier)**
- Implements the **core business rules, workflows, and validation**.
- Independent of UI and database technology.
- Examples: `OrderService.processOrder()`, `AuthService.login()`, `PaymentValidator.validate()`.
- Acts as a **transaction script** that coordinates between Presentation and Data layers.

**3. Data Access Layer (Bottom Tier)**
- Encapsulates all **persistence operations** (CRUD on the database).
- Examples: `UserRepository`, JPA/Hibernate DAO, Mongoose models, SQL queries.
- Translates between **domain objects** and **storage representation**.

**Diagram:** *(See Section 4.1 for the Mermaid diagram — reproduce in your answer)*

**Communication Rules:**
- A Presentation Layer component calls the Business Layer through a **service interface** (e.g., `IAuthService`).
- The Business Layer calls the Data Layer through a **repository interface** (e.g., `IUserRepository`).
- **The Data Layer never calls upward.** Neither does the Business Layer.
- **Cross-cutting concerns** (logging, security, transactions) are applied via **AOP / middleware** and span all layers.

**Benefits:**
- **Maintainability:** Changes are localized.
- **Testability:** Each layer can be unit-tested with mocks.
- **Reusability:** Same business logic serves web, mobile, and API clients.
- **Scalability:** Layers can be deployed on different servers (turning 3-tier into n-tier).

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast MVC, MVP, and MVVM design patterns. Which is most suitable for a Single Page Application (SPA) and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MVC | MVP | MVVM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Components** | Model, View, Controller | Model, View, Presenter | Model, View, ViewModel |
| **View Role** | Smart (knows the model) | Passive (forwards events to Presenter) | Passive (auto-sync via bindings) |
| **Communication** | Controller updates Model, selects View | Presenter calls Model and directly updates View | ViewModel exposes data; framework syncs View |
| **Testability** | Moderate | High (Presenter is plain JS/Java, easily unit-testable) | High (ViewModel is plain, View can be mocked) |
| **Data Binding** | Manual | Manual | Automatic two-way |
| **Coupling** | Controller ↔ View (loose) | Presenter ↔ View via **interface** (looser) | ViewModel ↔ View via **binding** (loosest) |
| **Best Suited For** | Server-rendered web apps (Django, Spring MVC) | Legacy desktop / ASP.NET Web Forms | Modern SPA frontends (Angular, Vue, React+Redux) |

**For a Single Page Application (SPA), MVVM is the most suitable pattern** for the following reasons:

1. **Two-way data binding** eliminates the need for manual DOM manipulation. When the ViewModel state changes, the View updates automatically — and vice versa. This is ideal for SPAs with many interactive UI elements.
2. **Reactive programming** support — frameworks like Angular and Vue are built on RxJS / reactive streams, naturally fitting MVVM's observer-based binding.
3. **ViewModel is plain and testable** — business logic is separated from the DOM, making unit testing straightforward.
4. **Component reusability** — the ViewModel encapsulates the state and behavior of a component, which can be reused across views.

**Note:** React technically implements a **unidirectional MVC variant** (sometimes called "one-way data flow"), but combined with state libraries (Redux, MobX) it provides MVVM-like behavior.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Design Patterns & Testing*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, Internal Choice B]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Front Controller design pattern with a suitable diagram. How does Spring's `DispatcherServlet` implement it?

**Model Answer:**

The **Front Controller** pattern consolidates all incoming requests through a **single entry point** — a central handler that performs common preprocessing (authentication, logging, locale resolution) before delegating to specific handlers.

**Benefits:**
- Centralized control flow → easier to add cross-cutting concerns.
- Reduces code duplication across servlets.
- Simplifies routing and security enforcement.

**Diagram:** *(See Section 4.5)*

**Spring's `DispatcherServlet` implements the Front Controller pattern as follows:**

1. **Request Arrival:** Every HTTP request to a Spring MVC app is intercepted by `DispatcherServlet` (configured in `web.xml` or via `SpringBootServletInitializer`).

2. **Handler Mapping:** `DispatcherServlet` consults the `HandlerMapping` bean to find which `@Controller` method matches the URL and HTTP method.

3. **Interceptor Chain:** It runs any registered `HandlerInterceptor`s (e.g., for auth checks, logging).

4. **Handler Execution:** The matched controller method is invoked.

5. **Return Value Processing:** The controller returns a `ModelAndView` or a `@ResponseBody` object.

6. **View Resolution:** `DispatcherServlet` uses the `ViewResolver` to find the actual view (JSP, Thymeleaf template).

7. **Response Rendering:** The view is rendered and the HTTP response is sent back to the client.

This is exactly the **Front Controller** pattern — one servlet handles everything, decoupled from the actual business handlers, enabling clean separation of concerns and easy extensibility.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the cyclomatic complexity of the following web application function. List the minimum number of independent test paths required and design test cases for them.

```javascript
function calculateDiscount(userType, purchaseAmount, isHoliday) {
    let discount = 0;
    if (userType === "premium" && purchaseAmount > 10000) {
        discount = 0.20;
    } else if (userType === "regular" && purchaseAmount > 5000) {
        discount = 0.10;
    } else if (isHoliday) {
        discount = 0.05;
    } else {
        discount = 0;
    }
    if (discount > 0 && purchaseAmount > 0) {
        return purchaseAmount * (1 - discount);
    }
    return purchaseAmount;
}
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify decision points (predicate nodes):**

1. `userType === "premium" && purchaseAmount > 10000` — compound decision (counts as 1 decision point, 2 branches)
2. `userType === "regular" && purchaseAmount > 5000` — 1 decision point
3. `isHoliday` — 1 decision point
4. `discount > 0 && purchaseAmount > 0` — 1 decision point

$$
D = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Apply shortcut formula:**

$$
V(G) = D + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Verify using full CFG formula:**

Nodes $N = 11$ (Entry, D1, D2, D3, D4-true, D4-false, final-if, return1, return2, Exit, plus merge nodes).
Edges $E = 14$. Components $P = 1$.

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P = 14 - 11 + 2(1) = 5 \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Independent Test Paths = 5**

**Test Case Design:**

| Path # | Test Case | userType | purchaseAmount | isHoliday | Expected Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Premium customer, high amount | "premium" | 15000 | false | $15000 \times 0.80 = 12000$ |
| 2 | Regular customer, high amount | "regular" | 6000 | false | $6000 \times 0.90 = 5400$ |
| 3 | Non-premium, non-regular, holiday | "guest" | 1000 | true | $1000 \times 0.95 = 950$ |
| 4 | Non-premium, non-regular, non-holiday | "guest" | 1000 | false | $1000$ (no discount) |
| 5 | Edge: discount > 0 but purchaseAmount = 0 (unreachable in practice, defensive path) | "premium" | 0 | false | $0$ (return purchaseAmount) |

**Step 5 — Coverage Achieved:**

- **Statement Coverage:** 100% (all statements executed)
- **Branch Coverage:** 100% (all `if-else` branches covered)
- **Path Coverage:** 100% (all 5 independent paths covered)

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — read this the night before the exam!**

### 🔹 Real-World Web Software Design
- ☐ Translation of requirements → architecture → implementation → test.
- ☐ Must address **scalability, security, concurrency, maintainability**.
- ☐ Always start with **requirements → use cases → architecture → design → code → test**.

### 🔹 Principle of Layering
- ☐ **Strict top-down calls only.** No sideways, no upward.
- ☐ Layers are **logical**; tiers are **physical** deployment.
- ☐ Interface contracts between layers → loose coupling.
- ☐ Cross-cutting concerns via **AOP / middleware**.
- ☐ Benefits: **change localization, testability, parallel dev, tech substitution**.

### 🔹 Design Patterns
- ☐ **MVC:** Controller selects view; View is smart.
- ☐ **MVP:** View is passive; Presenter pushes to view.
- ☐ **MVVM:** Two-way data binding; framework handles sync.
- ☐ **Front Controller:** One entry point (e.g., `DispatcherServlet`).
- ☐ **DAO/Repository:** Encapsulate persistence.
- ☐ **Singleton:** One shared instance (DB pool, logger).
- ☐ **Factory:** Abstract object creation.
- ☐ **Observer:** Event-driven updates (RxJS, DOM events).

### 🔹 Testing
- ☐ **Test Pyramid:** Many unit → fewer integration → very few E2E.
- ☐ **V-Model:** Unit ↔ Integration ↔ System ↔ Acceptance.
- ☐ **White-box vs Black-box:** Structural vs Functional.
- ☐ **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P = D + 1$.
- ☐ **Coverage:** Line, Branch, Function, Path. Target ≥ 70–80%.
- ☐ **DRE:** Defects found pre-release / total defects. Target > 95%.
- ☐ **Test techniques:** Equivalence partitioning, Boundary value analysis, Decision tables, State transition.

### 🔹 High-Yield Numbers to Memorize
- ☐ $V(G) \leq 10$ → low risk. $> 10$ → high risk.
- ☐ Test pyramid ratio: **70 : 20 : 10** (Unit : Integration : E2E).
- ☐ DRE benchmark: **> 95%** for mature organizations.
- ☐ Industry coverage threshold: **≥ 80%** for CI/CD quality gates.

### 🔹 Common 14-Mark Question Patterns
1. Explain layered architecture + diagram (7 marks).
2. Compare MVC / MVP / MVVM with diagram and use cases (7 marks).
3. Compute $V(G)$ for a code snippet, draw CFG, list test paths (7 marks).
4. Explain Front Controller + DAO + Singleton with examples (7 marks).
5. Compute code coverage and defect density for given data (7 marks).
6. Differentiate functional vs non-functional testing with examples (7 marks).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** Always draw the **diagram first** (3–4 marks secured), then write the explanation around it. Examiners scan for diagrams and award marks before reading text.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Services - Overview of Web Services - SOAP Services, REST Services, An Example Web Service, Web Server - Hosting Options
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST742 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Web Services - Overview of Web Services - SOAP Services, REST Services, An Example Web Service, Web Server - Hosting Options # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Web Service? A **Web Service** is a standardized, self-contained, modular software application component that is designed to be invoked over a network (typically the Internet) using standard XML-based messaging protocols. It is described, published, located, and invoked using standard web technologies, allowing disparate systems to interoperate regardless of platform, language, or operating system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **W3C Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *"A software system designed to support interoperable machine-to-machine interaction over a network. It has an interface described in a machine-processable format (specifically WSDL). Other systems interact with the Web service in a manner prescribed by its description using SOAP messages, typically conveyed using HTTP with an XML serialization in conjunction with other Web-related standards."* ### Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Waiter Imagine a **Restaurant** as your distributed system: | Component | Real-World Role | Web Service Equivalent | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Customer** | Wants food, speaks English/Malayalam | **Client Application** (Java, Python, .NET) | | **Menu Card** | Lists available dishes with descriptions | **WSDL (Web Services Description Language)** | | **Waiter** | Takes your order, brings food back | **SOAP / REST Protocol** (the messenger) | | **Kitchen** | Prepares the actual food | **Web Service Provider / Business Logic** | | **Order Slip (XML)** | Structured, written order format | **XML / JSON Message** | > [!NOTE] > Just as a waiter is a **standardized intermediary** who can serve any customer speaking any language (as long as they follow the menu format), a Web Service provides a **standardized, language-agnostic contract** that any client can call, regardless of whether it is written in Java, Python, PHP, C#, or Ruby. ### 1.2 Core Characteristics of Web Services (The 5 Pillars) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite — These five features appear repeatedly in 2-mark and 14-mark questions. Memorize them:** 1. **XML-Based Messaging**: Data exchange happens via **XML** (Extensible Markup Language), ensuring human-readable, platform-independent communication. 2. **Loosely Coupled**: The client has *no direct dependency* on the server-side implementation. If the server changes its code, the client is unaffected as long as the contract (WSDL) remains the same. 3. **Coarse-Grained**: Unlike method-level RPC calls, web services expose *large business operations* (e.g., "getCustomerProfile" returns a full record, not just the name). 4. **Ability to be Synchronous or Asynchronous**: A client can call a service and **wait** for the response (synchronous, like SOAP) or invoke it and **be notified later** (asynchronous, via callbacks or message queues). 5. **Supports Remote Procedure Calls (RPC) and Document Exchange**: Web services can either execute procedures on a remote machine OR simply exchange structured business documents. ### 1.3 The Two Architectural Flavours: SOAP vs REST > [!NOTE] > The KTU syllabus explicitly groups web services into two main categories. We will treat them in detail in Section 2, but here is the bird's-eye view: - **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)** → A **protocol** (strict rules). Heavyweight, enterprise-grade, XML-only. Best for banking, payment gateways, and high-security transactions. - **REST (Representational State Transfer)** → An **architectural style** (loose guidelines). Lightweight, uses HTTP methods, supports JSON. Best for mobile apps, social media, public APIs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 SOAP Services — A Deep Dive **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)** is a **W3C-standardized protocol** for exchanging structured information in the form of XML messages between distributed systems over computer networks, most commonly using **HTTP/HTTPS** as the transport layer. ### The Anatomy of a SOAP Message A SOAP message is an **XML envelope** containing two mandatory parts and two optional parts: $$ \text{SOAP Message} = \text{Envelope} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \text{Header (Optional)} \rightarrow \text{Authentication, Routing, Metadata} \\ \text{Body (Mandatory)} \rightarrow \text{Actual Call \& Response Data} \\ \text{Fault (Optional)} \rightarrow \text{Error \& Status Information} \end{array} \right. $$ ### Step-by-Step Working of a SOAP Transaction 1. **Service Creation**: Developer writes a class/method in Java, .NET, or Python that implements the business logic (e.g., `getTemperature(String city)`). 2. **WSDL Generation**: The framework (Apache CXF, JAX-WS, .NET WCF) automatically generates a **WSDL (Web Services Description Language)** file — an XML "contract" that exposes the service signature, input/output types, and the URL endpoint. 3. **UDDI Registration (Optional)**: The WSDL is optionally published to a **UDDI (Universal Description, Discovery, and Integration)** registry so clients can dynamically discover it. 4. **Client Stub Generation**: The client developer passes the WSDL to a tool (`wsimport`, `wsdl2java`, `svcutil.exe`) which auto-generates **client-side proxy classes (stubs)** that mirror the server's interface. 5. **Serialization**: The client stub serializes the function call into a **SOAP XML envelope** and wraps it inside an HTTP POST request. 6. **Transport**: The HTTP request travels to the server. The SOAP runtime on the server deserializes the XML back into a method call. 7. **Execution & Response**: The server executes the actual function, serializes the result into a SOAP XML response, and sends it back over HTTP. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** In SOAP, both the *request* and *response* are wrapped inside **SOAP Envelopes** — making it "XML-in, XML-out." The HTTP transport itself is essentially a "tunnel"; SOAP would still work over SMTP, TCP, or JMS. ### 2.2 REST Services — A Deep Dive **REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an *architectural style* (not a protocol) defined by **Roy Fielding** in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It treats every piece of data as a **Resource** that can be uniquely identified by a **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)** and manipulated using the **standard HTTP methods**. ### The 6 REST Architectural Constraints (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fielding's 6 Constraints** — these appear in 14-mark questions. A true RESTful service must satisfy at least the first five: | # | Constraint | Meaning | Why It Matters | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Client-Server** | Clear separation of UI (client) and data storage (server) | Improves portability and scalability | | 2 | **Stateless** | Each request contains ALL info needed; server stores no client session | Enables horizontal scaling | | 3 | **Cacheable** | Responses must declare themselves cacheable or non-cacheable | Reduces network latency | | 4 | **Uniform Interface** | Standardized resource identification (URIs) and manipulation (HTTP verbs) | Decouples client and server evolution | | 5 | **Layered System** | Client cannot tell if it's talking to origin server or an intermediary (proxy, CDN, load balancer) | Enhances security and scalability | | 6 | **Code on Demand (Optional)** | Server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript) to the client | The only optional constraint | ### The 5 HTTP Verbs Used in REST | Verb | CRUD Operation | Idempotent? | Safe? | Example URI | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `GET` | Read | Yes | Yes | `GET /api/users/101` | | `POST` | Create | No | No | `POST /api/users` | | `PUT` | Update / Replace | Yes | No | `PUT /api/users/101` | | `PATCH` | Partial Update | No | No | `PATCH /api/users/101` | | `DELETE` | Delete | Yes | No | `DELETE /api/users/101` | > [!NOTE] > **Idempotent** means calling the operation 1 time or 100 times produces the same final server state. **Safe** means the operation does not modify the server state. ### 2.3 SOAP vs REST — The Ultimate Comparison Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **This comparison table is the single most-asked conceptual question in KTU Module 4. Memorize the contrasts.** | Parameter | **SOAP** | **REST** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Type** | Protocol | Architectural Style | | **Standard Body** | W3C Standardized | Proposed by Roy Fielding, no formal standard | | **Data Format** | **XML only** | XML, **JSON**, YAML, plain text, HTML | | **Transport** | HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS | **HTTP / HTTPS only** | | **Statefulness** | Can be stateful (with WS-Security tokens) | Strictly **Stateless** | | **Contract Definition** | WSDL (mandatory) | No WSDL; often **WADL / OpenAPI / Swagger** | | **Security** | **WS-Security**, SSL, SAML | **HTTPS**, OAuth2, JWT, API Keys | | **Performance** | Slower (verbose XML, parsing overhead) | Faster (lightweight JSON, HTTP caching) | | **Bandwidth** | **High** (heavy XML envelope) | **Low** (compact JSON) | | **Error Handling** | Built-in `` element | HTTP status codes (404, 500, etc.) | | **Best Suited For** | Banking, payments, telecom, enterprise apps | Mobile apps, social networks, public APIs, IoT | | **Tooling** | JAX-WS, Apache CXF, .NET WCF, gSOAP | Postman, Spring Boot REST, Express.js, Flask | | **Discoverability** | UDDI Registry | Hypermedia / OpenAPI Documentation | ### 2.4 Web Server Hosting Options (KTU Module 4 Coverage) > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Web Server** is a software/hardware system that stores, processes, and delivers web content to clients over HTTP. Hosting refers to *where* and *how* this server runs. KTU expects students to know all four major hosting models: | Hosting Type | Description | Pros | Cons | Cost Model | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Shared Hosting** | Multiple websites share one physical server and its resources (CPU, RAM, OS) | Cheapest, easy to use, managed | "Noisy neighbor" effect, limited control, poor performance | **Low** (~$3–10/month) | | **Virtual Private Server (VPS)** | A physical server is partitioned into isolated virtual machines using a hypervisor (KVM, VMware, Hyper-V) | Dedicated resources, root access, scalable | Requires server admin knowledge | **Medium** (~$20–100/month) | | **Dedicated Hosting** | One entire physical server is leased to a single client | Maximum performance, full control, full hardware isolation | Expensive, requires in-house expertise | **High** (~$100–500+/month) | | **Cloud Hosting (IaaS/PaaS)** | On-demand, elastic, pay-as-you-go servers on platforms like **AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, DigitalOcean** | Auto-scaling, pay-per-use, global CDN, high availability | Can become expensive at scale, vendor lock-in | **Variable** (Pay-per-second/minute) | ### Sub-Categories of Cloud Hosting > [!NOTE] > **PaaS vs IaaS vs SaaS** — KTU sometimes asks students to differentiate these: - **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)**: You rent raw VMs (e.g., AWS EC2). You install the OS, web server (Apache/Nginx), and runtime. → *Maximum control.* - **PaaS (Platform as a Service)**: You deploy your code; the provider manages the OS, runtime, and server (e.g., Heroku, Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk). → *Maximum developer productivity.* - **SaaS (Software as a Service)**: You consume finished software (e.g., Gmail, Dropbox, Salesforce). → *Zero development needed.* ### 2.5 Popular Web Server Software | Web Server | Vendor | OS Support | Key Strength | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Apache HTTP Server** | Apache Software Foundation | Cross-platform | Most popular, `.htaccess` flexibility, modular | | **Nginx** | F5 Networks (originally by Igor Sysoev) | Cross-platform | Event-driven, excellent for static content & reverse proxy | | **Microsoft IIS** | Microsoft | Windows only | Tightly integrated with ASP.NET / Windows Server | | **LiteSpeed** | LiteSpeed Technologies | Linux | Apache-compatible, high performance | | **Tomcat** | Apache Software Foundation | Cross-platform | Java Servlet/JSP container (not a full static web server) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Apache vs Nginx:** A classic KTU question. Apache uses a **process/thread-per-connection** model (good for dynamic content like PHP). Nginx uses an **asynchronous event-driven** model (handles 10,000+ concurrent connections with low memory, ideal for static content and reverse proxying). # 3. Step-by-Step Examples & Analytical Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Example Web Service: A "Temperature Converter" — SOAP Implementation > [!NOTE] > The KTU syllabus mentions *"An Example Web Service"* explicitly. We will build a complete **Celsius-to-Fahrenheit converter** in both SOAP and REST styles so the student sees the full contrast. ### Step 1: Define the Service Interface (Java — JAX-WS) ```java // File: TempConverterService.java import javax.jws.WebService; import javax.jws.WebMethod; import javax.jws.WebParam; @WebService(name = "TempConverter", targetNamespace = "http://ktu.edu/temp") public class TempConverterService { @WebMethod(operationName = "celsiusToFahrenheit") public double celsiusToFahrenheit(@WebParam(name = "celsius") double celsius) { // Formula: F = (C × 9/5) + 32 return (celsius * 9.0 / 5.0) + 32.0; } } ``` ### Step 2: Auto-Generated WSDL (Truncated Excerpt) The JAX-WS runtime auto-generates the WSDL contract. The critical part is the `` and `` definitions: ```xml ``` ### Step 3: The Actual SOAP Request XML Sent Over the Wire When the client invokes `celsiusToFahrenheit(100)`, the following XML envelope is transmitted: ```xml 100 ``` ### Step 4: The SOAP Response ```xml 212.0 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notice the Verbosity:** For a single double value `100`, SOAP transmitted an entire XML envelope (~400 bytes) with namespace declarations. This is the #1 reason developers prefer REST for lightweight scenarios. ### Step 5: Verify the Temperature Formula $$ F = (C \times \frac{9}{5}) + 32 $$ For $C = 100$: $$ F = (100 \times \frac{9}{5}) + 32 = (100 \times 1.8) + 32 = 180 + 32 = 212.0 $$ The response `212.0` is **physically correct** (water boils at 212°F and 100°C). --- ## 3.2 The Same Service — REST Implementation A REST equivalent is far simpler. The URI identifies the *resource* and the HTTP verb specifies the *action*. ### Step 1: REST Endpoint Design | HTTP Method | URI | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `GET` | `http://api.ktu.edu/temp/convert/100` | Convert 100°C to Fahrenheit | | `POST` | `http://api.ktu.edu/temp/convert` | Send JSON body with conversion request | ### Step 2: The REST Request (GET Method) ``` GET /temp/convert/100 HTTP/1.1 Host: api.ktu.edu Accept: application/json ``` ### Step 3: The REST Response (JSON) ```json { "celsius": 100, "fahrenheit": 212.0, "status": "success", "timestamp": "2024-11-15T10:30:00Z" } ``` ### Step 4: REST Request Using POST with JSON Body ``` POST /temp/convert HTTP/1.1 Host: api.ktu.edu Content-Type: application/json Accept: application/json { "celsius": 37 } ``` ### Step 5: REST Response ```json { "celsius": 37, "fahrenheit": 98.6, "status": "success" } $$ Verification: $F = (37 \times 9/5) + 32 = 66.6 + 32 = 98.6$ (Normal human body temperature, correct!). > [!NOTE] > **Byte-Size Comparison:** The REST request+response payload is roughly **60% smaller** than the SOAP equivalent, with no namespace declarations and no envelope overhead. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Hosting a Web Service on Different Servers **Scenario:** A student deploys a Node.js-based REST API. The KTU exam might ask: *"Which hosting option would you choose for a startup with 10,000 daily users expecting 10x traffic spikes on result day?"* | Requirement | Best Choice | Reasoning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | College project, minimal users, free | **Shared Hosting** (e.g., InfinityFree, 000webhost) | Zero cost, zero admin overhead | | Small business, predictable traffic | **VPS** (e.g., DigitalOcean Droplet $6/month) | Dedicated resources, root access, predictable cost | | Spiky traffic, unknown scale | **Cloud PaaS** (e.g., Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk) | Auto-scales from 1 to 1000 instances based on load | | Banking app, strict compliance, predictable high load | **Dedicated Server on Private Cloud** | Full control, data sovereignty, deterministic performance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The choice is driven by the **scalability triangle** — *Cost*, *Control*, and *Complexity*. Shared = cheapest but least control. Dedicated = most control but most complex. Cloud PaaS = the sweet spot for most modern startups. --- ## 3.4 SOAP Fault Example (Error Handling) If the user sends a non-numeric value, the SOAP service returns a structured Fault: ```xml soapenv:Client Invalid input: celsius must be a number NaN detected at line 1 $$ In REST, the equivalent would simply be: ``` HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request Content-Type: application/json { "error": "Invalid input", "message": "celsius must be a number", "code": 400 } ``` # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Web Service Communication — High-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Client App
Java, Python, Mobile] -->|HTTP Request
SOAP or REST| B[Web Server
Apache, Nginx, IIS] B -->|Parse and Route| C[Application Server
Tomcat, Node.js, JBoss] C -->|Invoke Method| D[Web Service
Business Logic] D -->|Query / Update| E[(Database
MySQL, MongoDB)] D -->|Return Result| C C -->|HTTP Response
XML or JSON| B B -->|Send Back| A ``` ## 4.2 SOAP Web Service — Detailed Interaction Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Client App participant Stub as SOAP Stub
(Auto-generated) participant Net as HTTP Transport participant Server as SOAP Runtime
(JAX-WS / CXF) participant Service as Business Logic Client->>Stub: 1. Call method celsiusToFahrenheit(100) Stub->>Stub: 2. Serialize call into
SOAP XML Envelope Stub->>Net: 3. Wrap in HTTP POST
Content-Type: text/xml Net->>Server: 4. Transmit over network Server->>Server: 5. Parse XML Envelope
