KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse WSDL with UDDI.** WSDL is the *service description* (interface contract). UDDI is the *registry* where services are published for discovery. Students regularly mix these up and lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **REST is NOT a protocol.** This is the most common KTU trap. REST is an *architectural style*. SOAP is the *protocol*. Writing "REST protocol" in an exam is an instant 1-mark deduction. > 3. **In SOAP comparisons, do not write "SOAP is better" or "REST is better."** Always frame the answer in terms of *use cases*. SOAP for enterprise/banking, REST for public APIs/mobile. > 4. **HTTP verbs in REST are case-sensitive.** Always use uppercase: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`. Writing "get" or "post" in lowercase may lose a mark. > 5. **Stateless does not mean "no state exists"** — it means *the server does not maintain client session state between requests*. The database still has state. This nuance is tested frequently. --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Web Service. List any four of its characteristics. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Web Service** is a software system designed to support interoperable machine-to-machine interaction over a network. It has an interface described in a machine-processable format (WSDL) and interacts with other systems using SOAP-mandated XML messages conveyed via HTTP. **Four Characteristics:** 1. **XML-Based Messaging** — uses XML for data representation and transport headers. 2. **Loosely Coupled** — clients are not tightly bound to the server's implementation. 3. **Coarse-Grained** — exposes large business operations rather than fine-grained methods. 4. **Language and Platform Independent** — works across Java, .NET, PHP, Python, etc. > [!NOTE] > For full marks, students should also mention the **synchronicity, discoverability, and self-describing** nature of web services. ### Question 2: Differentiate between WSDL and UDDI. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | **WSDL** | **UDDI** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Full Form** | Web Services Description Language | Universal Description, Discovery, and Integration | | **Purpose** | Describes *what* a service does (its interface, operations, input/output types) | A *registry* or *directory* where WSDL files are published and discovered | | **Nature** | An XML-based interface definition document | A yellow-pages-like XML-based registry specification | | **Analogy** | The **Menu Card** of a restaurant | The **Restaurant Directory** that lists all restaurants and their menus | | **Role in Web Service** | Contract between client and server | Discovery mechanism to find services dynamically | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A: SOAP Web Services `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the architecture of a SOAP-based web service with a neat diagram. Describe the role of WSDL and UDDI in this architecture. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a SOAP request and response XML message for a service operation `getSquare(int n)` that returns the square of an integer. Show the full Envelope structure. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks **Architecture of SOAP Web Service:** The SOAP architecture consists of **three primary roles** and **three core operations**: **Three Roles:** 1. **Service Provider** — Creates and hosts the web service. 2. **Service Requester (Client)** — Consumes the web service. 3. **Service Registry (UDDI)** — A central directory where providers publish their services. **Three Operations:** 1. **Publish** — Service provider publishes WSDL to the UDDI registry. 2. **Find** — Client queries the UDDI registry to locate a suitable service. 3. **Bind** — Client retrieves the WSDL and uses it to invoke the service via SOAP over HTTP. **Role of WSDL:** WSDL is an **XML-based contract** that describes the service's: - Abstract interface (`portType`, `operation`, `message`) - Concrete binding (SOAP over HTTP) - Endpoint address (URL where the service is hosted) The WSDL acts as a **machine-readable agreement** — neither the client nor the server needs human intervention to understand each other. **Role of UDDI:** UDDI provides a **standards-based discovery mechanism**. It is essentially a global registry of businesses and their web services. While modern systems often skip UDDI and use direct WSDL distribution or API gateways, UDDI was envisioned as the "yellow pages" of web services in early SOA architectures. **(Diagram: Refer to the SOAP Envelope block diagram in Section 4.5 for visual reference. Full 1 mark for the diagram, 6 marks for explanation.)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks **WSDL Excerpt for the `getSquare` Service:** ```xml ``` **SOAP Request (Client → Server) for `getSquare(7)`:** ```xml 7 ``` **SOAP Response (Server → Client):** ```xml 49 ``` **Verification of the Calculation:** $$ \text{Square}(n) = n^2 = 7^2 = 7 \times 7 = 49 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (Question A(b)):** Correct Envelope structure = 2 marks, correct Body and operation name = 2 marks, correct request parameter = 1 mark, correct response structure = 1 mark, correct numerical verification = 1 mark. --- ### Question B: REST Web Services and Hosting `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the REST architectural style. List the six constraints proposed by Roy Fielding, and explain any four of them. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare SOAP and REST in detail. Also discuss the four major web hosting options available for deploying a web service. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks **REST — Representational State Transfer** is an architectural style proposed by **Roy Fielding** in 2000. It treats data on the web as **resources** that are uniquely identified by URIs and manipulated using standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). REST is **stateless** and relies on a **uniform interface**. **The Six Constraints:** 1. **Client-Server** 2. **Stateless** 3. **Cacheable** 4. **Uniform Interface** 5. **Layered System** 6. **Code on Demand (Optional)** **Detailed Explanation of Four Constraints:** **1. Client-Server Constraint:** There must be a clear separation of concerns. The client handles the **user interface and user state**; the server handles **data storage and business logic**. This separation improves portability (the client can run on mobile, browser, or desktop without server changes) and scalability (servers can be replicated independently). **2. Stateless Constraint:** Each request from the client must contain **all the information** necessary for the server to fulfill it. The server does **not store any client context** between requests. If a session is needed, the client sends a session token (e.g., JWT) with every request. This enables horizontal scaling because any server in a cluster can handle any request. **3. Cacheable Constraint:** Responses must explicitly indicate whether they are cacheable or not, typically using HTTP headers like `Cache-Control` and `Expires`. Well-designed caching drastically reduces network round-trips, server load, and perceived latency. **4. Uniform Interface:** This is the most defining constraint. It mandates that resources are identified by **URIs**, manipulated through **representations** (JSON, XML), use **standard HTTP verbs** (GET, POST, etc.), and include **hypermedia links** (HATEOAS — Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State) so clients can navigate the API dynamically. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (Question B(a)):** Definition of REST = 2 marks, listing all 6 constraints = 2 marks, explanation of 4 constraints = 3 marks (roughly 0.75 each). --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks **SOAP vs REST — Detailed Comparison (5 marks):** | Parameter | **SOAP** | **REST** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Nature** | Protocol with strict W3C standards | Architectural style (loose guidelines) | | **Data Format** | XML only | JSON (preferred), XML, YAML, plain text | | **Transport** | HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS | HTTP/HTTPS only | | **Contract** | WSDL mandatory | No formal contract; OpenAPI/Swagger optional | | **State** | Can maintain state via WS-* specs | Strictly stateless | | **Security** | WS-Security, SAML, X.509, SSL | HTTPS, OAuth2, JWT, API Keys | | **Performance** | Slower (heavy XML parsing) | Faster (lightweight JSON) | | **Use Cases** | Banking, payments, telecom B2B, airline booking | Social media APIs, mobile apps, public web APIs | **Web Hosting Options (2 marks):** 1. **Shared Hosting**: Multiple websites share one physical server's resources. *Cheapest*, but performance suffers due to "noisy neighbors." Suitable for small blogs, college projects. 2. **Virtual Private Server (VPS)**: A physical server is partitioned into isolated VMs via a hypervisor. Each VPS has *guaranteed resources* and *root access*. Ideal for small-to-medium businesses. 3. **Dedicated Hosting**: An entire physical server is leased to a single client. Provides *maximum performance and control* but is expensive and requires in-house system administration. 4. **Cloud Hosting**: On-demand, elastic infrastructure from providers like AWS, Azure, GCP, or DigitalOcean. Offers *auto-scaling, pay-per-use pricing, and global CDN*. Best for unpredictable or rapidly growing workloads. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** When comparing hosting options in a 14-mark answer, always conclude with a **recommendation** based on a use case. "For a startup expecting rapid growth, cloud hosting (PaaS) is the most cost-effective because it eliminates upfront capital expenditure and scales elastically." This adds 1–2 impression marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Print This Page Before the Exam** - [ ] A **Web Service** is a *machine-to-machine* interaction system over a network, using XML/SOAP over HTTP, described by WSDL, and discovered via UDDI. - [ ] The **5 core characteristics** are: XML-based, loosely coupled, coarse-grained, synchronous-or-asynchronous, RPC-or-document. - [ ] **SOAP = Protocol** (strict, W3C-standardized, XML-only, heavy). - [ ] **REST = Architectural Style** (lightweight, JSON-friendly, stateless, HTTP verbs). - [ ] The **SOAP Envelope** contains `Header (optional)` + `Body (mandatory)` + `Fault (optional, inside Body)`. - [ ] **WSDL** = Description (the *contract* / "menu card"). - [ ] **UDDI** = Discovery (the *registry* / "yellow pages"). - [ ] The **6 REST constraints** by Fielding: Client-Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-on-Demand. - [ ] **HTTP Verbs**: `GET` (read, safe, idempotent), `POST` (create, not idempotent), `PUT` (replace, idempotent), `DELETE` (delete, idempotent), `PATCH` (partial update). - [ ] **Idempotent** = calling once or many times produces the same result. `PUT` and `DELETE` are idempotent; `POST` is **not**. - [ ] **Stateless** ≠ "no state exists." It means the *server* does not maintain *client session* state between requests. - [ ] **4 Hosting Options**: Shared (cheapest, least control), VPS (medium, root access), Dedicated (expensive, full control), Cloud (elastic, pay-per-use). - [ ] **Cloud sub-models**: IaaS (raw VMs), PaaS (managed platform), SaaS (ready-to-use software). - [ ] **Popular Web Servers**: Apache (process-per-connection, `.htaccess`), Nginx (event-driven, reverse proxy), IIS (Windows-only, .NET-friendly), Tomcat (Java servlets). - [ ] **Temperature Formula** (for the example service): $F = (C \times 9/5) + 32$. - [ ] **SOAP uses XML-in, XML-out** (both request and response are XML envelopes). **REST typically uses JSON** for the body but can use XML. - [ ] **WS-Security** is a SOAP extension. **OAuth2 / JWT** are REST-friendly security mechanisms. > [!WARNING] > **One-Line Mnemonics for SOAP vs REST:** > - SOAP = **S**trict **O**ld-fashioned **A**rchitecture for **P**ayments (heavyweight, enterprise, banking). > - REST = **R**eally **E**asy **S**imple **T**ransfer (lightweight, modern, mobile-friendly). > - WSDL = **W**hat does the service offer? (Description) > - UDDI = **U**niversal **D**iscovery **D**irectory **I**ndex (Registry) --- **End of Module 4 Notes — Web Services, SOAP, REST, and Web Server Hosting Options.** *Prepared in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) syllabus and B.Tech outcome-based education (OBE) framework.*
